issues.title: Installation / usage with ndistro not possible
issues.body: Since a full install location isn't specified in wscript, systems that build modules in a separate location to where they're checked out (ndistro) fail on including a successfully compiled / installed node-glob module, like [in this gist](https://gist.github.com/774812).

I'm not familiar with ndistro.  Wanna submit a patch to make it work?  What's involved?

node-glob works fine with npm.

issues.title: support extended globs
issues.body:
As far as I can tell "extended globs" have been implemented independently in Ruby's dir.c, bash 4.01's lib/glob.c, in zsh, and somewhere in the Perl ecosystem.

GNU Bash's glob is GPL, of course, which is unfortunate.  Ruby's is GPL or some custom stuff, probably workable.  Bash's and zsh's are the best in terms of spec compliance and general utility, but both are less than completely independent from their respective programs.  Ruby's seems pretty complete as well, but I'm not sure how great a fit it is for node's use.

Another option is to take a look at the posix spec and implement it that way.  But that sucks.
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: papandreou
comments.body:
I know I'm not exactly helping out by saying this, but: +1
:)

comments.body: NetBSD has an implementation that's BSD-licensed.  So that'd work.

comments.body: Implemented in v2.0.0

issues.title: fnmatch should be a separate thing
issues.body: Dunno if maybe it should be `require("glob/fnmatch")` or maybe segregate the constants somehow, but the `GLOB_` constants and the `FNM_` constants should probably not live on the same object, or else it's a bit tricky to translate them back and forth.
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: implemented in 2.0.0.  `require('glob').glob` has the `GLOB_` constants, and `require('glob').fnmatch` has the `FNMATCH_` constants.

issues.title: fnmatch doesn't use the same algorithm as glob
issues.body: failing a few tests.  `fnmatch('foo/**/bar', 'foo/baz/asdf/bar')` should return true, but `fnmatch('foo/*/bar', 'foo/baz/asdf/bar')` should return false.
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Will be solved by #23

issues.title: GLOB_NOSPACE error when glob results in >128 files
issues.body:
To reproduce, generate 129 or more files in an empty directory:

```
perl -e 'for (1..129) {system "touch $_.dat";}'
```

And then run this snippet in the node repl:

```
require('glob').glob('*', function (err, fileNames) {console.log(arguments);});
```

On Ubuntu 10.10 and glob 2.0.5 this results in:

```
> { '0': 
   { message: 'GLOB_NOSPACE'
   , stack: [Getter/Setter]
   , errno: -1
   }
, '1': '*'
}
```

From a couple of quick tests I concluded that it's the number of entries in the result that matters, not the number of candidate files.

glob 1.1.0 worked fine with any number of entries I've thrown at it so far.
issues.author: papandreou

comments.author: timjb
comments.body: I get the same error on Fedora 14 regardless of the number of entries.

comments.author: dunkfordyce
comments.body: this is biting me too - any progress/fix for this?

comments.author: dunkfordyce
comments.body: of course now i read the docs i see GLOB_LIMIT should not be set and everything works as expected. Personally id say this flag shouldnt be set by default - or a big fat warning should be in the docs.

comments.author: papandreou
comments.body:
@dunkfordyce: You're right, everything works as expected for me when I supply `glob.GLOB_DEFAULT & ~glob.GLOB_LIMIT` as the second argument for glob. Thanks a lot for figuring that out and sharing!

+1 for not having that as the default.

comments.author: timjb
comments.body: Btw, on my new installation of Fedora 14 it's working fine.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: That was supposed to be for #48, whoops.

issues.title: Problem installing glob with npm bundle
issues.body:
Hey,

I'm having an issue running "npm bundle" with npm version 0.3.18 and it seems to be failing with glob. Running "npm bundle install glob" gives the following output:

```
# npm bundle install glob
npm info it worked if it ends with ok
npm info using npm@0.3.18
npm info using node@v0.4.1
npm info preinstall glob@2.0.7
chdir(): Permission denied
npm info glob@2.0.7 Failed to exec preinstall script
npm ERR! install failed Error: glob@2.0.7 preinstall: `node-waf clean || true; node-waf configure build`
npm ERR! install failed `sh "-c" "node-waf clean || true; node-waf configure build"` failed with 127
npm ERR! install failed     at ChildProcess.<anonymous> (/usr/lib/node/.npm/npm/0.3.18/package/lib/utils/exec.js:49:20)
npm ERR! install failed     at ChildProcess.emit (events.js:45:17)
npm ERR! install failed     at ChildProcess.onexit (child_process.js:171:12)
npm info install failed rollback
npm info uninstall [ 'glob@2.0.7' ]
npm info preuninstall glob@2.0.7
npm info uninstall glob@2.0.7
npm info auto-deactive not symlink
npm info postuninstall glob@2.0.7
npm info uninstall glob@2.0.7 complete
npm info install failed rolled back
npm ERR! Error: glob@2.0.7 preinstall: `node-waf clean || true; node-waf configure build`
npm ERR! `sh "-c" "node-waf clean || true; node-waf configure build"` failed with 127
npm ERR!     at ChildProcess.<anonymous> (/usr/lib/node/.npm/npm/0.3.18/package/lib/utils/exec.js:49:20)
npm ERR!     at ChildProcess.emit (events.js:45:17)
npm ERR!     at ChildProcess.onexit (child_process.js:171:12)
npm ERR! 
npm ERR! Failed at the glob@2.0.7 preinstall script.
npm ERR! This is most likely a problem with the glob package,
npm ERR! not with npm itself.
npm ERR! Tell the author that this fails on your system:
npm ERR!     node-waf clean || true; node-waf configure build
npm ERR! You can get their info via:
npm ERR!     npm owner ls glob
npm ERR! There is likely additional logging output above.
npm ERR! System Linux 2.6.32-305-ec2
npm ERR! argv { remain: [ 'glob' ],
npm ERR! argv   cooked: 
npm ERR! argv    [ 'bundle',
npm ERR! argv      'install',
npm ERR! argv      'glob' ],
npm ERR! argv   original: 
npm ERR! argv    [ 'bundle',
npm ERR! argv      'install',
npm ERR! argv      'glob' ] }
npm not ok
```

Trying a pure "npm install glob" the install runs correctly and I'm able to require('glob'). Even after doing this "npm bundle" fails. I'm not sure how to diagnose the problem further, so any advice would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks!
Greg
issues.author: gbelote

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
If at all possible, it's best to use npm 1.0, _especially_ if you prefer the "bundle" approach to dependency management.

This is a known issue with npm 0.x when bundling packages that have build steps, and will not be fixed.

comments.author: gbelote
comments.body: Ah, thanks! Upgrading fixed the issue :-)

issues.title: port to use minimatch instead
issues.body:
Use https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch instead of the bsd glob.c file.

Compilation is an unnecessary evil in this case.
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: augustl
comments.body: The minimatch library has a different API and does not implement file system traversal. Perhaps it's a god idea to keep node-glob alive in order to not break all projects that depend on node-glob?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
No, the plan is to use minimatch's matching, but add file system traversal, so that it's the same API.

I'm not interested in porting bsdglob.c.

comments.body: So the plan is to update the glob module by depending on minimatch and using the fs module to traverse the file system?

issues.title: 3.0.1: CWD is stripped from glob results when pattern starts with /
issues.body:
The current working directory shouldn't be relevant when globbing an absolute path, but:

```
andreas@ubuntu:~$ npm install glob@3.0.1
[...]
andreas@ubuntu:~$ node
> require('glob').sync('/home/andreas/foo');
[ 'foo' ]
```

Same thing happens when the pattern contains wildcards.

It worked fine in previous versions:

```
andreas@ubuntu:~$ npm install glob@2.0.9
[...]
andreas@ubuntu:~$ node
> require('glob').sync('/home/andreas/foo');
[ '/home/andreas/foo/' ]
```
issues.author: papandreou

comments.author: TooTallNate
comments.body: @isaacs This was an unexpected change for me as well. What's the deal?

comments.author: TooTallNate
isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, it's a bug.  Haven't had a chance to fix it yet.

The solution is to detect whether each pattern in the set is absolute or not, and only strip the cwd from each result if it was not an absolute pattern.

Also, weird though it may be, there were previous cases where `{/foo,/foo}` would return the same thing more than once, so each match needs to be tied to a given glob in the set.  That's the ultimate solution, though @TooTallNate's workaround is ok for the short-term.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed on master, published as glob 3.1

comments.body: Can you please try with the latest push to master?  Also published as glob@3.1.0.

comments.author: cadorn
comments.body: Cannot tell because `glob` is not working for me at all now. Seems to be a regression. Locked to 3.0.x for now. No time to look further ATM.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Could you explain a little bit what "not working for me at all now" means?

Is it throwing an error?  Hanging?  Doesn't install?

comments.author: cadorn
comments.body:
No error. Exact same input, now no output.

Code: https://github.com/sourcemint/bundler-js/blob/fa9e5a02e25e189b1d6efe6f53d6f399552798b7/lib/bundler.js#L176-196

Broad test case: https://github.com/sourcemint/platform-nodejs (npm test)

Things should fail.

I'll try and narrow things down when I have time if you don't beat me to it.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Aha.  It's getting confused when the cwd isn't process.cwd.

comments.author: cadorn
comments.body: Ah, that would explain it. What is the exat difference between the `cwd` and `root` options?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: v3.1.3 should have what you need.  Thanks!

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
`root` is used when you want patterns starting with `/` to be interpreted as being in some specific directory.

Seems like that's broken atm too, though.

comments.author: cadorn
comments.body: I thought that is what `cwd` is for. Can you explain the difference?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Say you have this structure:

```
a
+-- b
|    `-- c
+-- c
```

Then `glob('/c', {root: 'a', cwd: 'a/b'})` would match `a/c` but not `a/b/c`.  If you drop the slash, then you see that `glob('c', {root:'a',cwd:'a/b'})` would match the `a/b/c` file, but not the `a/c` file.

What's not immediately clear is how best to express this in the results.  I'm thinking that any and all patterns that start with `/` should show up in the results as a full absolute path, with the `root` config pre-pended.

Another option would be to resolve everything against the cwd, but that breaks the symmetry with how it works by default, when cwd is process.cwd(), and root is /.  The "evil owl" pattern `{*,/*}` returns `/etc, /usr, /home, etc...` for the "absolute" part, but `foo, bar, baz, ...` for the "relative" part.

Setting a root is important for handling situations like ignore files.  `/foo.txt` in a .gitignore will prevent `./foo.txt` from being included, but not `./bar/foo.txt`.

When building a tarball or something, though, you probably don't want to get the full path, since that'll result in adding a bunch of noise into your tarball, so this will have to be configurable, with the default perhaps being to prepend the root, and an option to disable, akin to rsync's '--no-implied-dirs' option.

comments.author: cadorn
comments.body:
Why not default `root` to `cwd` which defaults to `process.cwd()` and always return relative path from `root` in result.

Prefixing `root` to result paths requires me to strip it again in most cases (I need relative paths from a given root). Also, if you pass in `root` it is trivial for you to map the result array after and prefix `root` if you need to.

So result paths always start with `/` so you can do `PATH.existsSync(root + result[x])` where `root` may be `/etc` and `result[x]` = `/hosts`.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Because the default `root` is `/`, not the cwd.  The evil owl `{/*,*}` needs to return everything in `/` as well as everything in `.`.  So, by default, yes it _is_ prefixed.

If you want to disable this, set `{ nomount: true }` in the options object.

comments.body: v3.1.4 works now. Thanks.

issues.body:
```
andreas@ubuntu:~/foo$ npm install glob@3.1.1
npm WARN request@2.0.1 package.json: bugs['web'] should probably be bugs['url']

comments.body:
On Mon, Mar 12, 2012 at 7:06 PM, Isaac Z. Schlueter <

cool, thanks :-) but I took this as a chance to upgrade node :-)

##

issues.title: Rake's FileList like matching against several patterns
issues.body: Hi. I think it's useful to have general FileList like utility and it seems to be easy to extend current implementation to handle multiple include-exclude patterns. I am going to do it anyway, but it's much easier to do for creator.
issues.author: eldargab

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I don't know what you're actually asking for.  What change would have to be made in node-glob?

comments.author: eldargab
comments.body:
``` javascript
var glob = new Glob
glob.include('a/b/c')
glob.include('d/e')
glob.exclude('test')

glob.on('match', doSomeThing)
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I see.  Yes, supporting an array of glob patterns is planned for ignorefile support, but it'll probably be a different module.  Something like this:

``` javascript
var mg = new MultiGlob(['a/b/c', 'd/e', '!test'], options)
mg.on('match', doSomething)
```

If it turns out to not be much of a change, or too difficult to do externally, I might fold this into node-glob directly, and just have Glob take an array or a single pattern. Would this API satisfy your use-case?

comments.author: eldargab
comments.body: It should satisfy.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Turns out this is complicated and hard, and I ended up doing IgnoreFile as its own module.

issues.title: Glob filter
issues.body:
I need to be able to filter the result of a glob operation using gitignore rules.

Would it make sense to add a filter plugin API which I can use to implement the gitignore rule set?

Should I use glob through another lib that already does something like this?
issues.author: cadorn

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Doing gitignore handling is a much more involved process, since it requires the ability to pause and resume the walk at any arbitrary point.

I write https://github.com/isaacs/fstream-ignore to do this.  It's very extensible.  I'm using it in https://github.com/isaacs/fstream-npm.  However, if you just want the list of matches, it's a bit heavy, since it stats and reads every file.

If you want to add this functionality to glob, it should be a separate module.  This is strictly an implementation of glob(3) in javascript.

issues.title: Absolute paths on Windows do not work
issues.body:
Steps to repro:
- Create a folder C:\test
- Put some *.txt files in there
- Try to get them using glob.sync('/test/*.txt')

Note:
- All backslashes were correctly replaced by forward-slashes

Expected result:
- An array with a list of files of the folder C:\test

Actual result:
- [](empty array)

PS:
- It works using relative paths, it's only absolute paths that seem to be broken
issues.author: goloroden

comments.author: joewhite
comments.body:
When I try this, I do get results, although they're a bit odd.

```
> glob.sync('/temp/*.txt')
[ '\\temp/1.txt' ]
```

The initial `/` is unexpectedly replaced with a `\`, but I do get results. Using glob@3.1.9 with Node v0.6.12 on Windows 7 64-bit.

comments.author: unknownbrackets
comments.body:
The issue here is that this line:

read = prefix = path.join("/", prefix)

On windows turns into:

read = prefix = "\"

Which it then prepends onto things here:

if (prefix !== "/") e = prefix + "/" + e

The same line is causing issues for drive letters in issue 42.

-[Unknown]

comments.author: springmeyer
comments.body: I just hit this as well. I worked around it by passing in the absolute path for the `cwd` option, and then a relative path for the actual search term.

comments.author: kylealanhale
comments.body:
I had thought this was a minimatch problem, so I left a comment there outlining the inconsistencies: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/pull/11#issuecomment-9188506.

For now I am doing pretty much what @springmeyer suggested, with a normalization step following to clean up the weird path separators.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed in the latest release.

issues.title: Drive letters on Windows return empty results
issues.body:
If I pass `glob` a search spec that includes a drive letter, it returns an empty array.

```
> process.cwd()
'C:\\dev'
> glob.sync('/temp/*.txt')
[ '\\temp/1.txt' ]
> glob.sync('c:/temp/*.txt')
[]
```

The two queries should return the same results (since C: is the current drive), but when I specify the full path including drive letter, `glob` returns nothing.
issues.author: joewhite

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Confirmed.  That is a bug.

comments.author: unknownbrackets
comments.body:
The basic problem seems to be here:

read = prefix = path.join("/", prefix)

Essentially this turns it into /c:/temp/ which ain't right.

If I skip that for win32 (basically ignoring UNC, drives, etc.), and just use the else branch, it works fine.  That said, I'm not really interested in all the other features (like mounting) that this might possibly break, so probably nothing patch quality...

This is somewhat related to issue 40.

Actually, there's also another issue.  Using e.g. c:/\* splits into:

c:
*

Which of course has a different meaning on Windows.  c:\* means current directory, c:* means root of c:.  This could affect any drive letter.  I was able to work around this with basically this in the default case:

if (process.platform == "win32" && prefix.length === 2 && prefix[1] === ":")

Though I'm not sure if this is a complete fix either.

-[Unknown]

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Working on latest release.

issues.title: UNC (\\?\, \\hostname\) paths don't work
issues.body:
I realize these are "evil" for a glob, but I would normally expect this:

"//\?/C:/*"

To return results.  Realistically, on win32, it's reasonable to check if the path starts with //?/ (or \?\ but that's havoc, I understand) and stop right there.

I think the "root cause" is actually in minimatch, because it turns the pattern into /?/.

This is probably a pretty low priority issue.  It's honestly not uncommon for UNC paths not to work sometimes in programs, and except for remote resources it's easy enough to mount the volume to a path or use the drive letter.

-[Unknown]
issues.author: unknownbrackets

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I'm gonna call that a wontfix.  If you have a patch that does this in some elegant way, then fine, but otherwise just normal paths work with glob.

issues.title: using with require('graceful-fs') hangs writes, require('fs') works fine
issues.body:
I have a process which writes a large build package which I'm trying to convert to use node-glob rather than a homegrown glob routine. Unfortunately, while the sync() calls work great for read operations, it appears that graceful-fs is causing hanging write operations. 

This is running on node 0.6.20 on Mac OSX 10.7.4. Changing glob.js to require('fs') fixes everything. 

One question is would it be possible to change how glob operates such that you could choose the fs implementation you want? In my case I'd be happy with standard node. 

Alternatively, can you suggest any reason why graceful-js would cause this behavior? I've tried adjusting the limits on open files etc. but that didnt' seem to have any effect.

issues.title: "mark" option broken when pattern ends with "/"
issues.body:
Using `glob@3.1.12` and given this directory / file structure:

```
$ tree deep
deep
├── deep.txt
└── deeper
    ├── deeper.txt
    └── deepest
        └── deepest.txt
```

The `mark` option behavior appears to be inverted when the pattern ends with a `/`:

``` js
var glob = require('glob');

// Expected behavior:

glob.sync('deep/**')
[ 'deep',
  'deep/deep.txt',
  'deep/deeper',
  'deep/deeper/deeper.txt',
  'deep/deeper/deepest',
  'deep/deeper/deepest/deepest.txt' ]

glob.sync('deep/**', {mark: true})
[ 'deep/',
  'deep/deep.txt',
  'deep/deeper/',
  'deep/deeper/deeper.txt',
  'deep/deeper/deepest/',
  'deep/deeper/deepest/deepest.txt' ]

glob.sync('deep/**/')
[ 'deep/',
  'deep/deeper/',
  'deep/deeper/deepest/' ]

// Inverse behavior:

glob.sync('deep/**/', {mark: true})
[ 'deep',
  'deep/deeper',
  'deep/deeper/deepest' ]
```
issues.author: cowboy

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed by 1b0c060, put the wrong issue number in it.

issues.title: "nocase" option fails when * or ? is not present
issues.body:
When the glob pattern doesn't have any "fancy" characters in it, the `nocase` option doesn't appear to work.

The first two examples below behave as expected, but I expect the final example to return `[ 'Foo.txt', 'foo.txt' ]` instead of `[ 'Foo.txt' ]`.

I tested this in a Mac OS Extended (Case-sensitive, Journaled) partition disk image.

``` shell
$ npm install glob --silent
glob@3.1.20 node_modules/glob
├── inherits@1.0.0
├── graceful-fs@1.2.0
└── minimatch@0.2.9 (sigmund@1.0.0, lru-cache@2.0.4)

$ touch Foo.txt foo.txt

$ node -pe 'require("glob").sync("Foo.*")'
[ 'Foo.txt' ]

$ node -pe 'require("glob").sync("Foo.*", {nocase: true})'
[ 'Foo.txt', 'foo.txt' ]

$ node -pe 'require("glob").sync("Foo.txt", {nocase: true})'
[ 'Foo.txt' ]
```
issues.author: cowboy

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Very tricksy!  The reason is that, if you don't have any magic in the pattern, glob "shortcuts" and just checks for the exact string you gave it.  Since it's a case-sensitive file system, that means that it will only find that one exact thing, regardless of the `nocase` flag.

It would be nice if there were a way to detect a case-sensitive file system, but afaik, there is no such way.  I think perhaps we just need to treat `nocase` patterns as if they had magic at all levels, even if they don't.  Consider this:

```
mkdir /tMp
touch /tmp/foo
touch /tmp/fOo
touch /tMp/Foo
touch /tMp/foO

glob.sync('/tmp/foo', {nocase: true})
// expect all 4, so we need to test *every* part of the pattern!
```

A test would be awesome.  The only way I can see to test this would be to mock out the fs object, which is tedious, but not super hard.  You've messed around with glob a lot, wanna try writing a patch?  Probably just a matter of changing some check somewhere to assume that patterns are always magical if nocase is set.

comments.author: cowboy
comments.body: Is this entirely handled in minimatch, or is it in glob as well? I'm finding it difficult to puzzle through this one.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I think it's just in minimatch, iirc.  Search for `hasMagic`.  It should
default to `true` if nocase is set.  I think it currently defaults to
false, then gets set to true if there are any magic chars.  If there's no
magic, then it returns a string instead of a regexp.

On Thu, Feb 21, 2013 at 7:45 AM, Ben Alman notifications@github.com wrote:

> Is this entirely handled in minimatch, or is it in glob as well? I'm
> finding it difficult to puzzle through this one.
> 
> —
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHubhttps://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/59#issuecomment-13895393.

mm.match(["foo.txt", "Foo.txt"], "Foo.txt", {nocase: true}) // [ 'foo.txt', 'Foo.txt' ]
```

So I'm guessing the problem isn't in minimatch at all.

issues.title: Windows absolute paths ignores root path
issues.body:
The fix from isaacs/node-glob#53 causes paths prefixed with `/` to not mount the path on top of the passed in root path.

When I pass in `{ root: 'c:\foo' }` or something like that, and try to match `/bar` i will end up with `c:\bar` instead of what I expect, which is `c:\foo\bar`. Or at least I've understood that's how it works from reading the documentation.
- `root` The place where patterns starting with `/` will be mounted onto.  Defaults to `path.resolve(options.cwd, "/")` (`/` on Unix systems, and `C:\` or some such on Windows.)

```
path.resolve('/foo/bar', './baz')
// returns
'/foo/bar/baz'

path.resolve('/foo/bar', '/tmp/file/')
// returns
'/tmp/file'

path.resolve('wwwroot', 'static_files/png/', '../gif/image.gif')
// if currently in /home/myself/node, it returns
'/home/myself/node/wwwroot/static_files/gif/image.gif'
```

Passing in a leading `/` to any argument of `path.resolve()` seems to mount it at root. My suggestion is to not try and do too much to Windows absolute paths in `prefix`.

It all works for me when I uncomment these lines, but I understand it breaks the previous issue: https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/master/glob.js#L369-L370

comments.author: bcardarella
comments.body:
@isaacs I believe I have found the cause of the issue but need guidance on how best to fix.

https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/blob/master/minimatch.js#L237-L242
In minimatch: for any pattern that does not begin with a `"!"` the `for` loop is never run so the pattern is never marked as "negated". During the `parse` function the pattern is iterated over for any `!`s and will stringSub `^` to negate that pattern: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/blob/master/minimatch.js#L553

`parse` never uses the `negate` flag to flip the pattern. This is why `./(!whatever)` works but `!(whatever)` doesn't.

There could be two solutions:
1. The easiest is to just remove `parseNegate` all together. This might be ideal as the `parse` function seems to be better about where to negate. the `makeRe` function doesn't seem to be used, but it does respect the `negate` flag: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/blob/master/minimatch.js#L806. So `makeRe` could be removed
2. Refactor `parse` to use `makeRe` and respect the `negate` flag.

Thoughts on how to approach? I can work on the fix either way.

comments.author: bcardarella
comments.body: @isaacs I'm going to try to implement options 2 as the README seems to imply that `makeRe` is supposed to be used anyway.

comments.author: bcardarella
comments.body: Although now I'm confused. Upon further inspection this commit: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/commit/b458f5ddc281a07ca51de994f64e477191787b2a is from 2 and half years ago is when `makeRe` was removed from use in favor of `parse`. The commit message only mentions a "rewrite". Not really sure which is the best path to take. It seems that `parse` and `makeRe` are duplicating effort

issues.title: Duplicate matches in all subfolders when using evented / streaming glob
issues.author: chbrown

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Right, I see why this is happening.

Let's say you have a tree like this:

```
.
└── y/
    ├── d/
    │   └── e.c
    ├── a
    ├── a.c
    ├── b
    └── b.c
```

and you search for the glob pattern: `**/*.c`.  You want to find these things:

```
y/a.c
y/b.c
y/d/e.c
```

When it has a globstar, that can match 0 or more path segments.  So, the way that it does that is to read the dir, then test each child as if they were below the globstar, and if they replace the globstar.

So, you have a pattern like `[**, *.c]`.  So, you read the cwd, and get `[x, y]`.  For each child, test if it matches without the globstar, if the child replaces the globstar (to find its grandchildren) and also below the globstar (for further descendants).  This results in the following patterns:

```
[ *.c ]
[ y, *.c ]
[ y, **, *.c ]
```

We then process each of those.

The first matches nothing.

The children of y are `[a a.c b b.c d]`.  For the first pattern, this is simple, and then for the next, we do the same special globstar approach.

```
[ *.c ] --> [] (no matches)
[ y, *.c ] --> [ y/a.c, y/b.c ]
[ y, **, *.c ] -->
  [ y, *.c ] (without the globstar, note the duplicate!)
  [ y, a.c, *.c ]
  [ y, a.c, **, *.c ]
  [ y, b.c, *.c ]
  [ y, a, *.c ]
  [ y, a, **, *.c ]
  [ y, b, *.c ]
  [ y, b, **, *.c ]
  [ y, d, *.c ]
  [ y, d, **, *.c ]
```

I'm not sure just how much of a problem this really is.  After all, if you listen on the 'end' event (or just use the top-level function instead of the object) then you'll get the deduped list.  Solving this problem without breaking the other trickier examples would be pretty tough.

comments.body: I haven't read the `glob` source; how do the post-processing methods (like the `'end'` event or a naked `glob()`) de-dupe? If it's by using object keys as a set data structure, couldn't the same be applied in the streaming scenario?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> Ouch. The issue immediately before this one...

Haha, no worries.  I guess it just shows that more people are affected by this than I'd thought, that's all.

> how do the post-processing methods de-dupe?

Yes, it's using Object keys, as long as you don't set `nounique:true` in the options.  That flag is important if you want brace-expanded portions to behave exactly like Bash (where dupes will show in that case).

I think the right solution is not to dedupe along the way, but rather to find an algorithm for globstar that doesn't suffer from this shortcoming.

issues.title: ENOTDIR and ENOENT stat errors from glob
issues.body:
Spotted this while looking at some performance issues, not sure if this is a recent error.

First, a simplified example of the problem I am seeing.

So, we have a directory structure

```
.
└── c/
    ├── w/
    │   ├── f.json
    │   └── w.js
    ├── x/
    │   └── x.js
    ├── y/
    │   ├── f.json
    │   └── y.js
    ├── z/
    │   └── z.js
    └── a.txt
```

and are only interested in the f.json files, so a fragment of code (coffee script)

```
    glob path.join('c', '**', 'f.json'), (e, files) ->
       if e then return res.e(e)
       files = files.map (file) -> 
```

and while this only returns the f.json file, I am seeing ENOTDIR and ENOENT errors from stat operations from the glob code.

So, using the above directory example, ENOTDIR errors for stat on `./c/a.txt/f.json`, and ENOENT for stat on `./c/x/f.json`

Given that the real directory structure is significantly bigger, the glob is taking far longer than it should.

The stack trace for both these errors appear to be the same, which may or may not help

```
Stack trace
[0]: at Glob._stat (.../node_modules/glob/glob.js:533:8)
[1]: at Glob._process (/.../node_modules/glob/glob.js:336:12)
[2]: at Glob._process._readdir.pn (.../node_modules/glob/glob.js:427:14)
[3]: at Array.forEach (native)
[4]: at Glob._process (.../node_modules/glob/glob.js:426:9)
[5]: at Glob._afterReaddir (.../node_modules/glob/glob.js:635:15)
[6]: at Object.oncomplete (fs.js:297:15)
```
issues.author: paul90

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Expected behavior.

You're looking for `c/**/f.json`, right?  Well, glob does a readdir on a dir like `c/w`, and gets `f.json, w.js` as results.  At that point, it doesn't know whether the items returned are directories or files.  And, it doesn't care: `c/w/f.json` is a match, but if `f.json` is a dir, then `c/w/f.json/f.json` would also be a match, and `c/w/f.json/a/b/c/d/e/f/g/f.json` would be a match as well.  So, it does a readdir on `c/w/f.json`, which tells it two things: (1) Is f.json a dir? and (2) if it's a dir, what's inside it?  The ENOTDIR tells is that it's not a directory, and it moves on.

The fact that it's calling stat at all is a bit odd in this case.  Can you please provide an actual test script in JavaScript, with a real folder structure?

issues.title: Issue with UNC paths on Windows

comments.author: staticshock
comments.body:
Have a look at this pull request, which purports to implement decent UNC path handling. `\\host\dirname` is basically treated as if they were a drive letter, like `C:`, so wildcards don't work on that portion of the path, just like `*:` wouldn't have worked for a drive letter.

That can probably be improved, but, alas, I couldn't figure out a quick way to do it, because `path.resolve("\\\\host")` gets treated as a relative path and resolves to `$PWD\host`, so `\\host` can't be used as a valid root.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
There isn't a working fix to merge, or else I would have done so by now.  What you're waiting for is not "this fix to land", but rather "a fix to exist".

Here's what's needed for a valid solution, to this or any defect in any code I maintain:
1. Code that fixes the problem.
2. Tests that fail without the patch, and pass with the patch.
3. Those tests have to run on Unix systems (mock everything, I don't care, but the test has to actually run)

Without (2), it's not clear that the code actually works.  Without (3), the tests will start failing some day and no one will notice.  These aren't weird desert island requirements or unrealistic doom and gloom predictions.  They're just what happens.

I haven't had time to dig into this particular issue.  @stefanpenner made a courageous attempt, but then seems to have faded away with an ominous last message about a Pandora's Box.

I understand that this is important to you all.  That importance does not change the fact that I cannot accept changes that are going to reduce test quality.  Maybe if one of you work at a company that benefits from having a glob implementation in JavaScript, and is affected by this Windows-specific issue, you can try to convince your boss to let you spend a few days working on it.

issues.title: [FEATURE] option to sort by numeric value?
issues.body:
If you have files named:
- 1
- 9
- 10

then the resulting Array of filename will be sorted as:
- 1
- 10
- 9

This is because the sort is based on ASCII character value (I think), and the first character of "10" is "1", which is less than "9".

It would be neat if there was an option to sort based on numeric value, taking into account the actual value of all leading digits together.

issues.title: fs.readdir called twice as much as necessary with **/* pattern
issues.author: papandreou

comments.author: isaacs

issues.title: Performance order of magnitude slower than native
issues.body:
For patterns like `**/*.txt`, `node-glob` seems to be an order of magnitude slower than native globbing.

Here is a script to demonstrate this (save it as `node-glob/benchmark.sh`): https://gist.github.com/joliss/7469304

Here are the timings from [running this on my system](https://gist.github.com/joliss/7469324):
- Bash timing: 0m0.696s
- Node statSync and readdirSync timing: 0m0.977s
- Node glob.sync timing: 0m9.676s
- Node glob async timing: 0m22.130s (fluctuating somewhat)

Based on the recursive statSync + readdirSync performance, it seems that it should be _possible_ to make node-glob nearly as fast as native, at least for simple patterns.

This is an actual issue for me, with sets of only a few hundred files: In a JavaScript build tool I'm writing, the execution time of the most performance-critical code path is currently bottlenecked on `node-glob`. I know I could avoid using `node-glob` if I wanted to, but I'd love to expose globbing syntax in my tool's API.

I'm guessing that there are two possible sources for this slowness:
- Duplicate stat or readdir calls (related: #76)
- The pattern matching burns too many CPU cycles (perhaps [minimatch](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch) is the culprit)

If someone wants to dig into this and figure out what's going on, you'd be my hero!

If it's technically hard to fix this, I'd also like to find out sooner rather than later, because it means that I maybe shouldn't rely on globbing in my tool's API.
issues.author: joliss

comments.author: joliss
comments.body: https://github.com/joyent/node/issues/6662 seems related, but it's looking like node-glob is losing _another_ order of magnitude over the pure stat performance. (Curiously, it seems like Bash's own glob matching might also spend a decent chunk of CPU time on things that are not `stat` and `readdir`.)

comments.author: joliss
comments.body:
This continues to be a real problem in [Broccoli](https://github.com/joliss/broccoli): `glob` takes 100 ms to match 1000 files. So unless we want to go without globbing in the API, we're spending nearly 50%(!) of our rebuild time on globbing alone.

As for a solution, Node's `path` functions turn out to be [rather slow](https://twitter.com/jo_liss/status/425736822354767872) (see also https://github.com/joyent/node/commit/b9bec20), and `glob` uses them extensively. A good first approach might be attempting to replace them with primitive string manipulation. (Avoiding `path.join` made a big difference in a directory-walking function that I used in place of `glob('**')` in my library.) _Update:_ Isaac points out that there's also ["regexps and other monkey business"](https://twitter.com/izs/status/425876261253033984). Monkey business is definitely bad for performance too.

comments.author: joliss
comments.body: Mentioning by request: @trevnorris

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Why doesn't broccoli use `glob.sync` then?  It's a command-line builder-type app, right? (Or am I missing the point of it...?)

Except in extremely rare scenarios, async fs I/O will _always_ be strictly slower than sync fs I/O.  If you don't _need_ to be doing many things in parallel, there is no benefit.

This is my primary objection to saying "Just add `await`!" or other coro-style approaches to making code "look sync".  How it looks is the smallest downside of asynchronous code!  If you need to run lots of stuff in parallel, and responding consistently is worth a bit of a slowdown across the board, and a huge percentage of your time is spent in a waiting sleep mode, then async I/O is great.

This describes most web servers, but _not_ most command-line programs, which typically do a small number of things at a time, and care more about just blasting through it all as fast as possible.

comments.author: joliss
comments.body:
Oh, yes yes, agree 100% - I'm using `glob.sync` in Broccoli too. (I was just mentioning the async performance for completeness in the original report, and my complaining on Twitter today is rather unrelated to this issue.)

The reason I opened this issue is not the async performance, but the sync performance: It still takes >100ms to match 1000 files. Running a sample build with Broccoli on 1000 files, a single `glob.sync` call took up over over 50% of the entire build time.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Oh, ok.  Then yeah, it's just slow.  I've done a lot of combing through it to remove unnecessary syscalls, but haven't profiled the JS itself.  Would love patches to make it faster.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Ok!  Initial investigation suggests that the biggest source of the delay is reliance on node's `fs.readdir` to determine both directory-ness and contents.  Since this uses a file descriptor, it's getting wrapped by `graceful-fs` which is explicitly designed to trade speed in favor of managed resource utilization.  So that sucks.

Additionally, there's some just plain shoddy JavaScript that's accreted over time in the readdir callback handling that V8 is having trouble optimizing.  I think that whole area needs a thorough refactoring to remove a few steps, and especially since glob matching is being used in some very time-sensitive applications, all of the `.bind()` and `.call()` usage needs to be eliminated.  Wrapping callbacks to do stuff at the end, tons of anonymous closures being defined inside of methods, it's kind of a giant mess.  I have enlisted the help of [The Dark Pony Zalgo](http://blog.izs.me/post/59142742143/designing-apis-for-asynchrony) to have a single code path that is used for both sync and async behavior.  I thought that I could preserve sanity if His Dark Tendrils were buried deep enough below the API layer.  I was wrong.

Lastly, in the globstar case specifically, both bash 4.3 and the next version of node-glob have to do an `lstat` on all directories encountered in the crawl, since symlinks to directories should _not_ be crawled.  So, the `Node statSync and readdirSync timing` benchmark test is not quite an apples-to-apples comparison.  It should probably lstatSync each directory as well as readdiring it.  It's still a LOT faster than the node-glob tests, but falls a bit more behind the bash/zsh test cases, as one would expect.

I've got the bash43 style globstar behavior implemented properly on a branch now, but it's even slower.  I'd like to refactor and optimize it to get the speed up before merging into master, which will make this much better.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
On the splitsync branch, I added another 10x to the benchmark script, and got these numbers:

```
Bash timing: real   3m26.991s
Zsh timing: real    1m56.420s
Node statSync and readdirSync timing: real  2m0.762s
Node readdirSync error-catching timing: real    1m52.183s
Node glob.sync timing: real 3m43.829s
Node glob async timing:
FATAL ERROR: CALL_AND_RETRY_2 Allocation failed - process out of memory
benchmark.sh: line 70: 52477 Abort trap: 6           node -e '
  var glob=require("../");
  glob("**/*.txt", function (er, files) {
    console.log(files.length)
  })'
```

`glob.sync` is comparable to Bash 4.3 on a huge dataset, and the master async glob runs out of memory.

With the 100000 file set, it's this:

```
Bash timing: real   0m5.656s
Zsh timing: real    0m3.711s
Node statSync and readdirSync timing: real  0m3.291s
Node glob.sync timing: real 0m12.116s
Node glob async timing: real    0m39.727s
```

The sync approach is about 2.5 times Bash 4.3, 4x zsh and the naive node sync approach.

Next step is to refactor the async approach similarly, and then it's profile time.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Currently, `glob.sync` is about 1.5 to 2x the time of bash 4.3, and `glob` is about 4-5x the bash time.  Zsh is a little bit faster, but that's because its globstar still crawls symlinked dirs, so it gets away with saving an extra lstat call.

Calling this mission accomplished!  Look for massive speed improvements on glob 4.1.

comments.author: joliss
comments.body: Yay! Thanks @isaacs :)

comments.author: bpasero
comments.body:
This is some good reading how to implement fast file traversal in node.

@isaacs you wrote:

> Except in extremely rare scenarios, async fs I/O will always be strictly slower than sync fs I/O. If you don't need to be doing many things in parallel, there is no benefit.

I am not sure I follow, isn't async always faster because you can process directories in parallel which is happening a lot when recursing down a file system structure? Using sync APIs might be faster per method call but will always pay the penalty of sequential processing.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@bpasero Even if you do the file actions asynchronously, there is usually a limit to just how async the fs can be, and there is a hard limit to how many threads node will use to perform async fs operations.  (Once upon a time that was 4, but I recall it being increased, maybe there's a cleverer heuristic involved since last I saw that code, which was a few years ago.  It might not be as "hard", but there is still definitely a limit.)

So, if you are parallelizing more fs ops than node allocates threads, it's going to back up, and be effectively serialized anyway.  (Of course, "serialized" with multiple parallel tracks, as each thread finishes what it's doing and then takes on a new job.)

However, all of this is ultimately limited by the file system itself!  Spinning disks can only be in one position, so if you're trying to read 2 files in parallel, the fs will either thrash (making both operations slower than they would've been), or make one operation wait for the other.

Stat and readdir calls are less thrashy and more cacheable by the fs, since they're only dealing with the file link table (at least, on most Unixy systems).  But in fact, if every bit of data is available in the operating system's fs cache, they'll be very fast, and adding the async thread management overhead will be relevant.

"Parallelizing" operations on a single machine (whether that's a single cpu or a single disk) is more like "multitasking" than "teamwork".  When you parallelize things across a _network_, of course, it's a whole different story, which is why things like Hadoop are so great for massively big data problems, compared with writing a simpler program for a single machine.  But when you parallelize a bunch of operations that are effectively throttled by a single bit of hardware, as a general rule, you're almost certainly not going to make things faster.

Lots of qualifiers there.  It's highly dependent on a lot of factors, like what specific type of disk is involved, the operating system, the file system type, as well as just whatever else the machine happens to be doing at the time.

If you _are_ doing many things in parallel (for example, it's a web server, where you don't want to pause in replying to one user's HTTP request while you read a file for another user), then async is absolutely the way to go.  Node makes this the "default" path because it's designed for building network programs, where this usually matters.

If you're a web server, then providing users with a consistently fast connection pick-up, and a predictable first-byte time, is all that really matters.  The "average file read time" is 100% worthless as a metric, if you lost business because some unlucky user had to wait 2s for a web page to load, and decided to hit the back button.

But if you're a cli utility, especially one that doesn't make network requests, then the situation is completely reversed.  The better approach is to avoid thrashing the disk, and since you have exactly one user who is going to wait for every single task to be completed, reducing the average task time is the way to go, so sync isn't just _easier_, it's also _better_.

comments.author: bpasero
comments.body:
Yes, I also think it depends on the scenario and especially depends on what kind of complexity the code has that runs for each file node that is traversed. If all you do is just walking through the file system, I would not expect sync to be much slower than async. However, if for each node, there is some task happening that eats CPU cycles, being able to "offload" further traversing to other threads has a high performance impact.

I would assume node-glob would perform faster with async fs operations because for each path it needs to run its "glob" logic that potentially slows down the next fs call when done in sync.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> If all you do is just walking through the file system, I would not expect sync to be much slower than async.

What I'm saying is that your expectation is wrong. You should expect sync to be _faster_ than async.  Async is always slower, it just has less of an effect on slowing down _other_ things (servers etc.)

comments.author: yocontra
comments.body: Every implementation right now just globs everything and then checks the negations afterwards. When dealing with something like this `['**/*.js', '!node_modules/**']` the performance is awful because node-glob is still traversing the node_modules folder and finding results. This needs to be solved in node-glob itself otherwise the perf is going to stay bad. I think we can get a sizable bounty on this if somebody wants to dig in and make a PR for adding this.

issues.title: Callback function is called more than once
issues.body:
When using glob with a callback function, that function can be called multiple times when filesystem errors are encountered:

```
> var fs = require('fs')
> fs.readdir = function(path, cb) { cb(new Error()) }
> var glob = require('glob')
> glob('*', function(err, files) { console.log('callback', err, files) })
callback [Error] undefined
glob error [Error]
callback null []
```

Is this a bug or intended behavior?
issues.author: maxg

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yes, that is definitely a bug.

comments.author: despairblue
comments.body:
Any puppet module with tests, for example https://github.com/kietsy/kietsy-tinc

In that repo after initializing the test infrastructure
- `bundle install --path vendor/bundle`
- `bundle exec rake spec_prep`  
  issuing `node -e "console.log(require('glob').sync('spec/**/*spec.rb'))" > results` will result in a 5.6mb big results file. Doing this for a puppet module with like 5 dependencies will crash node after it consumed almost 2 gig of ram: 

```
node -e "console.log(require('glob').sync('spec/**/*_spec.rb'))" > result
FATAL ERROR: JS Allocation failed - process out of memory
[1]    14906 abort (core dumped)  node -e "console.log(require('glob').sync('spec/**/*_spec.rb'))" > result
```

So the problem is not an infinite loop, but rather that **node-glob** should stop at `foo/bar`, just like the shell globbing works (which only gives you unique files). Another solution would be to be able to tell **node-glob** not to follow symlinks at all.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Hm.  So, shell globbing doesn't "only give you unique files", nor does it "not follow symlinks".

Example:

```
$ tree
.
├── bar/
│   ├── baz -> ../baz/
│   ├── foo -> ../foo/
│   ├── barfile
│   └── lnsrcfile
├── baz/
│   ├── bar -> ../bar/
│   ├── foo -> ../foo/
│   └── bazfile
└── foo/
    ├── bar -> ../bar/
    ├── baz -> ../baz/
    ├── dir/
    │   ├── a/
    │   │   └── b/
    │   │       └── c/
    │   │           └── d/
    │   │               ├── foolink -> ../../../../../../bar/foo/bar/foo/
    │   │               ├── recurselink -> ../../../../../../
    │   │               ├── weirdlink -> ../../../../../../bar/foo/bar/
    │   │               └── deepfile
    │   └── dirfile
    ├── foofile
    └── lnfile -> ../bar/lnsrcfile

17 directories, 7 files

$ shopt -s extglob; shopt -s globstar

$ for i in +(foo|bar|baz)/**/*file; do echo $i; done
bar/barfile
bar/baz/bazfile
bar/foo/foofile
bar/foo/lnfile
bar/lnsrcfile
baz/bar/barfile
baz/bar/lnsrcfile
baz/bazfile
baz/foo/foofile
baz/foo/lnfile
foo/bar/barfile
foo/bar/lnsrcfile
foo/baz/bazfile
foo/dir/a/b/c/d/deepfile
foo/dir/a/b/c/d/foolink/foofile
foo/dir/a/b/c/d/foolink/lnfile
foo/dir/a/b/c/d/weirdlink/barfile
foo/dir/a/b/c/d/weirdlink/lnsrcfile
foo/dir/dirfile
foo/foofile
foo/lnfile
```

Note that symbolic links ARE being followed by `**`, and the "same" files DO appear multiple times (eg `bar/foo/lnfile`, `baz/foo/lnfile`).

It seems, however, that it does not explore symbolic links that reference a path portion of the current path to the symbolic link itself.  So, even though `foo/bar/foo/foofile` would match, it detects that `foo/bar/foo` refers to the `foo/` part of itself, so it doesn't explore that bit.

Furthermore, this `**` behavior appears to have changed between Bash 4.1 and Bash 4.3!  Probably because it is unwieldy to deal with a gigantic mess of results when there are recursive symbolic links.

The crash that you're seeing is happening because there are a large _number_ of recursive symbolic links, rather than a particularly _deep_ set of them, so you're hitting the memory limit before hitting the ECYCLE error from the fs.  Updating to Bash 4.3-style behavior would fix the problem.

comments.author: despairblue
comments.body:
It's actually crashing because of the large set of redundant results. There is just one symlink.

```
$ ll spec/fixtures/modules
total 4.0K
drwxr-xr-x 7 despairblue users 4.0K Oct 15 00:44 stdlib
lrwxrwxrwx 1 despairblue users   37 Oct 15 00:44 tinc -> /home/despairblue/vcs/git/kietsy-tinc
```

I used this script

```
#/usr/bin/bash

echo $*
```

and the tab completion of either bash or zsh as a reference and compared it to node-globs behaviour. [This gist](https://gist.github.com/despairblue/63e23514449bf889fa09) illustrates two cases, yours above and mine for a puppet module with one dependency. As one can see, the current shell globbing outputs each file only once, if there is another path to the same file it's omitted in the results. Don't know if it's always been like this, I can only test it with zsh 5.0.7 and bash 4.3.030 (oh and I just noticed that the script does not work with bash since tab globbing does not seem to be supported for bash, it just replaces the `spec/**/*_spec.rb` with the first match, instead of all matches, but `tree` works as expected and won't follow the symlink)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Your test is interesting, but non-authoritative.  The reference implementation that node-glob targets is bash 4's globstar and extglob behavior.  The example I posted above does show that Bash 4.1 and Bash 4.3 have different behavior, and it seems that Bash 4.3's globstar behavior is superior, so that's what we should target.

It doesn't matter what zsh or tab-completion do, ultimately, though this is one example of a use-case where the more "conservative" results are much more useful.

comments.author: despairblue
comments.body: You're right, I didn't grasp everything in your comment at first. Adopting the bash4.3 globstar behavior would fix this (and would actually matches the one from zsh). Just to be sure, the command `for i in +(foo|bar|baz)/**/*file; do echo $i; done` in your example illustrates the bash4.3 behavior, right? And if so, what was the bash4.1 behavior? I simply don't see two different outputs for the same command, but your comment suggests that you demonstrated both behaviors.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
No, I don't have bash 4.1 installed any longer (upgraded due to shellshock some time ago).

But if I re-generate the test fixtures with bash 4.3, I see different results.  So, yeah, we're doing the wrong thing now (but only because "the right thing" changed!)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Here's the diff in the test fixture results from bash 4.1 (used to generate them originally) and bash 4.3 (which I have now) https://gist.github.com/isaacs/f748618f744f61756f55

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
So, it seems like the behavior can be summed up as: "While expanding matches for a `**` section, do not explore any directories already part of that particular `**` expansion".

This, of course, is _after_ consecutive `**` segments are collapsed.

comments.author: despairblue
comments.body: :+1: Yep, if adopting bash's new behavior is OK for you that would be great. It would suffice my use case and probably the other two's referenced above as well.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Landed in 4.0, made MUCH faster in 4.1.

issues.title: Glob never returns, no error
issues.author: yanickrochon

issues.title: nonull results confusing when {} are used
issues.body:
Using `glob@3.2.9` I see this behavior:

``` js
var glob = require("glob");
glob.sync("*.{a,b,c}", {nonull: true}) // [ '*.a', '*.b', '*.c' ]
```

Which is consistent with bash 4.2.45:

``` sh
$ echo *.{a,b,c}
*.a *.b *.c
```

This seems to be a docs issue and not a code issue.. In the README, I see this explanation:

> `nonull` Set to never return an empty set, instead returning a set containing the pattern itself. This is the default in glob(3).

I'm not seeing **the pattern itself**, eg. `*.{a,b,c}` but instead I'm seeing something different. Maybe a different wording can be used?

Also, it would be helpful for troubleshooting purposes if there was an option to get back the pattern itself in a null match, but I understand if that's something you don't want to support here.
issues.author: cowboy

comments.author: cowboy
comments.body:
And FWIW, `minimatch@0.2.14` returns **the pattern itself**, which is different:

``` js
var minimatch = require("minimatch");
minimatch.match([], "*.{a,b,c}", {nonull: true}) // [ '*.{a,b,c}' ]
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
minimatch is more like fnmatch, not an exact corrollary to glob.

Glob is doing brace expansion, which is a shell-ism, rather than a glob-ism, per se.  On the shell, when you do `echo *.{a,b,c}` you are literally doing the exact same thing as `echo *.a *.b *.c`.  Check it out:

```
$ node -p process.argv *.{a,b,c}
[ 'node', '*.a', '*.b', '*.c' ]
```

I'd be happy to accept a doc patch that clarifies that brace expansion happens before anything else, just like with the shell.

issues.title: A brace expression with only one member returns an empty list
issues.body: I would expect `glob('{*}')` to return every item in the current working directory, but it instead returns `[]`. Is that an unreasonable expectation?
issues.author: NickHeiner

comments.author: isaacs
NickHeiner
comments.body: Fair enough - thanks.

comments.author: uclaeamsavino
comments.body: Glad I searched for the right thing in the issues here - this was driving me crazy. I was trying to test braces with one element before adding another.  Something in the Readme might help - also maybe a bunch of examples of legal and not legal patterns. I'll be glad to add it and submit a PR.

issues.title: glob uses ^ semver, but lists engines: {node: "*"}
issues.body:
Should list engine as v0.10+ and "engineStrict": true.

This is currently breaking a lot of modules in v0.8 that depend on `glob` including `jshint` and `mocha`. I will say though, that part of the problem is that they are using >= semvers instead of fixing their major versions, which is also a cause of this.

At any rate, if it is your intention for v4 to be unsupported in node v0.8, please be explicit and ideally also set engineStrict to true to avoid breaking other modules in this manner.

Happy to submit a pull request if you'd like. =)

issues.author: mreinstein

comments.author: mreinstein
comments.body: not sure if it matters but I'm running `npm@1.4.13`

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
What's happened is that you installed both browserify and glob.  Since browserify's glob dep was satisfied by the fact that you already had a glob 3.x there, it didn't install a second copy.  However, when you then installed glob 4.x, that clobbered the glob that browserify was depending on.

`npm i browserify` will fix the situation.

comments.author: mreinstein
comments.body: ah, derp I see what you mean now. re-installing browserify forces npm to re-evaluate it's dependencies and add the missing ones. Thanks, and sorry for the issue clutter!

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @mreinstein YES! this is something I'm sorta working on part-time, whenever I'm slacking from my CEO duties.  It's part of the "refactor install to not be so dumb" project.

issues.title: Glob returning ["0", "expected_result"]
issues.body:
**EDIT: Not sure this is a real issue anymore; I found the problem while writing this ticket**

My project is using grunt-jasmine-node-coverage, which internally uses glob 3.2.x (Note: I also tried manually updating that to the latest version 4.0.x) to get a list of coverage.json files; this worked for awhile, but then it started breaking, and when it did, `glob.sync('coverage/node/*/coverage.json')` started returning `["0", "/path/to/my/project/coverage/node/Node.js 0.10.22/coverage.json"]` which was not expected.

Even stranger is that it seems to be tied to one of my spec files in particular. If I disable all of the tests in that one specific file, the issue goes away, and glob.sync starts returning `["/path/to/my/project/coverage/node/Node.js 0.10.22/coverage.json"]` again.

After more digging in, I (just now) determined that the following buggy line (in MY code) was responsible for breaking glob: `Array.prototype.push(result, args);` Note the missing `.apply` after push (boom)!

issues.title: Is there a way to determine if the pattern to glob is actually just (one or more) flat files ?
issues.author: doberkofler

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I don't understand the question.

What is a "flat file"?

comments.author: doberkofler
comments.body: Sorry for the unclear question. With flat file as was referring to a physical file that actually exists in the files system in contrast to a pattern that will be expanded to no, one or multiple files.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: So you mean, you want to know if it has any special characters in the glob pattern?

comments.author: doberkofler
comments.body: Yes, but generally speaking, I would like to know if the pattern is for one specic file of might expand to 0 to n files.

comments.author: staticshock
comments.body: Why not just expand the glob and check `.length`?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Well, technically speaking, _any_ pattern might expand to 0 files.  But it's not hard to add a function that just returns true or false if the glob has any pattern segments in it.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The fact that you say "one more more" files is confusing to me.  I'm going to add a `hasMagic` function since it's pretty easy and would be very useful, and I _think_ it meets your needs, but I'm going to need more detail if not.

Thanks.

comments.author: doberkofler
comments.body:
Maybe I best explain it based on my actual use case:
We use glob as part of our Gulp based build system and typically there are two kind of uses.
Either we process one single file or we process a group of files.
In the case that one specific file needs to be processed, we assume that the file must exist and if it does not most likely something changed or is wrong (typically the file was renamed, is missing or was misspelled) in the build environment itself.
We would therefore like to identify a glob pattern as:
a) one that can only expand to exactly one file (or none if something is wrong)
b) one that can expand to 0 or many files (if there are any wildcards in it)
In the case of a and if the glob pattern does not expand to exactly one file, we would simply throw an error and alter the user that something must be wrong.

Basically we would need a method that allows us to differentiate between a glob pattern a or b before we execute it.

issues.title: nocase + drive letter on Windows = match nothing
issues.author: whitetrefoil

comments.author: whitetrefoil
comments.body:
If I move the JS file to somewhere in C:, and remove `C:` from the pattern

``` javascript
glob('/Temp/*.txt',...
```

then run it again, it will output the correct:

```
C:\tmp>node glob-test.js
case
[ 'C:/Temp/t.txt', 'C:/Temp/t2.txt' ]
nocase
[ 'C:/Temp/t.txt', 'C:/Temp/t2.txt' ]
```

comments.author: staticshock
comments.body:
The root cause is that minimatch might return a regex for `pattern[0]`, which glob.js isn't ready for:

``` javascript
// Good minimatch pattern (nocase === false):
pattern = [
  "C:",
  "Temp",
  /^(?!\.)(?=.)[^/]*?\.txt$/
]
prefix = "C:/Temp"

// Bad minimatch pattern (nocase === true):
pattern = [
  /^(?=.)C:$/,
  /^(?=.)Temp$/,
  /^(?!\.)(?=.)[^/]*?\.txt$/
]
prefix = null  // ...fails to match ./C:/Temp/*.txt
```

comments.author: staticshock
comments.body: See pull request #146 for a fix

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: The negation semantics are really odd, actually, and I've thought a lot about ripping them out.  It shouldn't have anything to do with symlinks, though.

comments.author: dead-claudia
comments.body: @isaacs Although I don't exactly have access to a computer where I can at the moment, I will note that the negation syntax is awesome for filtering out stuff without a complicated mess.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@impinball Yeah, totally.  It's just weird when it involves `**` stuff, and also it still only is limited to whatever non-globby prefix you provide, since this library takes a TON of shortcuts to avoid unnecessary directory walking.

So, for example, the pattern `!b*/c` will match anything named `c` that is not in the directories starting with `b`.  `!b/*` will not return anything.  `!b*/**` will match anything at any depth under any directory other than those that match `b*`.

This is clearly all wrong.

What I think you usually want is more like `** but not anything matching the pattern`.

For example, if my pattern is `!app/*` then it should match any file in the cwd, but _not_ anything in the `app` dir.  But should it match `app/x/y`?  That isn't matched by `app/*`, so it seems like perhaps it should be matched by `!app/*`?  But that means that `!` will always imply a massive `**` walk, and you might be very surprised for it to go more than one level deep.

Do you see what I'm getting at?  I'm a bit hesitant to change the negation behavior unless the change is to (a) completely remove it, or (b) first define what would be sensible.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
If you are interested in making it sensible, please go weigh in at https://gist.github.com/isaacs/fc635c852c64ffabf2fd

For the time being, unfortunately, this issue will not be addressed, because any change will make things worse.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Indeed, you've found a discrepancy between bash 4.3 and node-glob!  This is a bug!  Posted as https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/135

issues.title: Symlink behavior difference from Bash 4.3
issues.body:
From #134 

``` bash
$ tree
.
└── yo/
    ├── normalDir/
    │   ├── sym -> ../symDir/
    │   └── normFile.txt
    └── symDir/
        └── symFile.txt

4 directories, 2 files

$ for i in yo/**; do echo $i; done
yo/
yo/normalDir
yo/normalDir/normFile.txt
yo/normalDir/sym # symlink is not followed
yo/symDir
yo/symDir/symFile.txt

$ node -p 'require("glob").sync("yo/**").join("\n")'
yo
yo/normalDir
yo/normalDir/normFile.txt
yo/normalDir/sym
yo/symDir
yo/symDir/symFile.txt

$ for i in yo/**/*.txt; do echo $i; done
yo/normalDir/normFile.txt
yo/normalDir/sym/symFile.txt # however, files in symlink dir ARE returned
yo/symDir/symFile.txt

$ node -p 'require("glob").sync("yo/**/*.txt").join("\n")'
yo/normalDir/normFile.txt
yo/symDir/symFile.txt  # BUG! Lacking yo/normalDir/sym/symFile.txt!
```

The solution is not "follow symlinked dirs" as suggested in #134. The correct behavior is "stop following symlinked dirs as part of a globstar match, but still resolve their contents against subsequent parts of pattern".  The "fake a readdir response by returning `[]` as if the dir was empty" is not adequate.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@seanmwalker 

The intent wiht node-glob v4 is to match the behavior of Bash 4.3.  This is a breaking change, hence the bumped major version number.

So, with this structure:

```
~/my-module
~/my-module/lib
~/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder -> ~/symDir
~/my-module/lib/localFolder
~/my-module/lib/localFolder/aScriptFile.js
~/my-module/package.json
~/my-module/server.js
~/symDir
~/symDir/bar
~/symDir/bar/anotherThing.js
~/symDir/foo
~/symDir/foo/deeper
~/symDir/foo/deeper/more.js
~/symDir/foo/something.js
```

Bash 4.3 returns this:

```
$ echo $BASH_VERSION
4.3.30(6)-release

$ for i in ~/my-module/lib/**/*; do echo $i; done
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/bar
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/foo
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/localFolder
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/localFolder/aScriptFile.js
```

Bash 3.2 does this:

```
$ echo $BASH_VERSION
3.2.48(1)-release

$ for i in ~/my-module/lib/**/*; do echo $i; done
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/bar
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/foo
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/localFolder/aScriptFile.js
```

Node-glob does this:

```
$ node -p 'require("glob").sync(process.env.HOME + "/my-module/lib/**/*").join("\n")'
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/bar
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/aSymlinkedFolder/foo
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/localFolder
/Users/isaacs/my-module/lib/localFolder/aScriptFile.js
```

which is identical to Bash 4.3, working as designed.

issues.title: Changelog in the documentation
issues.body:
You should add a changelog in the readme file (even on the release description).

Updating to `4.1.0` from `4.0.6` made my project throw errors and I had to dive in the module code in order to fix it. I would prefer (I assume others as well) find the solution for things that break compatibility in the documentation.

PS: Be careful with versioning! A minor update should not break the system. Show a warning or something. As they say in the [Semantic Versioning](http://semver.org/):

> MINOR version when you add functionality in a backwards-compatible manner
issues.author: razvanz

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This project's goal is to match the behavior of Bash 4.3.  Any changes that bring it closer to the matching semantics of Bash 4.3 are considered bugfixes and bump the patch version.

Opt-in features that provide alternative behavior are considered additive changes, and bump the minor version.  Minor versions may also include bugfixes.

Removal of opt-in features, or changes to the API surface, are considered breaking changes, and bump the major version.  Major versions may also include bugfixes and additive changes.

If you are a depending on a bug, then a bugfix will break your system.

I'm happy to include a changelog, but I'd rather use a tool to generate it manually, as this is quite tedious.  Do you have any recommendations?

issues.title: Add an option to return real paths for symlinks
issues.body:
Node Inspector is using `glob` to find all javascript files that may be loaded later by `node` in order to allow developers to set breakpoints in files before they are loaded. This is useful e.g. when debugging unit-tests via `node --debug-brk`, as the test files are loaded later by the test runner.

At the moment, this mechanism does not work for symlinked files (typically via `npm link {module}`), because `glob` returns paths in project's node_modules folder, while node resolves all symlinks to real paths before passing the filename to V8.

I am proposing to add a `glob` option to tell it to convert symlinks to real paths. While this can be implemented by Node Inspector as a post-processing step calling `fs.realpath` on all items returned by `glob`, it seems to me that we can get better performance by implementing this feature in glob, as it is already `fs` API.

**Example**

File layout:

```
~/my-module
~/my-module/node_modules
~/my-module/node_modules/debug -> /usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug
~/my-module/index.js
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug/debug.js
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug/node.js
```

Expected result for `glob('**/*.js', { cwd: '~/my-module', realpaths: true })`:

```
~/my-module/index.js
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug/debug.js
/usr/local/lib/node_modules/debug/node.js
```
issues.author: bajtos

comments.author: bajtos
comments.body: Related: #139

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Patch welcome.  Make sure to add tests for both sync and async behavior.  The option should be `realpath:true` (to better match `stat:true` behavior).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Make sure to take advantage of the cache option to fs.realpath, so that it can avoid statting the same paths multiple times.

comments.author: bajtos
comments.body: Thanks. I don't have time to work on this right now, but perhaps one of Node Inspector users will be willing to contribute the patch.

issues.title: Match event order depends on the platform
issues.body:
I run into this when trying to figure out why gulp outputs files in [different order on windows](https://github.com/gulpjs/gulp/issues/687#issuecomment-64544815). This used to work with older version of gulp. Detective work pointed to this graceful-fs change: https://github.com/isaacs/node-graceful-fs/commit/08471b246bec73848b53e9ad4972f5fe02031805

If this was to be "fixed" now the most convenient and probably the only place would be in node-glob and it would be to sort each [readdir result](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/c5313b5e3a5f5227617b40d8914a41d5dfc8c095/glob.js#L453):

`self._readdirEntries(abs, entries, cb)` -> `self._readdirEntries(abs, entries.sort(), cb)`

I run benchmarks to see what kind of performance penalty there is but couldn't see anything consistent. The following is three run average and numbers are: [before fix] -> [after fix] (percentage change):

```
Node statSync and readdirSync timing:

    real 1.681 -> 1.633 (-2.9%)
    user 1.138 -> 1.123 (-1.3%)
    sys  0.530 -> 0.528 (-0.4%)

Node glob.sync timing:

    real 3.921 -> 3.879 (-1.1%)
    user 3.314 -> 3.284 (-0.9%)
    sys  0.647 -> 0.635 (-1.1%)

Node glob async timing:

    real 7.752 -> 7.816 (+0.8%)
    user 7.461 -> 7.515 (+0.7%)
    sys  1.501 -> 1.524 (+1.5%)
```
issues.author: heikki

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I see, so gulp is responding to `match` events rather than the list of all results which is sorted at the end.

Glob no longer uses graceful-fs, so that would be why the change happened.

comments.author: yocontra
comments.body: @isaacs Yep, this lets us start processing files as soon as they are found vs. waiting for the whole glob to be finished. Thanks for the quick fix!

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@contra @heikki I'm sorry to be the bearer of bad news.

It turns out that sorting the readdir results isn't actually enough of a fix, and in fact, it _can't_ be reliably fixed in the general case without some truly awful things.

Consider this:

```
a
├── b/
│   └── z/
└── c/
    └── z/
```

If you glob for `a/*/z` then it does the first readdir on the `a/` dir.  Some platforms this returns `['b','c']` and others, `['c','b']`.  So, we sort it.

Then, for each of those two dirs, _in parallel_, it does a `readdir` to see if there's a `z` inside.  They'll both hit the thread pool at effectively the same time, just a few ns off.  But sometimes, `readdir('a/c')` will return before `readdir('a/b')`, so even though we sort every readdir, we still get `a/c/z` before `a/b/z`.

To truly fix this, we'd have to somehow sort the `readdir` responses _and also_ sort the return processing from each child branch to manage the order that they return in.  That's way beyond my threshold for complexity.  A simpler approach would be to serialize the processing of each child dir, but then we'll be leaving a lot of performance on the table (like, 4x in some cases).

With that in mind, I'm going to revert this "fix", because it adds complexity and CPU cycles for no benefit.

You'll just have to handle out-of-order results.  They are sorted in the final tally, though, so you can either have them sooner (and maybe out of order), or all at once (and sorted).  Trade-off appropriately for your use case, I guess.

comments.author: heikki
comments.body: @isaacs Ok, thanks for info. Have to figure out alternative.

issues.title: Fails when pattern contains '[' char
issues.body:
Hi everybody,

I'm using karma which using node-glob. My karma test run locally but does not run when TFS launch it. The different was the path : 'C:\Build\7[Dev] - refonte\Myproject\'. In this path glob lib convert '[' in strange char. I don't know why ?
issues.author: felixbillon

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
`[` is a magic char in globs, just like regexps.  It'd be looking for:

```
C:\Build\7D - refonte\Myproject\
C:\Build\7e - refonte\Myproject\
C:\Build\7v - refonte\Myproject\
```

Escape the `[` with a `\` char, like `"C:\\Build\\7\\[Dev\\] - refonte\\\Myproject\\"` in js.

I'm on OSX BTW.

Sorry the issue seems to be related to `mem-fs-editor` which is using your lib, but no on your side, my bad.

issues.title: Clarify behavior of "*.(xxx|yyy)"
issues.body: In reading the "Glob Primer" section, I'm wondering what would be the matching behavior of a pattern like "_.(js|css)". I would expect this would match "_.js" and "_.css", but not "_.jscss" or "*.cssjs". However, nothing is said about the behavior of a "(xxx|yyy)" block WITHOUT a prefix character specifying the cardinality. If my guess about the behavior is correct, then I wonder what is the point of the "@" prefix character anyway, if the default is essentially the same?
issues.author: davidmichaelkarr

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: `*.(xxx|yyy)` isn't a valid glob pattern.  `(xxx|yyy)` is a regexp, which is equivalent to the `@(xxx|yyy)` glob pattern.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: You'd want to do `*.@(xxx|yyy)`

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Compare to Bash 4.3

```
$ echo *.@(xxx|yyy)
foo.xxx foo.yyy

$ echo *.(xxx|yyy)
bash: syntax error near unexpected token `('

$ node -p "require('../glob').sync('*.(xxx|yyy)')"
[]

$ node -p "require('../glob').sync('*.@(xxx|yyy)')"
[ 'foo.xxx', 'foo.yyy' ]
```

Here’s the strange thing: The subfolder pattern is matching files as well, but when I use the pattern `*/`, no files are matched. I’ve uploaded an example for your perusal.

I’m not sure if this is a `node-glob` issue or a `minimatch` issue, but since I’ve encountered it while using `node-glob`, I put it here.

Using node 0.10.36 and node-glob 4.3.5. node-glob is `g` in the screenshot below.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/13781/6052280/c0d5b186-ac85-11e4-9ef7-07e1373853a8.png)

At the moment I’m using a silly workaround to filter out files, but I’d rather have the glob pattern do that for me.

Is this a bug or intended behaviour? I did some issue searching and #49 looks tangentially related, but that was ages ago.

Here’s the output:

Contents of `~/glob-test`:

```

issues.title: having trouble with negation patterns
issues.body:
Given the following files, how would i write negation pattern to exclude index.html and js/main.js?
- index.html
- js/lib.js
- js/main.js

```
glob('!(index.html|js/main.js)**/*', function(e,files){console.log(files)}) # => []
```
issues.author: lfender6445

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Try this:

``` javascript
glob('**', { ignore: [ 'index.html', 'js/main.js' ], nodir: true }, console.log)
```

Negative extglob patterns like `!(foo)` cannot contain slashes.  So, another approach might be to do something like one of these, depending on how many _other_ files are in there that youer' trying to exclude.

``` javascript
> glob.sync('!(*.html)/!(main.js)', { nonegate: true })
[ 'js/lib.js' ]
> glob.sync('js/!(main.js)', { nonegate: true })
[ 'js/lib.js' ]
> glob.sync('js/!(main.js)')
[ 'js/lib.js' ]
```

(Note that the `nonegate` is required for now to interpret the first part as a negative extglob section rather than a fully negated pattern.  The next major release will deprecate using negated patterns, in favor of using ignores instead.)

comments.author: ilanbiala
comments.body: @isaacs does `ignore` accept glob syntax as well?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @iLanbiala yes it does.

fourcube@devbox:/tmp$ stat bar

1. A symlink is set on a temporary directory by X

issues.title: Has v4.4.1 been removed?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yes, this was on purpose.

`rimraf` version 2.3.0 depends on `glob@^4.4.1`, which has a bug that is not present in `glob@4.4.2`.

Because `rimraf` is so widely used, this caused a major problem for many users.
