issues.title: Allow setup path separator of result
issues.body:
Ok. glob() not accept match with back-slash. But the result need match with system path.sep, or at least, allow we set it manually. Else, in some cases, we need replace it "manually".

My suggestion is an option to set path separator like `{ "sep": path.sep }` or `{ "sep": "/$" }`.
issues.author: rentalhost

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I would be open to a change to support back-slashes on windows machines.  Probably the best solution would be to, on windows, replace all back-slashes with forward-slashes, and then swap back to back-slashes in the result.

Part of the blocker to doing stuff for windows is that I don't have a windows environment for CI tests, and it's quite tedious to run tests in Windows since I develop in a unix environment.  Do you have any tips on this?

comments.author: rentalhost
comments.body:
I did not understand this part:

> replace all back-slashes with forward-slashes, and then swap back to back-slashes in the result.

You mean, on unit test, you will replace `\` to `/` to match with result, is that? If yes, is a good idea.

I opened a topic on SO to listen someone about test unit in all systems: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28918855/running-node-tests-in-all-systems-without-have-it

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
No, I mean, at the very start, we replace `\` with `/`, so that the logic all works as it is.  Then, before emitting a `match` event or calling the cb with the results, we swap back to `\` so that you get paths that match the system.  (Of course, this is a bit complicated, because UNC paths in windows have to remain with `/` chars.)

Such a change would have to come with tests, and I'd want to run those tests regularly to make sure they don't break.

comments.author: ryanblock
comments.body:
👋 
Re: https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/173#issuecomment-77702787 just got bitten by wrong path delimiters on Windows.

But hey, good news: GitHub Actions supports Windows! Would a GitHub Actions PR with Windows testing be helpful in working towards normalizing path delimiters per platform and closing this issue?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
As of v9,

- paths are always returned in the results with their canonical platform-specific path separators
- glob patterns must use `/` for their path separation, unless `windowsPathsNoEscape` is used, in which case `/` is _only_ a path separator, and may not be used to escape glob characters.

issues.title: Allow check if a filename match by glob pattern.
issues.body:
For instance:

```
// Very useful.
glob.match("**/*.less", "myfile.less"); // true
glob.match("**/*.less", "path/myfile.less"); // true
glob.match("**/*.less", "path/myfile.js"); // false
glob.match("**/*.less", "path/myfile.css"); // false

// Optional 1
glob.match([ "**/*.js", "**/*.less" ], "myfile.less"); // true
glob.match([ "**/*.js", "**/*.less" ], "path/myfile.less"); // true
glob.match([ "**/*.js", "**/*.less" ], "path/myfile.js"); // true
glob.match([ "**/*.js", "**/*.less" ], "path/myfile.css"); // false

// Optional 2
glob.match("**/*.less", [ "file.less", "other/file.less" ]); // return [ "file.less", "other/file.less" ]
glob.match("**/*.less", [ "file.css", "other/file.less" ]); // return [ "other/file.less" ]
glob.match("**/*.less", [ "file.css", "other/file.css" ]); // return [] or false
```

Definition:

```
glob.match(string, string): boolean; // First example.
glob.match(string[], string): boolean; // Optional 1.
glob.match(string, string[]): string[]; // Optional 2.
glob.match(string[], string[]): string[]; // Optional 1 and 2 together.
```
issues.author: rentalhost

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: You want minimatch, not this module.

If you want to have a script that does it automatically, you need have each commit in a specific format. 

If you don't want to do that, what I've done in other projects is before each release look through the commits since the last release and determine which you should let users know about (interface changes, bug fixes, new features). You could use the following command to get the commits since the last release: `git log <last-version-tag>..master`.

I doubt backfilling the changelog to the initial version is all that useful anyway, so just starting one now and adding to it with each new release would be greatly appreciated.

issues.title: ignore certain folders
issues.body:
I have a project that looks like this

```
ls foo/
- file0.js
- a/file1.js
- b/file2.js
- c/file3.js
- d/file4.js
```

How do I write a [glob pattern][1] to **exclude the c & d folder** but get all other javascript files?

I imagine the solution would look similar to this:

```
glob('**/*.js, {ignore: ['c','d']}, function(e,f){console.log f})
```

I am wanting back 

```
- file0.js
- a/file1.js
- b/file2.js
```
issues.author: lfender6445

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Use the "ignore" feature.

```
glob('**/*.js', { ignore: '{c,d}/**' }, cb)
```

comments.body: ahh just found ignore, let me try that

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: The issue here is that you can't put slashes inside of extglob patterns.  They can only refer to path portions.

issues.title: realpath results different from Glob eventEmitter matches
issues.body:
realpath results are absolute paths and when there are symlinks to the same directory, de-dup'ing will produce a smaller set of results than the eventEmitter matches.

i'm not sure if the inconsistency with `match` events was intentional -- if it was,  perhaps a mention in readme would be helpful.

otherwise, if you plan to make `match` events consistent with realpath results, it would be helpful, when both `realpath` and `follow` are set, to use realpath matches to avoid symlink cycles during directory traversal. This would help prevent inadvertent long delays in tools like node-inspector when module developers accidentally create cycles with `npm link`.
issues.author: jldec

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Doc patch welcome.

The `match` event is the specific thing that matched, as matches are found.  It is not deduplicated in the normal case, and is not yet resolved to a realpath.  If we had to move the `fs.realpath` call up earlier in the process, then this would require some inconvenient refactoring.

comments.author: jldec
comments.body:
posted small PR #185 for the readme.

the [node-inspector](https://github.com/node-inspector/node-inspector/pull/586) issue i was working on doesn't require streaming matches from glob a.t.m - but probably as more modules adopt streaming this will resurface.

thanks for implementing the `realpath` and `follow` options!

issues.title: Absolute ignore doesn't work
issues.body:
Absolute ignores are not working correctly.

Here's an example using the test fixtures:

``` js
glob('**', {
  ignore: [ '/abcdef', '/abcdef/**' ],
  cwd: 'a',
  root: path.resolve('a')
})
```

This still finds "abcdef", "abcdef/g", and "abcdef/g/h".
issues.author: feross

comments.author: feross
comments.body: Here's a PR with a failing test: https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/pull/190

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This is tricky.

The idea for ignores is "exclude all potential matches which would be returned by a search for any of the ignore patterns".  It's not "exclude all potential matches which refer to files that would be found by any of the ignore patterns".

So, for example, `/a/b/c` _doesn't_ return `a/b/c` as a match, even if that file exists.

```
> matches = glob.sync('tmp/x/y/z', { cwd: '/' })
[ 'tmp/x/y/z' ]
> ignore = glob.sync('/tmp/x/y/z', { cwd: '/' })
[ '/tmp/x/y/z' ]
> require('array-intersection')(matches, ignore)
[]
```

If we tell it to give us the realpath value, then it'll do the right thing:

```
> real = glob.sync('tmp/x/y/z', { cwd: '/', ignore: '/private/tmp/x/y/z', realpath: true })
[]
```

That's because the `real` option makes it so that you only get absolute paths.

In order to satisfy the test in #190, we'd have to add a `path.resolve` and double-check any non-absolute results.  And I'm not sure it's really the ideal semantics, but it's worth discussing the trade-offs.  At least, it's a breaking change.

Regarding feross/standard#116, it's worth noting that `glob('**', { ignore: gitignoreLines })` is not quite what an ignore file does, anyway.  The patterns and the file names all need to be mounted on the directory where the ignore file lives.  You could check out https://github.com/npm/fstream-ignore if you want to use the same file selection logic that npm uses for ignore files in packages.

issues.title: UNC Path on Windows 64bit gives empty array
issues.author: DilipK01

comments.author: DilipK01
comments.body: Upon submitting this issue I noticed that all my double-backslashes are converted to single-backslashes. For example, four backslashes are converted to two backslashes, and two backslashes are converted to single backslashes. (I might have some typo also) But I think you get the point.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
In JavaScript, you must always double-up your back-slashes because backslash is the escape character.  So, for example, `a\nx` is the string `{0x61 0x0a 0x78}` (a, LF, x), but `'a\\nx` is the string `{0x61 0x5c 0x6e 0x78}` (a, slash, n, x).

Glob has some difficulties with UNC paths.  I'm guessing this is a duplicate of #74.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: No longer an issue in v9.

On the documentation, this is written :

comments.body:
The documentation suggests to use the [`ignore` option](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob#options):

```
It is better to use the ignore option to set a pattern or set of patterns 
to exclude from matches. If you want the "everything except x" type of behavior, 
you can use ** as the main pattern, and set an ignore for the things to exclude.
```

issues.title: top-level like function definition under the function scope
issues.body:
Actual issue is : `functions can only be declared at top level or immediately within another function.` (message from v8 under strict mode)

There are some next, done etc. function definitions (under another functions scope) raising this problem under glob.js. I can happily contribute to the fix ?
issues.author: obastemur

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I don't see the error you're describing.  How are you getting this?

comments.author: obastemur
isaacs
obastemur
comments.body:
Although conditional scope is yet another issue, the piece of code Ive shared (from glob.js) exactly samples the problem you have shared.

That's the reason I had opened this issue. So, are you planning to apply it to glob or carry the functions to top?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
You are getting confused about the difference between "function declaration" and "function expression".

A function used as a callback like `fs.readdir(dir, function (er, entries) { ... })` is not a function declaration.  It is a function expression.

Furthermore, the issue in V8's strict mode where it says "functions can only be declared at top level or immediately within another function", is in fact not compliant with the ES6 specification.  The actual rule is that function declarations inside of blocks in strict mode are local to the block.  Io.js and future versions of Node have newer versions of V8 which observe the ES6 specification in this regard.  Observe: https://gist.github.com/isaacs/ea2b667d7336b64843e1

No, I will not be changing node glob.  It functions properly in strict mode, both in old (incorrect) versions of V8 as well as new (correct) versions of V8.  The error you cite is not applicable in either case, because these are function expressions, not function declarations.

issues.title: Glob fails with ENOTSUP on Windows when working of network drive
issues.body:
Version 3.2.11: 
Using Grunt which still uses this older version -- If this belongs over there, I can certainly move it. Not sure if you all are still maintaining the older versions.

When doing globs with paths that contain `"**"` like:
    `"src/**/*.js"`` 
The error: 
ENOTSUP, operation not supported on socket 'W:\folder\project\src\file.js' 
Happens and the glob fails, when working off some Windows Network Mapped Drives using DFS (I am not sure of the specific underlying network filesystem details).  

See also:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/30199739/enotup-using-grunt

It looks like the error occurs specifically when the globber calls:
readdirsync on a file, and then gets the "unsupported" error back.

When it handles the error in glob.js ~Line 606:

``` javascript
 // now handle errors, and cache the information
if (er) switch (er.code) {
      case "ENOTDIR": // totally normal. means it *does* exist.
        this.statCache[f] = 1
        return cb.call(this, er)
      case "ENOENT": // not terribly unusual
      case "ELOOP":
      case "ENAMETOOLONG":
      case "UNKNOWN":
        this.statCache[f] = false
        return cb.call(this, er)
      default: // some unusual error.  Treat as failure.
        this.statCache[f] = false
        if (this.strict) this.emit("error", er)
        if (!this.silent) console.error("glob error", er)
          return cb.call(this, er)
    }
  }
```

It does not allows for the error "ENOTSUP" only the error "ENOTDIR", adding 
`case "ENOTSUP":`
Right after `case "ENOTDIR":``
Seems to resolve the problem, but it is hard to know if this is the only situation this error would come up.
issues.author: jvoccia

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, I wonder if there are any other cases where `ENOTSUP` might be raised by `readdir` other than "this is a regular file".

One approach might be to stat the file when we get an `ENOTSUP` just to make sure it is a real file that exists?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Meh. Adding stat is a pita.  Just gonna treat ENOTSUP the same as ENOTDIR.  It already was shown on a previous `readdir` call, so it's probably there.

issues.title: Callback or Promise
issues.body:
I saw there was a closed pull request to add a `glob.promise` wrapper using Q.
I would like to propose that you make the callback optional, and in the absence of a callback, return a promise (if Promises are available).

The LESS precompiler applies this signature to their `render` function.
http://lesscss.org/usage/#programmatic-usage
issues.author: TuckerWhitehouse

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I'd rather not.

Most promise libs have easy shorthands for promise-ifying a callback-taking function.  I'd rather let node-glob focus on globbing, and stick to the simplest possible API pattern for asynchrony: a function.

comments.author: TuckerWhitehouse
comments.body: Understandable, thanks for the great library.

issues.title: Patterns with escaped characters are incorrectly handled on Windows
issues.body:
## Problem

The `node-glob` README states (under the [Windows heading](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob#windows)):

> Back-slashes will always be interpreted as escape characters, not path separators

However `minimatch`s implementation includes [the following](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/blob/v2.0.9/minimatch.js#L114-117):

``` javascript
// windows support: need to use /, not \
  if (path.sep !== '/') {
    pattern = pattern.split(path.sep).join('/')
  }
```

On Windows (where `path.sep` is `\`), this code means all escape characters are treated as path separators. Therefore since `minimatch` does not treat `\` as an escape character on Windows, then neither does `node-glob`, despite what its REAMDE says.

The impact is that on Windows platforms, escape characters in glob patterns will not work as intended.

This is an issue for cross-platform projects using this `node-glob` for globbing support.
## Example

Suppose I want to match javascript files whose names start with `[` and end with `]`.

A glob pattern for this, which requires escape characters, might be as follows:
`\[*\].js`

On Linux, `minimatch` converts the above pattern into the following Regex:
`/^(?:(?=.)\[[^/]*?\]\.js)$/`

On Windows, the regex created by `minimatch` is:
`/^(?:\/(?=.)\[(?!\.)(?=.)[^/]*?\/\]\.js)$/`

This latter regex does not match the specified glob pattern.
## Solutions?

Arguably this is a `minimatch` issue, but `minimatch`'s README makes no particular claims about Windows support or handling, whereas `node-glob` does make the above claim.

Possible solution in `node-glob` (leaving `minimatch` unaffected):
- amend the `node-glob` README to state that escaped characters are not supported on Windows.

Possible solution in `minimatch` (leaving `node-glob` unaffected):
- amend the `minimatch` README to require the same thing `node-glob` requires on Windows (ie: 'You must use forward-slashes **only** in glob expressions. Back-slashes will always be interpreted as escape characters, not path separators.')
- remove the `minimatch`code (snippet shown above) that turns `\`s into `/` on Windows.
- --or-- add a `minimatch` option to explicitly specify the character to treat as the path separator.
issues.author: yortus

comments.author: yortus
comments.body: Related: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/issues/64

comments.author: erikkemperman
comments.body:
@isaacs This inconsistency between node-glob and minimatch on Windows is quite annoying... Escaping seems impossible in minimatch currently.

People have [submitted](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/issues/64) [issues](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/issues/78) about it for some time, and there's a [PR](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/pull/103) that seems eminently reasonable. 

Although having said that, I personally would probably prefer a major bump in minimatch adopting the same attitude as node-glob (forward slashes as path separators, backslashes as escape char, on all platforms).

Anyway, this has been going on for a while now. I don't mean to sound impatient, but is there anything that can be done to move this forward? Or do you not agree this is a problem?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: This has been handled in both minimatch and glob now. Patterns must use `/` unless `windowsPathsNoEscape` is set, in which case `\` is only a path separator and not an escape character.

issues.title: `dir/**` matches `dir`
issues.body:
A lone globstar in the last path segment currently matches the parent directory. I'm not sure if this is a bug, but it diverges from `find`:

```
➜  glob-test  nodetree .
.
└── dir1
    └── dir1.1

2 directories, 0 files
➜  glob-test  find dir1/** -maxdepth 0
dir1/dir1.1
➜  glob-test  node -p 'require("glob").sync("dir1/**");'
[ 'dir1', 'dir1/dir1.1' ]
```
issues.author: UltCombo

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
`find(1)` is not the model that this module matches, but rather the globbing pattern-matching behavior in Bash 4.3.

```
$ mkdir -p dir/subdir/subsub
$ for i in dir/**; do echo $i; done
dir/
dir/subdir
dir/subdir/subsub
```

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body:
Makes sense, thanks for the explanation. :smile:
That behavior seems rather counterintuitive imho, but it is compliant to the bash standard after all.

The output is different when I test your sample in bash 3.2 and in zsh, and I also noticed there are discrepancies regarding `dir/**/*`. I wish there was a real standard for globbing.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Bash 3 didn't have globstar support, so of course it would not do anything special with `**`; it'd be the same as `dir/*/*`.  (You can emulate this behavior by setting `{ noglobstar: true }` in the options object.)

I'm not super familiar with zsh, so I can't speak to how that works.

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body:
Thanks for the info.
FWIW, testing on Zsh 5.0.5, `dir/**` seems to match the same as Bash 3, but `dir/**/*` will do deep matching as expected:

```
$ mkdir -p dir/subdir/subsub
$ for i in dir/**; do echo $i; done
dir/subdir
$ for i in dir/**/*; do echo $i; done
dir/subdir
dir/subdir/subsub
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@UltCombo It's not doing globstar matching at all, that's why.  You're getting the same results as `dir/*/*`.

You can observe this if you do `mkdir -p dir/a/b/c/d/e/f/g` and see what it matches.

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body:
@isaacs nope, Zsh seems to have its own glob variation:

```
$ mkdir -p dir/a/b/c/d/e/f/g
$ for i in dir/**; do echo $i; done
dir/a
$ for i in dir/**/*; do echo $i; done
dir/a
dir/a/b
dir/a/b/c
dir/a/b/c/d
dir/a/b/c/d/e
dir/a/b/c/d/e/f
dir/a/b/c/d/e/f/g
```

Also, if `dir/**/*` was treated the same as `dir/*/*`, it would not match `dir/subdir` in the [comment above](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/213#issuecomment-126111904), right?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Wild!  Yeah, that's totally different.  What does `dir/**/` match?

> Also, if `dir/**/*` was treated the same as `dir/*/*`, it would not match `dir/subdir` in the comment above, right?

Ah, good point.

Anyway, zsh's globbing is not the reference implementation that this module targets :)

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body:
Oh yes, that was just a side note that ended up diverging the topic a bit too much.
I'll update my Bash so I can properly test the globbing before reporting issues.
Thanks for the help and explanations. :smile:

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body:
Oh sorry, I missed your question:

> Wild! Yeah, that's totally different. What does `dir/**/` match?

It matches all directories including the parent (`dir`), exactly like node-glob.

comments.author: iulo
comments.body: what about windows ?  seems like `dir/**` also matches the parent directory

comments.author: UltCombo
comments.body: @iulo That's the expected behavior, I believe. Isaac explained it [above](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/213#issuecomment-126047563).

issues.title: Sort order on different versions of node
issues.body:
When using `glob.sync` (version 5.0.14), I am observing different behaviors when using v0.10 and v0.12 on node. When running the following program:

``` JS
var glob = require('glob');
console.log(glob.sync('app/vendor/**/*.css'));
```

On v0.10.33 and v0.10.37 I get the following output:

``` js
[ 'app/vendor/css/MarkerCluster.Default.css',
  'app/vendor/css/MarkerCluster.css',
  'app/vendor/css/bootstrap-datetimepicker.min.css',
  'app/vendor/css/bootstrap.min.css',
  'app/vendor/css/colorPicker.css',
  'app/vendor/css/daterangepicker-bs3.css',
  'app/vendor/css/jquery-ui-1.11.1.custom.css',
  'app/vendor/css/leaflet.css',
  'app/vendor/css/ng-grid.min.css' ]
```

and on v0.12.7 I get:

``` JS
[ 'app/vendor/css/bootstrap-datetimepicker.min.css',
  'app/vendor/css/bootstrap.min.css',
  'app/vendor/css/colorPicker.css',
  'app/vendor/css/daterangepicker-bs3.css',
  'app/vendor/css/jquery-ui-1.11.1.custom.css',
  'app/vendor/css/leaflet.css',
  'app/vendor/css/MarkerCluster.css',
  'app/vendor/css/MarkerCluster.Default.css',
  'app/vendor/css/ng-grid.min.css' ]
```

The difference being the order of the `MarkerCluster*.css` in the array.  Any ideas about why about why the behavior is inconsistent?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Different versions of V8.

In 0.12:

```
$ node -p '"abc".localeCompare("XYZ")'
-1

$ node -v
v0.12.7
```

compare to v0.10 (and io.js)

```
$ node -p '"abc".localeCompare("XYZ")'
9

$ node -v
v0.10.40
```

and io.js:

```
$ node -p '"abc".localeCompare("XYZ")'
9

$ node -v
v2.2.1
```

Looks like there was a bug in 0.12 that then got fixed in later versions of V8?

issues.title: Support multiple patterns
issues.body:
There are two packages that do more or less the same at the moment:
- https://www.npmjs.com/package/glob
- https://www.npmjs.com/package/globby

If `glob` package were to support multiple patterns like `globby`, then there would be no need for the latter and everyone would be a winner.
issues.author: gajus

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
A) How would you like the API to look?  (Note that this may involve some trade-offs that turn out to not be too costly.)

B) How does that differ from just using `{,,,}` to break up your patterns?  Eg, instead of doing `glob([pattern1, pattern2], cb)` you could do `glob('{' + pattern1 + ',' + pattern2 + '}', cb)`.  I guess it's different if the patterns have `,` chars in them?  Is there any other reason why this workaround is objectionable?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Especially: if a file matches more than one pattern, should it be included in the results more than once?  And, do you need to know which pattern a file matched?

comments.author: gajus
comments.body:
First of all, I was not aware that `glob` package does support multiple paths.

Regarding the different between using `{,,,}` and an array of patterns is that:
- you do not need to worry about escaping when providing an array of patterns
- easier to debug

However, I'd like to suggest that @sindresorhus is made a contributor to this library. You could join the forces and I am sure @sindresorhus would be happy to share his insights given that he is maintaining a very popular branch of your package that supports array syntax.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I took a look at globby.  It seems like it just extends node-glob to take an array of patterns, and uses a Promise for the async API rather than a callback.

Unless this feature gets a champion willing to do the work, I'm not super excited to do it myself, especially since globby already exists.  There are some interesting optimizations that could maybe be taken advantage of (like having the multiple different glob instances share cache objects, for example), but no one's complaining about the speed of globby right now.

comments.author: JPeer264
stevenvachon
comments.body:
This would be partially solved by #332.

I'd think that redundant paths should be ignored via memoization.

comments.author: wzup
comments.body:
@isaacs 

> do you need to know which pattern a file matched?

Yes, this would be a good advantage. Is it possible to have a result as an object `pattern: match`?

Suppose a pattern is:
```
'?(index|commons|vendors).*.js'
```

So, currently the match is:
```
["commons.6018ac96ba9a08ad3719.js","index.b0f8a1ea8725f3d65633.js"]
```

A preferred match would be:
```
{
    'index/*.*.js': "index.b0f8a1ea8725f3d65633.js",
    'commons/*.*.js': "commons.6018ac96ba9a08ad3719.js",
}
```

And it would be great if the plugin could generate the latter map from this combined pattern:
```
'?(index|commons|vendors).*.js'
```

Or does this functionality (a match is returned as a map) exist already? I just start testing the plugin and don't know it yet.

comments.author: mgadda
comments.body:
`{${pattern.join(',')}}`; doesn't quite solve the problem. If `pattern.length == 1`, the result is always empty. I would have expected that the pattern `{foo}` would be the same as `foo`. 

`Minimatch.braceExpand` is to blame for this behavior:

```
> mm.braceExpand("{a,b}")
[ 'a', 'b' ]
> mm.braceExpand("{a}")
[ '{a}' ]
```

comments.author: slavafomin
comments.body: It would definitely be very beneficial to have this feature built-in. I've spent some time today trying to figure out how to specify multiple patterns for it and I couldn't find a way to do this, looking at the documentation. At least you should add a section regarding this directly to the README. And if you are not willing to implement it, just mention the `globby` there. It would save a lot of time for end developers.

comments.author: stevenvachon
comments.body: [fast-glob](https://npmjs.com/fast-glob) is what [globby](https://npmjs.com/globby) depends on. They both support multiple patterns.

comments.author: safizn
comments.body:
**Convert pattern JS Array to multiple Glob matching pattern:**
_Multiple patterns are represented in globs as `{pattern1, pattern2, pattern3}`_

```js
const convertToMultiplePatternMatching = array => (array.length > 1 ? array.join(',') |> (multiplePatternString => `{${multiplePatternString}}`) : array[0]) 
```

And then usage: 
```
[ '**/*.js', '**/*.css', ] |> convertToMultiplePatternMatching
// results in {**/*.js,**/*.css}
``` 

Where `|>` is a [pipe operator proposal transformed](https://babeljs.io/blog/2018/07/19/whats-happening-with-the-pipeline-proposal) by Babel
- [Repository](https://github.com/babel/babel/tree/master/packages/babel-plugin-proposal-pipeline-operator)
- [Character sequence ligatures](https://github.com/tonsky/FiraCode)

comments.author: WORMSS
safizn
comments.body:
@WORMSS What you wrote is neater. The purpose may be to use a separate function for wrapping a string and separate the stages of manipulating a target value, e.g. `array.join(',') |> wrap('{','}')`
Mostly I don't give these styles a lot of weight, I use whatever seemed to be comfortable while writing the code.

comments.author: WORMSS
comments.body: I was more interested in what `|>` was.. I have since managed to find it. It's an experimental pipe feature that doesn't seem to be implemented in node/typescript/chrome so I had no reference to what it was or how to run it. I will atleast know this for the future.

comments.author: Diokuz
comments.body:
So, `glob` does not respect polymorphism, and that is official?)

PS: Have the same problem. Had a big issue with single array item. Now I have to write several lines of code to handle this problem. Probably will move to globby.

comments.body: Implemented on v9

issues.title: Can't get !(pattern) to work
issues.body:
I'm trying to do this for my grunt clean script:

`"{**/node_modules/!(grunt*)/,**/bower_components}"`

So I delete every node and bower module except grunt and its contrib.

Blob is returning (among many others) `node_modules/grunt/node_modules/hooker/`

I'm clearly misunderstanding how the operator work...
issues.author: sieira

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Let's simplify this a bit by looking only at the first braced group section.  Clearly it's not the second part of the pattern that's matching, since it doesn't end in `bower_components`.

`**/node_modules/!(grunt*)` will match `node_modules/grunt/node_modules/foo` because the `**` matches `node_modules/grunt/node_modules` and then `foo` is not `grunt*`, so there you go.  The pattern will match any thing containing `node_modules` that doesn't _end in_ `grunt*`, so this will match most of grunt's deps.

I think what you probably want is to drop the `**`, and just use this: `node_modules/!(grunt*)`, or combined with the `bower_components`, `{node_modules/!(grunt*),**/bower_components}`.

comments.author: sieira
comments.body:
Ok, this was it:

`{node_modules/!(grunt*),!(node_modules)/**/node_modules/,**/bower_components}`

it can surely be improved, because I only need the higher level node_modules on each sub directory,and only keep grunt modules on the highest, but I will leave a TODO and think about it another day.

I know this was not the best place to ask this kind of question. Thank you for your kind answer.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I'm going to lock this thread just to make sure this is the final word on this issue.

When I get around to it, I'm going to refactor node-glob to be a hybrid module written in TypeScript, with a promise based API.  I've been doing that for all my node packages. In the last year, I've updated node-lru-cache, minimatch, and a bunch of others. This module needs quite a bit of TLC, and its number hasn't come up yet.  But I am getting around to it.  First I had to update minimatch, and I've been busy with some next-gen node-tap work.  But all of this is only fitting into a tiny sliver of my available time.

I don't get paid to work on OSS (other than the Tier node sdk, of course).  I have a kid, and a life, and a job.  This is a hobby, one that I wish I could devote more time to than I do, but still a hobby.  I don't stress over it.  I'll get to it when I get to it.

If you want to use another glob implementation in the meantime, ok, cool, go for it.  The benefit of this one (or maybe the drawback, depending on who you ask) is that it matches the Bash pattern matching behavior about as closely as it is possible to in JavaScript. But there are others that get more attention, have been updated more recently, etc. Taking shortcuts on correctness can sometimes result in greater speed, and maybe that's a benefit you care about more than matching shell behavior as closely as possible.  More power to you.

If you'd like to get me to prioritize this issue ahead of other things going on in my life, and treat it like my job, then you can do that with money, because like most of us, I am indentured to the capitalist machine in order to live and provide for my family.  My time is not cheap, but if you are in a hurry, that's the way to get me moving sooner.  https://izs.me/hire or https://github.com/sponsors/isaacs or you can email me at <i@izs.me> and we can work something out.

issues.title: Invalid negate matching
issues.body:
``` js
glob("./**/*!(.dyn).css", function(err, res) { console.log(err, res) });
```

returns files with `.dyn.css` extension.
issues.author: dangreen

comments.author: thirdcreed
comments.body:
I think that negation has been fully deprecated.  Use the ignore option instead.
UPDATE:
I'm wrong. Read on.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@thirdcreed Only the "negate the whole pattern by prefixing with a !" has been deprecated.  This kind of negation should still work.

@dangreen what does your file tree look like?  What gets returned?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
As far as I can tell, this is behaving exactly like bash 4.3

```
[gh:node-glob/master]isaacs@urdva:~/dev/js/glob/a node@v4.0.0
$ tree
.
├── b/
│   ├── c/
│   │   ├── bar.dyn.css
│   │   └── foo.css
│   ├── bar.dyn.css
│   └── foo.css
├── bar.dyn.css
└── foo.css

2 directories, 6 files

[gh:node-glob/master]isaacs@urdva:~/dev/js/glob/a node@v4.0.0
$ for i in ./**/*!(.dyn).css ; do echo $i; done
./b/bar.dyn.css
./b/c/bar.dyn.css
./b/c/foo.css
./b/foo.css
./bar.dyn.css
./foo.css

[gh:node-glob/master]isaacs@urdva:~/dev/js/glob/a node@v4.0.0
$ node -p 'require("../").sync("./**/*!(.dyn).css")'
[ './b/bar.dyn.css',
  './b/c/bar.dyn.css',
  './b/c/foo.css',
  './b/foo.css',
  './bar.dyn.css',
  './foo.css' ]
```

What's happening here is that if you have a file like `foo.dyn.css`, then the `*` matches `fo`, and then `o.dyn` is not equal to `.dyn`, and then the `.css` matches `.css`.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
If you do use an `ignore` pattern like @thirdcreed suggests, then it works as you probably want:

```
$ node -p 'require("../").sync("./**/*.css",{ignore:"./**/*.dyn.css"})'
[ './b/c/foo.css', './b/foo.css', './foo.css' ]
```

issues.author: Wandalen

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: If the file system is case insensitive, then this module will be case insensitive. There's not much to be done about that, since all the fs functions will play along with the "wrong" case.

comments.author: Wandalen
comments.body:
File system on OSX is case sensitive by default. Ok it is case preserving.
Still it decreases area of application if function cant act predictable on most platforms.

Anyway thank you for your contribution into open source. It's great deal.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
It is only case preserving in some cases.  In this case, node-glob and bash do the same thing:

```
$ mkdir -p A/B/C
$ echo a/**
a/ a/B a/B/C
$ node -p 'require("./").sync("a/**")'
[ 'a', 'a/B', 'a/B/C' ]
```

However, there IS a discrepancy here:

```
$ echo readme.*
README.md
$ node -p 'require("./").sync("readme.*")'
[]
```

Gonna open a new bug for this.  Thanks for helping to find it :)

issues.title: case sensitivity
issues.body:
This is weird.

https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/237#issuecomment-162802643

This is as expected, on case-insensitive file systems:

```
$ mkdir -p A/B/C
$ echo a/**
a/ a/B a/B/C
$ node -p 'require("./").sync("a/**")'
[ 'a', 'a/B', 'a/B/C' ]
```

However this is wrong.

```
$ echo readme.*
README.md
$ node -p 'require("./").sync("readme.*")'
[]
```

That second case is the same behavior in Bash 4.3 on both case-sensitive and non-case-sensitive file systems, and is a bug in this library.

Glob path parts should always be case-insensitive if they contain magic characters, even on Linux, but should always return the path part value that `readdir` returned (thus being case-insensitive and case-preserving).

```
# linux
$ echo a/**
a/**
$ echo A/**
A/ A/B A/B/C
$ echo a*/**
A A/B A/B/C
```
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Also, maybe related, but maybe a separate issue, note the difference:

```
$ echo A/**
A/ A/B A/B/C

$ echo A*/**
A A/B A/B/C

$ node -p 'require("./").sync("A/**")'
[ 'A', 'A/B', 'A/B/C' ]

$ node -p 'require("./").sync("A*/**")'
[ 'A', 'A/B', 'A/B/C' ]
```

The `A*/**` doesn't preserve the `/` on `A/`, but `A/**` does preserve the `/`.  Technically they're both correct results, but the difference is a minor bug.

issues.title: files without permission entries are not found on Windows
issues.body:
Steps to reproduce:
1. create a file `deleteMe.txt`
2. `glob.sync('deleteMe.txt')` returns `[ 'deleteMe.txt' ]`
3. remove all permission entries (Windows Explorer)
4. `glob.sync('deleteMe.txt')` returns `[ ]` w/o error

(same with async…)
issues.author: schnittstabil

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: What do you get when you call `fs.statSync('deleteMe.txt')`?  Or run the glob with `glob.sync('deleteMe.txt', { stat: true })`?

comments.author: schnittstabil
comments.body:
1. `fs.statSync('deleteMe.txt')`
   
   ```
   Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, stat 'C:\Dokumente und Einstellungen\Administrator\Eigene Dateien\deleteMe.txt'
   at Error (native)
   at Object.fs.statSync (fs.js:892:18)
   at Object.<anonymous> (C:\Dokumente und Einstellungen\Administrator\Eigene Dateien\test.js:6:31)
   at Module._compile (module.js:413:34)
   at Object.Module._extensions..js (module.js:422:10)
   at Module.load (module.js:357:32)
   at Function.Module._load (module.js:314:12)
   at Function.Module.runMain (module.js:447:10)
   at startup (node.js:139:18)
   at node.js:999:3
   ```
2. `glob.sync('deleteMe.txt', { stat: true })` returns `[]` w/o error
3. But `fs.readdirSync('.')` returns `['deleteMe.txt', …]`

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Hm.  Ok, that seems kinda like a bug to me.  It should be handling `EPERM` from readdir calls as an indication that the thing is either not a directory or not traversable, but an `EPERM` on a file seems like it should be an error.

I'll have to review the code to be sure there isn't some method to this madness, but I agree that it seems like an oversight somewhere, and I'll try to figure out what bash does.  On Unix, there's no combination of permissions that will prevent you from being able to stat a file, so this is probably just a windows-ism that I didn't think to account for.

comments.author: isaacs
schnittstabil
schnittstabil
comments.body:
> I don't know why it's warning in async and throwing in sync, though, that's strange. 

Found it:
[sync.js#L336-L342](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/f65f9eb7eda113528c5257b58fac4ca685ee6c4f/sync.js#L336-L342):

``` js
    default: // some unusual error.  Treat as failure.
      this.cache[this._makeAbs(f)] = false
      if (this.strict)
        throw er
      if (!this.silent)
        console.error('glob error', er)
      break
```

In [glob.js#L609-L619](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/cea11fea473f18c565a515b0c61c8f194fc23aaa/glob.js#L609-L619):

``` js
    default: // some unusual error.  Treat as failure.
      this.cache[this._makeAbs(f)] = false
      if (this.strict) {
        this.emit('error', er)
        // If the error is handled, then we abort
        // if not, we threw out of here
        this.abort()
      }
      if (!this.silent)
        console.error('glob error', er)
      break
```

issues.title: ignoring absolute paths is platform dependent
issues.body:
Having read https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/189#issuecomment-94215438, I'm not sure how `ignore` should work and what behavior to expect, but I think it should work platform independently.
### Test script

``` js
glob.sync('/build/**', {
    ignore: ['/build', '/build/goat.png']
})
```
### Linux

Files:

``` sh
/build/goat.png
/build/unicorn.png
```

Output:

``` sh
[ '/build/unicorn.png' ]
```
### Windows

Files:

``` sh
C:\build\goat.png
C:\build\unicorn.png
```

Output:

``` sh
[ 'C:/build', 'C:/build/goat.png', 'C:/build/unicorn.png' ]

# with `realpath: true`
[ 'C:\\build', 'C:\\build\\goat.png', 'C:\\build\\unicorn.png' ]

# with `ignore: ['C:/build', 'C:/build/goat.png']`
[ 'C:/build/unicorn.png' ]
```

(same with async…)
Maybe related: #189
issues.author: schnittstabil

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Hm. Yeah, that seems like a bug for sure.  `/build/goat.png` would match `c:/build/goat.png` on win32 systems, so that ignore pattern should prevent `c:/build/goat.png` from being returned in the results.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: No longer an issue on v9

issues.title: Options starting with `no-` are forced into boolean false
issues.body:
If I run with

`--ano-instrument-glob **/vendor/** --ano-instrument-glob shared/**`

in the arguments I get

`'ano-instrument-glob': [ '**/vendor/**', 'shared/**' ] }`

from node-glob, but when I run with 

`--no-instrument-glob **/vendor/** --no-instrument-glob shared/**`

in the arguments I get

`'instrument-glob': false`

from node-glob.

I am not sure if this is intended behavior but I think it should be more clearly documented if so, or made optionable.

I sincerely apologize if this is already documented somewhere, and moreso if it already has an option to toggle the behavior off, but I could not find it.
issues.author: r4j4h

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Sorry, I don't know what `--no-instrument-glob` means.

What program are you running?  Does it use this module?

comments.author: r4j4h
comments.body:
Ah mess, I meant to post this under a different project! :x This has nothing to do with node-glob, I accidentally posted here because the example parameter has glob in it. :3

To answer your question, it's an arbitrary parameter that's poorly named. A better example would have been --no-colors to indicate not inserting terminal color characters. Instead of no-colors true, it comes as colors: false. Which is fine, but just unexpected. I believe I meant to post this to substack's minimist.

Sorry for the bother!

comments.author: r4j4h
comments.body:
Well, I was going to post and update this with the link but it looks like it's already been discussed as being undocumented but hasn't been merged yet https://github.com/substack/minimist/pull/60 and https://github.com/substack/minimist/issues/54 .

Again, sorry to bother!

issues.title: Inflight complicates runtime, can it be made optional?
issues.body:
The behavior of "inflight" module seems to complicate greatly execution flow with glob, especially when callback could throw error.

What is intended use for it? Can it be made optional? After I disabled "inflight" usage in my private glob.js - all my functional tests are still passing. Maybe I'm missing some race condition? What dragons should I expect if I use version without wrapping callbacks with "inflight"?

Thanks!
# 

The reason why I'm asking:

It caught my attention when stepping through really weird behavior of async mocha tests, which were throwing assertion errors. Glob seems to "leak" inflight callback from previous test and that callback interferes later with other tests. I'm still trying to grasp the details of what exactly happens. Once I remove "inflight" usage from glob.js - behavior is simple and expected.

Aside from test issue - I'm concerned how glob would operate in production environment if something would manage to throw something in the chain of callback. Would Glob keep "inflight" callback for some keys when callback throws? Would that impact runtime going forward if callbacks were leaked from previous Glob usages?
issues.author: alexk-z

comments.author: alexk-z
comments.body:
All Glob tests are passing as well on Ubuntu after 'inflight' use is removed.

Should I prepare pull request with options parameter, which makes 'inflight' optional? Will it be accepted?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Here's the problem: without using inflight, this module can end up doing a **lot** more syscalls.

Do you have a reduced test case that shows how glob leaks callbacks when a callback throws?

comments.author: alexk-z
alexk-z
comments.body: What is the verdict? Should I create PR for providing option to avoid using 'inflight'?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@phated Ah, this is the glob bug you were talking about!  Thanks.

Yes, this should be fixed with the latest `inflight` release.

issues.title: Is there any AND logical operator?
issues.body:
I am using a library that uses glob for pattern matching, but does not provide any ignore option. I need to exclude `*.exclude.js` from `*.js`. Is this possible?

As far as I understand glob supports only the `OR` and `NOT` logical operators, while this would require something like `AND("*.js", NOT("*.exclude.js"))`.
issues.author: ghost

comments.author: ghost
comments.body: Hmm `AND(a, NOT(b))` can be written as `NOT(OR(NOT(a), b))`. I tried with `some/folder/**/!(!(*.js)|(*.exclude.js))`, but this does not work, the result is empty. :-(

comments.author: ghost
comments.body: I was able to do this in a simpler way: `some/folder/**/!(*.exclude).js`

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
``` javascript
glob('**/*.js', { exclude: '**/*.exclude.js' }, cb)
```

comments.author: ghost
comments.body:
@isaacs  

> I am using a library that uses glob for pattern matching, but does not provide any ignore option.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Oh, sorry, I skimmed past that, I guess. Seems like it'd be good if that library let you pass options to glob somehow, then.

issues.title: Glob returns different files if ran from npm script
issues.body:
Heya,

I'm getting different results from globs when I run them directly through the CLI than I get when I run the same CLI command via a npm script.

I created a very simple repo to exemplify the CLI behaviour: https://github.com/filipesilva/glob-cli-test. It just runs `console.log(glob.sync(process.argv[2]));` and has a `glob-cli-test` binary.

Here is the issue in practice:

```
filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ ls -la
total 13
drwxr-xr-x 1 filipe.silva filipe.silva   0 Apr  6 14:50 ./
drwxr-xr-x 1 filipe.silva filipe.silva   0 Apr  6 14:21 ../
-rw-r--r-- 1 filipe.silva filipe.silva   0 Apr  6 14:06 file.ts
drwxr-xr-x 1 filipe.silva filipe.silva   0 Apr  6 14:46 node_modules/
-rw-r--r-- 1 filipe.silva filipe.silva 162 Apr  6 14:42 package.json

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ cat package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "glob": "glob-cli-test '**/*.ts'"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "glob-cli-test": "git+https://github.com/filipesilva/glob-cli-test.git"
  }
}

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ cat file.ts

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ ./node_modules/.bin/glob-cli-test '**/*.ts'
[ 'file.ts' ]

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ npm run glob

> @ glob E:\sandbox\glob-test
> glob-cli-test '**/*.ts'

[]

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$
```
issues.author: filipesilva

comments.author: filipesilva
comments.body:
I forgot to mention: I am running Windows 10, using gitbash. 

As far as I can tell, this doesn't happen in OSX/Linux.

comments.author: filipesilva
comments.body:
As per https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/5796#issuecomment-206717297, the issue is completely unrelated to `glob` but rather a quirk of `cmd`, and npm scripts use `cmd`.

For npm scripts to correctly pass arguments to node binaries, double quotes (`"`) must be used instead of single quotes (`'`).

The above example works perfectly well with double quotes:

```
filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ ./node_modules/.bin/glob-cli-test "**/*.ts"
[ 'file.ts' ]

filipe.silva@DESKTOP-7ND6T3R MINGW64 /E/sandbox/glob-test
$ npm run glob

> @ glob E:\sandbox\glob-test
> glob-cli-test "**/*.ts"

[ 'file.ts' ]
```

issues.title: Tests are broken on various Linux platforms on master / v6 rc
issues.body: will be digging into this further today, but wanted to give you a heads up
issues.author: MylesBorins

comments.author: MylesBorins
comments.body:
It looks like the breakage has been caused by https://github.com/nodejs/node/commit/b488b19eaf2b2e7a3ca5eccd2445e245847a5f76

Node is now relying on a native implementation of realpath that is provided by libuv

One of the changes is the removal of the cache, which is used by glob -- >https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/master/glob.js#L235

This does not seem to be causing the problem, as running tests with the cache removed is still throwing the same errors. @jasnell is digging into libuv to see if it is an implementation bug

comments.author: jasnell
comments.body:
Turns out to be fairly straight forward.. fs.realpath and fs.realpathSync now defer to libuv for the implementation which now throw errors that the previous implementation did not. Specifically, when the sym link depth reaches a certain point, both fs.readdir and fs.realpath both start complaining. While glob swallows the readdir throw, it doesn't swallow the realpath throw, causing the problem.

Now, we aren't seeing this happen on OSX or other platforms because on _those_ platforms, _readdir_ complains about the symlink depth _earlier_ than realpath, and since glob swallows that error and uses it as an exit criteria (by returning null for the readdir check), the code never gets to the depth that causes realpath to throw. 

The fix is fairly simple. In sync.js and glob.js, in the error handling for fs.realpath, check for `er.code === 'ELOOP'` and `set[real] = true`.

There is also a secondary issue with glob's use of `fs.realpath`. In the new implementation, the `cache` argument has been removed and was replaced with an `options` argument. This `options` argument does _not_ have the same semantics as `cache`. It allows for a single `encoding` parameter that specifies the character encoding of the returned path. Right now the cache object passed in by glob is ignored completely, but if that object happened to have an `encoding` parameter on it, it wouldn't be good. It's probably ok to continue passing the cache argument along (it would be treated as if it were an options object in v6) for backwards compatibility. But it's something to keep an eye on.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed by falling back to the old implementation of fs.realpath on Node 6 when new errors are thrown from the `realpath` syscall.  Using http://npm.im/fs.realpath module.

comments.author: MylesBorins
comments.body:
thanks for taking the time to find a userland solution that works for now, I'll remove node-glob from the flaky list on citgm

I will also be following up internally to help figure out a way to fix this in node proper

issues.title: statCache not consistently populated
issues.body:
I created a repository to help illustrate this issue. I would have preferred using something like JSBin but not sure how I would use it to describe this issue. Regardless, the repo is here: https://github.com/Macrofig/glob-test

If I have a pattern, say `/test/**/*.*`, Glob finds and populates the `cache` and `statCache` objects properly.  But if I have a pattern like `/test/**/*.{js,stache}`, some items are not populated in the file. In my case, it seems to be the `stache` files but I am not certain that is the case.

We were previously using version 5.0.3 of Glob and this pattern wasn't an issue. We are updating to version 7.0.4 and experiencing this bug. Specifically, through Karma. We were also updating Karma from 0.12.31 which was using Glob 3.x. The current version of Karma uses 7.x.

Doing some research, it seems like [this PR](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/pull/159) introduced this issue but I'm not entirely sure I understand the use case for #158 so I can't be certain what the fix is. I'd love to help with a PR but I would need some guidance.

Thoughts, ideas? Is it maybe my pattern that is incorrect? Thanks!

comments.author: feross
comments.body:
**UPDATE:** Nevemind. Ignore this comment. The correct pattern for the user to use is `**/node_modules/**` if they want nested `node_modules` folders to be ignore.

---

I had a user report the same issue on `standard`: https://github.com/feross/standard/issues/746

They have an absolutely insane number of `node_modules/` folders. We're calling:

```js
glob('**/*.js', {
        ignore: [
          '**/*.min.js',
          '**/bundle.js',
          'coverage/**',
          'node_modules/**',
          'vendor/**'
        ],
        nodir: true
      }, callback)
```

And it seems like every single `*.js` file is discovered first (and this user has a TON of them, including tons in `node_modules/`). I see `Glob.prototype._process` is called for tons of files with paths like `cerebral/debugger/function-tree-debugger/node_modules/sanitize-filename`.

Finally, the process crashes before the ignoring/filtering can happen.

Is there any way we could make ignoring `node_modules/` happen sooner in the process?

comments.author: isaacs

issues.title: Glob pattern not acting as expected
issues.body:
Hi there,

From `node-glob` [documentation](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/master/README.md):

> `!(pattern|pattern|pattern)``  Matches anything that does not match any of the patterns provided.

 I understand that `'**/*.!(css|js)'` shall list everything except `*.css` and `*.js`.

However on my tests it does not:

```
$ glob-all '**/*.!(css|js)' | sort >wglob
$ find ./ -type f | egrep --color=no -v "\.(css|js)" | sort | sed -e 's#^\./##' >wfind
```

`sed` was used to remove `./` from the start of the lines in `wfind` since find returns the path in relation to local directory (`./`). Then, a simple diff of results:

```
$ diff wfind wglob
20a21,24
> assets/css/jquery.fancybox.css
> assets/css/jquery.fancybox-thumbs.css
> assets/css/style-27vrduj1jopliavvnzi7ibndpmbvg7lzo4a2uswrsi4m5vwauf2qi8s1v3or.min.css
> assets/css/style-nuvue6sithwirecbhvw3dkaobiojqvtadsnhguwi7k04xklybw5djl1smadp.min.css
35a40,43
> assets/js/jquery.fancybox.js
> assets/js/jquery.fancybox-thumbs.js
> assets/js/script-i4qo6jx6jji9fg0dftpya6ivemizsbow4fhow76d8dwpm7m1wbvi378ssumx.min.js
> assets/js/scrip-tu4ziscb5jrkjxubeugdgizktyuugflfq5jvgz4agzzmwifuq01g1lmq8giw.min.js
```

Only `*.js` and `*.css` files are added to file `wglob` in relation to `wfind`.

Am I missing something?

Notice: I used `node-glob-all` in _cli_ mode for this test but I also used `grunt-aws-s3` task which makes node glob as shown in [this question at Stack Overflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/38647452/gruntjs-globbing-correct-way-to-match-some-files-extensions-but-not-others).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Ah, you've got a filename with multiple parts.  I missed that!  So yes, you'll have to use that more complicated pattern, then.

What `*.!(js|css)` says is "some characters, followed by a dot, followed by something that isn't `js` or `css`, followed by the end of the string".  So, if you have `multi.part.js` then you have `multi` (some characters) followed by `.`, followed by `part.js` (which isn't `js` or `css`).

What `*.!(*(*.)js|*(*.)css)` says is "some characters, followed by a dot, followed by something that is not zero or more instances of (character followed by dot) and then `js` or zero or more (characters followed by a dot) and then `css`".

If you were comfortable getting file entries that don't have dots in them (which will mostly be folders, but you can avoid by using the `nodir` option passed to glob) you can use the somewhat simpler pattern `**/!(*.css|*.js)`, which says "something that isn't `*.js` or `*.css`.

This matches bash's pattern matching logic:

```
$ for i in **/*.!(css|js); do echo $i; done
css/multi.part.css
css/multi.part.js
css/multi.part.txt
css/one.txt
css/two.txt
js/multi.part.css
js/multi.part.js
js/multi.part.txt
js/one.txt
js/two.txt

$ for i in **/*.!(*(*.)css|*(*.)js); do echo $i; done
css/multi.part.txt
css/one.txt
css/two.txt
js/multi.part.txt
js/one.txt
js/two.txt

$ for i in **/!(*.css|*.js); do echo $i; done
css
css/multi.part.txt
css/one.txt
css/two.txt
js
js/multi.part.txt
js/one.txt
js/two.txt
```

issues.title: mismatching issue with when path contains '[***]
issues.body:
Hi, I encounter some issue with matching using glob : 

I'm a currently using this pattern to find my mp3 file "path.join(currentdirPath,"/*.mp3") where currentdir change thanks to a parameters.

I noticed when currentdirpath contains "[somecharacter]" like in this exemple : 
Senki Zesshou Symphogear Original Soundtrack 1 [mp3], glob found nothing.
but in this exemple :  Senki Zesshou Symphogear Character Song 1 - Zwei Wing

glob found all the mp3 files

i think that's beacause [...] is a pattern, but how can I escape [ and ]
issues.author: qvandekadsye

comments.author: hhamilto
comments.body:
> how can I escape [ and ]

How about with back slashes like this:

```
currentdirPath.replace(/[[\]]/g,'\\$&')
```

That replaces `[` with `\[` and `]` with `\]`, which causes them to be treated as literal opening and closing bracket characters.

I imagine that there are other edge cases that should be taken care of when your path might contain special characters, but I'm not sure about how to handle all cases off the top of my head.

comments.author: hhamilto
comments.body:
I was looking through the documentation just now and found a better solution to your problem, rather than escaping `currentdirPath`. 

You should pass in the `cwd` property as `currentdirPath` on the options object when calling glob. eg:

```
glob('*.mp3', {cwd: currentdirPath})
```

comments.author: sinedied
comments.body: @hhamilto I have a similar issue, except that the [ and ] character are part of the filename. I tried escaping with a backslash using your regexp, but it still doesn't work. Any ideas?

comments.author: woojoo666
comments.body:
confirmed as of today (version 7.1.3) that escaping square brackets (eg `\\[`) does not work. I have a suspicion it's because it supports windows-style paths, eg this is a path that glob supports: `C:\\this\\is\\a\\windows\\path`. However, if you try to escape brackets, eg `C:\\foo \\[bar\\]`, then how does it differentiate between interpretting it as a path separator (equivalent to "C:/foo/[bar/]") or as an escape character (equivalent to "C:/foo [bar]")?

Also, as @sinedied noted, using the `currentdirPath` solution doesn't work if you are trying to match the square bracket character in the file name.

comments.author: woojoo666
comments.body:
actually, as I was typing the previous comment, I came up with a devious hack: just wrap all square brackets with `[...]`, so it gets treated as a character in the match pattern.

Eg if you want to match the path `C:/foo [bar]` use the match pattern `C:/foo [[]bar[]]`

Ugly, but works, even as far back as package version 7.1.1 (that's as far back as I checked). This should be put in the ReadMe though because it's the only solution I've found so far.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: The pattern `c:/foo \[bar\]` should work fine now in v9.

issues.title: Slashes don't work inside logic operators
issues.body:
For instance, querying the following

```
glob.sync("!(a/b)", {})
```

will throw this error:

```
node_modules/glob/sync.js:147
  var dotOk = this.dot || rawGlob.charAt(0) === '.'
                                 ^

TypeError: Cannot read property 'charAt' of undefined
```
issues.author: ntrrgc

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
It is correct that slashes do not work inside of extglob patterns such as `!(a|b)`.  Those can only be used to indicate path parts, not entire paths.  This is how Bash works as well.  In that case, the pattern is read as a file named `b)` inside of a folder named `!(a`, which is weird, but not impossible.

That being said, you _have_ found a bug here, because it should not throw if you use a pattern like that.

issues.title: Add support to pass in fs interface
issues.body: I enjoy node-glob, but I need its use on a virtual file system which is separate from the node fs, but with the same interface. If we modify fs.realpath and add in a fs parameter in the options which falls back to the default native fs t should be quite easy
issues.author: ghost

comments.author: erikkemperman
comments.body:
That would be exactly what I was looking for! Currently [`vinyl-fs`](https://github.com/gulpjs/vinyl-fs) is being updated to split off a module for use by other vinyl adapters, [`vinyl-prepare`](https://github.com/gulpjs/vinyl-prepare). With any luck it'll be in Gulp 4.

It would be great if implementers of custom vinyl adapters, such as purely in-memory ones, would not have to do their own globbing (or disallow glob patterns completely) -- but instead just provide the needed subset of `fs` calls as an option.

At this time, our call to glob can't be transplanted to `vinyl-prepare` because of its file system access, but I think it should be.

@isaacs I'd be willing to spend some time on a PR, unless of course you don't like the idea to begin with?

issues.title: Discussion: emitting absolute paths
issues.body:
In `glob-stream`, we do processing on each glob to resolve it to an absolute path before passing it to a globber.  This allows us to ensure we always have absolute paths emitted by this module.  However, I noticed that absolute paths are already being calculated (generated?) at https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/master/glob.js#L471

How would you feel about an added option, like `emitAbsolute`, to always emit the absolute path instead of the relative one?
issues.author: phated

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I would accept a PR to add something like this, yes.  Maybe just `absolute: true` as an option.

comments.author: phated
comments.body: Excellent.  I'll dig into it

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I think you could basically just `this.emit('match', this._absolute ? abs : e)` in that function, and set `this._absolute = !!options.absolute` in the ctor.  Then tests and docs, and you're done :)

issues.title: Is it possible to specify a maximum depth?
issues.body:
For example if i have;

```
\some\path\
\another\directory\
\some\longer\path\
\an\even\longer\path\
```

Is there a way to specify a maximum depth that the tree will be traversed, such that executing glob against the above would only return the first two paths, being 2 deep?
issues.author: staff0rd

comments.author: bluelovers
comments.body: need this

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
For two, the easiest way would be to use a pattern like this: `glob(['*', '*/*'])` and it'll only go two directories deep.  Obviously this isn't so great when you want to set the limit to something like 100 folders deep.

Currently, there's no way to set a maximum depth for patterns like `**` though, no.  It would be a bit tricky to do, but not entirely impossible.  I'd approach it by first adding some concept of a "depth" field in PathScurry, which might actually have other benefits anyway.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Now that PathScurry tracks the depth, I'm thinking it might be possible to do something like:

```
glob(pattern, { maxDepth: <some number> })
```

which would only go as deep as the depth of the current working directory, plus the number specified.

Would it be worthwhile to set a _minimum_ depth as well?  For example, to prevent absolute patterns or anything starting with `..`?

This definitely has a few edge cases, because we translate `../**/x/x` into the combined patterns `../x/x` and `**/x/x`, since it's equivalent and shaves a factor off of the combinatorial explosion.  So if you set `minDepth: 0`, then it would prevent the first part, but not the second.  But a pattern like `../x/y/z`, run in `x`, would not return anything, because it'd refuse to walk the parent directory.  So that's a little weird.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Also, would minDepth prevent the _walk_ or just the _match_? 🤔 

Like, in this case: `glob('/a/b/c/d/**', { cwd: '/w/x/y/z', minDepth: 4 })`

Would that match all the files in `/a/b/c/d`, because they're all above the depth of 4, or would it _not_ do that, because to do that it has to walk to `/a` and that's depth 1?

And if it _wouldn't_ match anything, what about this?  `glob('/a/b/c/d/**', { cwd: '/a/b/c/d', minDepth: 4 })`

I think it's probably simpler to just say it limits maximum depth only, and prevents both the walk and the match.  So:

```js
glob('../**', { cwd: '/a/b/c', maxDepth:2 })
```

would find `/a/b` (at relative depth -1), and `/a/b/c/d` (relative depth 1) and would match `/a/b/c/d/e` but not walk over its children.

comments.body:
I have a spike of this in progress, but I want to have a `--cmd -c <exec command>` option that would get all the expanded globs as arguments (if no command set, then it just writes them all to stdout).

To do that, I wanted to use `foreground-child`, and that uses `signal-exit`.  Both of which are super dated and not hybrid TS, so they needed to be rewritten first. Which I would have had to get to anyway as part of the tap 18 project, so it's all good, but they're blockers for this as well.

Should get to this pretty soon.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I think the main caveat for usage will be that you _really_ want to quote the arguments if you're using this cross platform.

Imagine you have a file like `app/routes/foo/[id].ts`, and you do `glob app/routes/**/*.ts`.  On posix shells, it will expand the globs in the command line before calling `glob`, so it'll run `glob app/routes/[id].ts app/routes/foo/[bar].ts`, try to expand those `[]` expressions, and not find any matches.

One possible option would be to bring back the `nullglob` option.  Right now, that's not supported, and the use cases for it for anything _other than_ shell arguments are pretty minimal.  But then, if `app/routes/[id].ts` doesn't match anything, it'll use that exact string.  The hazard, of course, is that if you _do_ have something that matches, like `app/routes/i.ts` or `app/routes/d.ts`, then it'll still do the wrong thing.

Yet another approach would be a sort of "prefer exact match" option.  So anything it gets as an argument that _is_ an existing path on the filesystem, it'll just use that and not expand it.  That might be the ideal default, in fact, in which case there ought to be a `--no-prefer-exact-match` option to say "No, really, I know that's a real thing, but I actually want you to try to expand _all_ of these as glob patterns no matter what".

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: "Prefer exact match" is a bit tricky, because eg `"*"` is a valid filename on posix systems, which is also matched by the `*` pattern.  So you could probably footgun yourself if you did `touch '**'` in your project.  But maybe that's a weird enough thing to do that we can just document it as a caveat and be done with it.

comments.author: json2d
comments.body:
Standard regex patterns are not supported currently, so the quantifiers you're using (e.g. `n+`, `n*`, `n{3,5}`) will not work as you expected.

However, there is support for the range pattern `[...]`, which works similar to the one in regex.

To match your numeric file extensions, you can use: `*.[0-9][0-9][0-9]`

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:

Globs are not regular expressions.  Type `man bash` in your terminal, and look for the section on Pattern Matching.

issues.title: False match for inacessible directories
issues.body:
It seems glob gives false match if directory is inaccessible. 

If there is a directory in ./test/ that is not accessible (i. e. insufficent privileges), it will be returned as match, even though it doesn't contain matching file. 

For example, if test directory contains:
/test$ ls -la
total 0
drwxrwxr-x. 4 user user 42 Mar 28 10:20 .
drwxrwxr-x. 4 user user 69 Mar 28 10:18 ..
drw-r--r--. 2 root  root  6 Mar 28 10:20 inaccessibledir (empty directory)
drwxrwxr-x. 2 user user 28 Mar 28 10:19 somedir (contains matching file)

`glob.sync("./test/*/some_result.log");` will return following result:

'./test/inaccessibledir/some_result.log',
'./test/somedir/some_result.log'

If we change inaccessibledir permissions with chmod +x, results become correct.

This behavior is the same if we use { nodir: true } option.

issues.title: Switch to faster micromatch lib
issues.body:
> ## Why switch to micromatch?
> 
> * Native support for multiple glob patterns, no need for wrappers like [multimatch](https://github.com/sindresorhus/multimatch)
> * [10-55x faster](#benchmarks) and more performant than [minimatch](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch) and [multimatch](https://github.com/sindresorhus/multimatch). This is achieved through a combination of caching and regex optimization strategies, a fundamentally different approach than minimatch.
> * More extensive support for the Bash 4.3 specification
> * More complete extglob support
> * Extensive [unit tests](./test) (approx. 1,300 tests). Minimatch fails many of the tests.

See: https://github.com/jonschlinkert/micromatch#why-switch-to-micromatch
issues.author: AndyOGo

comments.author: curbengh
comments.body: Check out https://github.com/micromatch/glob-fs and https://github.com/micromatch/bash-glob, both created by micromatch's author.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
No.

There are several cases where micromatch does not implement bash semantics as faithfully as this library requires.  These are explained in detail on the readme.  Also, the benefit of using minimatch is that I maintain it, so when it needs to change to support glob, that's very easy for me to do.  Both of these would be dealbreakers anyway.

Furthermore, please be aware that benchmarks must be read very carefully, and can easily be misleading, even if the creator of those benchmarks isn't intending them to be.

The actual _matching_ of glob is (a) not the slow part, and (b) not significantly improved by using micromatch's patterns rather than minimatch's.  Micromatch _creates_ patterns faster, but that happens exactly 1 time per pattern per glob operation.  The `n` for a glob operation is the number of files it has to _test_, the number of directories that need to be read, etc.  If a module can generate a million patterns per ms, that's not significantly different than a module that can only generate 1 pattern per ms, because the glob walk takes tens (or in large folder trees with complicated patterns, hundreds) of ms to complete, with variance well above ±1ms.

I had wondered if maybe minimatch's anchoring and dot-preventing lookaheads imposed a performance penalty.  Towards that end, I've tested extensively how this impacts the actual glob performance, if there's some benefit to using the regular expressions generated by picomatch or micromatch.  There really isn't.  Even just running them in a tight loop calling `pattern.test(path)` over and over again, it's not measurably different.  (There is often a difference but it's owing to noise; it is inconsistent and not statistically significant.)

Also, since the most common patterns, by far, in use in glob expressions are `**`, `*.<some extension>`, `*`, and some number of question marks followed by an extension, minimatch now has fast-path shortcuts for those patterns in particular.  Meaning, in a very practical sense, using minimatch with glob is both more correct, _and_ faster, than using micromatch or picomatch, _even though those libraries are faster when used directly_.

comments.author: AndyOGo
comments.body:
Thank you for your answer @isaacs.

I wonder why these statements are made then?
May I ask you to provide concrete sources?

https://github.com/micromatch/micromatch#why-use-micromatch
> - More complete support for the Bash 4.3 specification than minimatch and multimatch. Micromatch passes all of the spec tests from bash, including some that bash still fails.
> - **Fast & Performant** - Loads in about 5ms and performs [fast matches](https://github.com/micromatch/micromatch#benchmarks).
> - **Accurate** - Covers more scenarios https://github.com/yarnpkg/yarn/pull/3339

How does `micromatch` handle security concerns?
> - **[Safe](https://github.com/micromatch/braces#braces-is-safe)** - Micromatch is not subject to DoS with brace patterns like minimatch and multimatch.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I'm not really the one to ask about those statements, since I didn't make them ;)

The differences between Bash and micromatch/picomatch that I've found are explained in some detail in the readme on this repo, under the "Comparisons with Other JavaScript Glob Implementations" heading.  (One point that's missed there, the POSIX standard classes are actually not as accurate as they are in minimatch, for example `[[:alpha:]]` won't match `'é'`.  But minimatch having support for those at all is a very recent development.)  It passes the spec tests in bash 4.3's test suite, but there are clear examples where it _doesn't_ behave the same as bash, as evidenced by the extensive test suite that minimatch and glob use, which submit bash 5 to a battery of extreme examples and verifies that they all are handled the same way.  Minimatch isn't exactly 100% correct either, though; there are some edge cases that are just nearly impossible to do well in a regular expression, but it's _much_ closer.

Micromatch _is_ faster, as I said, but not in any way that matters for glob's use case.  If you're evaluating a lot of patterns to essentially test once and throw away, especially if it's in a hot path, yeah, it'll be worthwhile to use micromatch or picomatch if the bash coherence isn't a dealbreaker for you (and there are a lot of cases like that).  But glob doesn't do that, it parses the pattern one time in way less than 1ms (whether using micromatch or minimatch), then spends _absolute ages_ by comparison doing a ton of file system operations.

The security thing is sort of weird to bring up.  Minimatch had a redos vulnerability that was reported and swiftly fixed.  I assume that micromatch would have done the same thing (or may even have done so in the past), as this is a pretty normal thing in any software library that processes strings.

So I'm not saying one is "better" than the other, just that the tradeoffs of using minimatch make it a much better, um, match for this library.

comments.author: AndyOGo
comments.body:
Alright.

Thank you for taking the time to give an thorough answer.

issues.title: How to find a `folder` that are not inside `folder`
issues.body:
The task is to find all `node_modules` in the project tree, that are not inside other node_modules.

I tried unsuccessfully something like:

- `('**/node_modules/', {ignore: '**/node_modules/**/node_modules/'})`

- `('**/node_modules/', {ignore: '**/node_modules/**'})`


Any advice on this?
issues.author: wclr

comments.author: vjpr
comments.body:
`('**/node_modules, {ignore: '**/node_modules/**/*'}`

The ignore doesn't prevent it from searching inside `node_modules` dirs though. You can check this by passing in `cache` and reading it afterwards.

comments.author: vjpr
comments.body: This is also discussed here: https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/213#issuecomment-126047563

comments.author: vjpr
comments.body:
@isaacs After a lot of testing It seems there is no way to achieve this.

`('**/node_modules, {ignore: '**/node_modules/**/*'})` - returns `node_modules` folders (what we want), but ends up searching every file in the tree because the ignore pattern doesn't finish in `**`.

`('**/node_modules, {ignore: '**/node_modules/**'})` - doesn't return any `node_modules` dirs, but also doesn't search inside them (what we want).

I feel like the addition of an option to not match the parent dir of a globstar expression would help solve this.

Use case: Excluding `node_modules` folders from indexing in a text editor when using a mono-repo or a repo with many projects inside it.

comments.author: vjpr
comments.body:
`('**/node_modules, {ignore: '**/node_modules/*/**'})` comes a bit closer.

Will search all `node_modules` and their direct children, but not further nested `node_modules`.

But if you have 100s of packages in your `node_modules` in multiple projects you are going to see a huge slow down.

comments.author: wclr
comments.body: Yes I see that glob is not actually right solution for this case, actually I had some other problems with `glob` returning strange results (at least on alpine-node container), so I use it with caution =)

jasonkuhrt

comments.author: NMinhNguyen
comments.body:
> This did the job for us [mrmlnc/fast-glob#how-to-exclude-directory-from-reading](https://github.com/mrmlnc/fast-glob#how-to-exclude-directory-from-reading)

could you specify what pattern you used exactly? I tried `globby(['**/node_modules', '!**/node_modules'])` and `globby(['**/node_modules', '!**/node_modules/**'])` but to no avail :confused:

comments.author: NMinhNguyen
comments.body:
> We used ignore option [mrmlnc/fast-glob#ignore](https://github.com/mrmlnc/fast-glob#ignore).

What syntax did you use? `'**/node_modules', { ignore: '**/node_modules', onlyFiles: false }` and `'**/node_modules', { ignore: '**/node_modules/**', onlyFiles: false }` both return an empty array for me.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This works in v9, but yes, it does read the `node_modules` directory unnecessarily.

```
> require('./').globSync('**/node_modules', {ignore:'**/node_modules/*/**'})
[
  'node_modules',
  'old/node_modules',
  'old/8/node_modules',
  'old/7/node_modules',
  'bench-working-dir/node_modules'
]
```

I think it would be a good feature addition in v9 to allow you to pass in a `ignore` and `ignoreChildren` functions explicitly as the `ignore` option.  It already does support receiving an `Ignore` object, I could just loosen the type to `{ignore:(Path)=>boolean,ignoreChildren:(Path)=>boolean}` instead, and it'd Just Work.

Then you could do something like:

```js
globSync('**/node_modules', {
  ignore: {
    ignore: (p) => false,
    ignoreChildren: (p) => p.name === 'node_modules',
  },
})
```

The challenge here is that the pattern `**/node_modules/**` _matches `.../node_modules`_ because the `/**` can match _zero_ or more path portions.  So you need to ignore `**/node_modules/*/**` (with the extra star), but then it doesn't realize that all children of `node_modules` will be ignored, and does a readdir unnecessarily.

Alternatively (or in addition to this), the Ignore logic should be smart enough to realize that `.../*/**` on an ignore pattern means "ignore the children, but not the thing itself", and save the extra useless readdir.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Custom ignores will be in the 9.2 release.
