issues.title: Glob#abort() is broken
issues.body:
Versions:

- glob 7.1.2
- Node.js both 6.10.3 and 8.0.0

I aborted a Glob instance, then another Glob instance has gotten broken.

**test.js**

```js
const Glob = require("glob").Glob

new Glob("*.js")
    .on("match", () => console.log("match 1"))
    .on("end", () => console.log("end 1"))
    .on("abort", () => console.log("abort 1"))
    .abort()
new Glob("*.js")
    .on("match", () => console.log("match 2"))
    .on("end", () => console.log("end 2"))
    .on("abort", () => console.log("abort 2"))
```

```console
$ node test.js
abort 1
$
```

`"end 2"` is never printed.

----

I guess the cause is at [glob.js#L529-L531](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/8fa8d561e08c9eed1d286c6a35be2cd8123b2fb7/glob.js#L529-L531).

```js
  cb = inflight('readdir\0'+abs+'\0'+inGlobStar, cb)
  if (!cb)
    return
```

This is called via `Glob#_processGlobStar` from the `Glob` constructor.
In the second call, the `inflight()` function returns `null`, so the original `cb` is never called. Then 

```js
Glob.prototype._processGlobStar = function (prefix, read, abs, remain, index, inGlobStar, cb) {
  var self = this
  this._readdir(abs, inGlobStar, function (er, entries) {
    self._processGlobStar2(prefix, read, abs, remain, index, inGlobStar, entries, cb)
  })
}
```

The `_processGlobStar2` method is never executed.
issues.author: mysticatea

comments.author: szabyg
szabyg
isaacs
comments.body: This is no longer an issue in v9.  The way to abort a walk is with an AbortSignal, which aborts all walks in the context of a single Glob object.

issues.title: Option `nocase:true` seems to have no effect on `ignore` patterns
issues.body:
Consider this scenario:
```
$ mkdir test
$ mkdir test/BIG
$ touch test/BIG/file.txt
$ node
const gg = require('glob');
undefined
> gg('test/**',{ignore:['test/BIG/**'],nocase:true},(err,x) => console.log(x)),0;
0
> [ 'test' ]

> gg('test/**',{ignore:['test/big/**'],nocase:true},(err,x) => console.log(x)),0;
0
> [ 'test', 'test/BIG', 'test/BIG/file.txt' ]

> gg('TEST/**',{ignore:['test/big/**'],nocase:true},(err,x) => console.log(x)),0;
0
> [ 'test', 'test/BIG', 'test/BIG/file.txt' ]

> gg('TEST/**',{ignore:['test/BIG/**'],nocase:true},(err,x) => console.log(x)),0;
0
> [ 'test' ]
```
I would expect that `nocase:true` is taken into account when processing `ignore:['test/big/**']`.

(The important implication of this problem is that `del(['test','!test/big','!test/big/file.txt'],{nocase:true})` can delete the test/BIG/file.txt on OSX.)

comments.body: In v9, the `nocase` option on the glob is also applied to the ignore patterns, and is inferred from the platform.  (Ie, defaults `true` on `darwin` and `win32`, `false` elsewhere.)  So there's no need to set it unless you know that the case sensitivity is not the platform default.  (And setting it differently from the platform can cause some really weird results.)

issues.title: Glob.sync results include files that don't exist
issues.body:
I've added a reproduce repository [here](https://github.com/mitchhentges/node-glob-returning-file-that-doesnt-exist).

Sometimes, `glob.sync()` will return paths to files that don't actually exist. For example, with the following tree:

```
$ tree -a
.
├── index.js
└── node_modules
    └── @namespace
        └── src-package-a
            └── node_modules
                └── lib-b
                    └── node_modules
                        └── lib-c
                            └── bin
                                └── file.js
```

The glob `'node_modules/**/node_modules/**/node_modules/**/node_modules'` will return  `node_modules/@namespace/src-package-a/node_modules/lib-b/node_modules/lib-c/bin/file.js/node_modules`, which doesn't actually exist.

Node `v6.11.3`, MacOS `10.12.6`

-----

This is because upon a `readdir` error happening, `glob.sync` assumes that a `ENOTDIR` error means that a path points to a file. However, in the case where a path _includes_ a file (e.g. `/etc/passwd/some_directory`, where `passwd` is the file in the middle of the path), `fs.readdir()` will throw `ENOTDIR`, not `ENOENT`.
The solution here is: when catching an `fs.readdir()` error, if it is of type `ENOTDIR`, verify that the result is indeed a real file, and not an invalid path with a file in the middle.

issues.title: Circular / cyclic dependency between glob.js and sync.js
issues.body:
When using glob in a project processed by Rollup, it identifies a circular dependency between `glob.js` and `sync.js`.

This issue is just a note, because we can use glob as it is and it is possible to work around this problem. But I'd like to notify you of that.
issues.author: paulocoghi

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: This is not a problem.  Why does rollup think it is?

comments.author: masone
comments.body: since #374 is not moving forward - what's the workaround @paulocoghi?

comments.author: paulocoghi
comments.body: @masone , unfortunately I don't remember exactly _but_ I believe the solution is to mark "node-glob" as an external dependency on Rollup config, as well as leaving it on the "dependencies" of your project, instead of the "dev-dependencies", since with this approach it will not be included in the final bundle generated by Rollup.

comments.author: paulocoghi
comments.body:
This is sad because one positive consequence of using Rollup is to dedup and bundle all deps in a single independent JS file with zero runtime deps, providing a better experience for the users of your library (lower lib size, lower install time, etc).

Such workarounds, like informing to do not bundle a specific library, undoes part of this benefit.

I don't know if Rollup should/could be able to solve such issues (circular dep) by itself.

comments.body:
Rollup workarounded it in https://github.com/rollup/plugins/pull/1038 (`@rollup/plugin-commonjs` 22.0.0).

It's a shame to tell `this is not a problem` in a PR which removes needless code and let us compile it.

comments.author: romellem
comments.body:
FYI, just to add some more detail here, the [docs](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob#events) show that the matches are sorted unless the `nosort` flag is set.

The code for this is in [common.js, in the `finish` function](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/b2aa29b/common.js#L152-L153).

comments.author: tarwich
comments.body: I could try adding `nosort` to see if it resolves the problem. To be clear, the problem is that in bash, for example, specifying `ls {b,a}*` implies precedence and we can expect `bar` to precede `ant`. This is powerful for setting the order of configuration files.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@tarwich The reason bash does it that way is that bash is doing an iterative pattern _replacement_ through the pattern string, rather than a recursive directory walk limited by the pattern.  While that's very intuitive in a lot of cases, and easy to tie the results back to the pattern that they expanded into, it's also a bit challenging to do in an optimally performant way in JavaScript.

As it happens, glob v9 takes it even _further_ from doing bash pattern expansion, in order to maximize parallelism in the file system walk for performance, and avoid ever testing the same path whenever possible.  So, you'll probably have to use some other mechanism for path precedence in the match results, I'm afraid.  There's fundamentally no way to get the performance gains in v9 without ditching the string-expansion approach in favor of a recursive fs walk style approach.

comments.author: Yqnn
comments.body:
It's due to the way this library works: each time a file is found, it's added inside several Objects (`this.matches` , `this.cache` and `this.statCache` in [glob.js](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/master/glob.js) ), using a significant amount of memory.
Disabling stats with `stat: false` option may help a bit to reduce the memory footprint.

Bit I guess it's better to use something else to handle huge filesystems.

comments.author: cmawhorter
isaacs
comments.body:
In v9 this might be possible by bringing back the `nounique` option, so that it won't even try to cache anything persistently along the walk.  The caveat will be that:

- `glob()` and `globSync()` will always be a memory hog, because it's giving you an array of results, so there's no way to do that without storing the results.  But iteration or streams would work fine.
- You will get a few duplicate entries, if you use `**` or `..` with any degree of complexity, and don't have backpressure to slow down the stream.

Alternatively, we could preserve uniqueness and support walks of up to several million entries, by having the `seen` set only include the path string rather than the `Path` object.  This will impact performance a tiny bit, because the JS engine can hash objects uniquely in a Set much more efficiently than strings.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The latest version of glob depends on minimatch 3.0.4 which does not have a vulnerability.  There is nothing more that I can do besides updating to a fixed version and moving forward.  The vulnerability is already fixed, it's on consumers to stay up to date with fixes.

Please update your environment to use the latest versions of browserify and glob, and you should be fine.  (Note that `npm audit` and `npm audit fix` make this pretty easy to do.)

issues.title: It fails when the set has only one option
issues.author: jalopezcar

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Not a bug, that's just how globs work.  `{x}` is literally `"{x}"`.  But `{x,y}` is either `"x"` or `"y"`.  Without the comma, it's not a set.

issues.title: Wrote a description of the Chinese version
issues.body:
## Glob 像在shell里面，用`*`等匹配模式来匹配文件。 Glob基于Javascript实现，使用`minimatch`库进行匹配。
### 使用 使用`npm`安装 ``` npm i glob ``` ```js var glob = require("glob") // options可选 glob("**/*.js", options, function (er, files) { // files是匹配到的文件数组 // 如果`options`中的`nonull`为true, 当未发现时， files返回["**/*.js"] // `er`是一个错误对象或者null }) ``` ### Glob 入门 "Glob"是一种模式，类似于在命令行中输入`ls *.js`，或是在`.gitignore`文件中写`build/*`。 在解析路径模式时，大括号内使用逗号进行分隔，分隔部分可以包含`/`，所以`a{/b/c,bcd}`会被展开为`a/b/c`和`abcd`。 在匹配路径使用时，以下字符有一些特殊的作用： - `*`：匹配单路径下的 0 个或 多个 字符串。 - `?`：匹配一个字符串。 - `[...]`：匹配指定范围内的字符串，类似于正则表达式中的`[]`。如果`[]`中的第一个字符串是`!`或者`^`，则匹配不在范围内的任意字符串。 - `!(pattern|pattern|pattern)`：匹配与提供模式中不匹配的内容。 - `?(pattern|pattern|pattern)`：匹配提供模式中的 0次 或 1次。 - `+(pattern|pattern|pattern)`：匹配提供模式中的 1次 或 多次。 - `*(a|b|c)`：匹配提供模式中的 0次 或 多次。 - `@(pattern|pat*|pat?erN)`：匹配与提供模式中完全匹配的。 - `**`：和`*`一样，可以匹配路径中的 0个 或 多个，而且`**`可以匹配当前目录和子目录。但无法抓去符号链接的目录。 #### `.`点 如果匹配的文件或目录部分以`.`开头，除非匹配的路径也以`.`开头，否则不会匹配任何模式。 例如 `a/.*/c`模式会匹配`a/.b/c`。然而`a/*/c`模式却不会进行匹配，因为`*`并不是以`.`开始的。 你可以通过设置option `dot:true`，让Glob把`.`做为正常字符串。 #### Basename的匹配 如果你在 option中设置`matchBase:true`，当模式中没有`/`，它会在任意目录下去匹配。例如`*.js`会匹配到`test/simple/basic.js`。 #### 空集 如果没有匹配到任何文件，它会返回空数组`[]`。它与shell不一样，shell会在未匹配的情况下返回本身。例如： ``` $ echo a*s*d*f a*s*d*f ``` 可以通过设置option`nonull:true`，来获得和bash shell一样的效果。 ### glob.hasMagic(pattern, [options]) 如果`pattern`中有任何特殊字符串，则返回为`true`，否则返回为`false`。 注意：`options`会影响结果。 如果options中设置`noext:true`，那么`+(a|b)`将不会视为魔术模式。 如果模式中支持`{}`表达式，那么也属于Magic，例如`a/{b/c,x/y}`。除非option设置`nobrace:true`。 ### glob(pattern, [options], cb) - `pattern {String}`： 匹配模式。 - `options {Object}` - `cb {Function}` - `err {Error | null}` - `matches {Array}`： 匹配模式后的文件名数组。 执行异步全局搜索。 ### glob.sync(pattern, [options]) - `pattern {String}`：匹配模式。 - `options {Object}` - `return: {Array} `：匹配模式下的文件名。 执行异步全局搜索。 ### Class: glob.Glob 通过实例化`glob.Glob`来创建`Glob`对象。 ```js var Glob = require("glob").Glob var mg = new Glob(pattern, options, cb) ``` 它是一个`EventEmitter`。它会立即执行遍历目录进行匹配。 #### new glob.Glob(pattern, [options], [cb]) - `pattern {String}`： 匹配模式。 - `options {Object}` - `cb {Function} `：成功或失败的回掉。 - `err {Error | null}` - `matches {Array}`： 匹配后的文件名数组。 注意 如果option设置`sync`标志, 那么匹配结果可以通过`g.found`获取。 #### 属性 - `minimatch` ：glob使用的`minimatch`对象。 - `options` ：传入的option对象。 - `aborted` ：布尔值，在调用`abort()`时会设置为`true`。 在`abort()`后无法再进行全局搜索，但是可以通过重新设置`statCache`去避免重复系统调用。 - `cache` ：Object，可能有以下值： - `false` - 路径不存在。 - `true` - 路径存在。 - `'FILE'` - 路径存在，但不是目录。 - `'DIR'` - 路径存在，是目录。 - `[file, entries, ...]` - 路径存在，结果是数组，类似于`fs.readdir`的结果。 - `statCache`：`fs.stat`结果缓存，避免多次计算相同路径。 - `symlinks` ：记录哪些路径是符号链接，与`**`模式相关。 - `realpathCache` ：传递给`fs.realpath`的可选参数，避免不必要的系统调用。存储在Glob的实例对象上，可重复使用。 #### 事件 - `end`：匹配完成后，收到匹配的结果。当`nonull`设置时，在没有匹配结果的情况下会在返回的数组中包含原匹配模式字符串，默认情况下匹配结果是被排序的, 除非设置`nosort`。 - `match`： 每次发现匹配结果就会触发，结果中包含匹配的信息。它不会删除重复数据和解析的真实路径。 - `error`：在发生意外错误时触发。 如果设置`options.strict`，那么任何`fs`的错误都会触发。 - `abort`：`abort()`调用时触发。 #### 方法 - `pause`：暂时停止搜索。 - `resume`：恢复搜索。 - `abort`：终止搜索。 #### 选项 所有`Minimatch`的选项都可以传递给Glob去改变匹配模式，此外还添加了一些特有的`glob-specific`。 除非另有说明，否则所有选项默认都为`false`。 所有选项也会被添加到Glob对象上。 如果正在运行多个glob操作，可以通过Glob对象的传递options给后面的操作使用，方便某些`stat`和`readdir`调用。可以通过共享`symlinks` `statCache` `realpathCache` `cache` option加快并行glob操作。 - `cwd`：要搜索的当前目录。默认`process.cwd()`。 - `root`：以`/`开始的挂载位置。默认是`path.resolve(options.cwd, "/")`(Unix系统是`/`，其他的一些Windows系统是`C:\`)。 - `dot`：在正常模式和`**`模式下包含`.`。注意，在模式字符串中定义的点将始终与`.`文件匹配。 - `nomount`：默认匹配模式中以`/`开始的会转到根目录的挂载点，以便返回有效的文件路径。设置`debug`标志关闭此行为。 - `mark`：在目录匹配中添加`/`。注意，这需要额外的`stat`调用。 - `nosort`：结果不排序。 - `stat`：设置`true`统计stat所有结果。它可能会降低性能，并且完全没必要, 除非`readdir`认为文件存在的不可靠标志。 - `silent`：读取目录遇到异常错误，并向`stderr`打印报警信息。设置option`silent`，关闭警告信息。 - `strict`：尝试读取目录遇到异常错误时，进行会继续搜索其他匹配项。设置`strict`后，当出现这些情况时会引发错误。 - `cache` 可以看上同的`cache`属性。传入之前生成的缓存对象保存一些`fs`调用。 - `statCache`：文件系统信息结果的缓存，防止不必要的`stat`调用。 通常不需要设置它，如果你知道文件系统不会在调用中改变，你可以将`statCache`从一个`glob()`中调用传递给另一个的options对象中。 - `symlinks` 已知符号链接的缓存。在解析`**`匹配时，你可以传入上一次生成的符号链接对象用来保存`stat`调用。 - `sync` 弃用：使用`glob.sync(pattern, opts)`替代。 - `nounique`：在一些情况中，`{}`模式会导致同一个文件在结果中出现多次。默认情况下，它会防止结果中出现重复值。设置这个属性会关闭这个行为。 - `nonull`：设置不返回空数组，而是返回一个包含模式本身的数组。这是` glob(3)`中的默认值。 - `debug`：开启后会在`minimatch`和`glob`开启日志记录。 - `nobrace`：不展开大括号，如`{a,b}`和`{1..3}`。 - `noglobstar`：针对多个文件名不匹配`**`（即 会替换为正常的`*`）。 - `noext`：不去匹配`+(a|b)`“extglob”模式。 - `nocase`：不区分大小写的匹配。注意：在一些不区分大小写的系统中`no-magic`模式默认会被匹配，`stat`和`readir`将不会引发错误。 - `matchBase`：如果模式中不包含`/`字符串，则执行仅基于basename匹配。也就是说`*.js`会被视为`**/*.js`，会匹配所有目录下的所有js。 - `nodir`：只匹配文件，不匹配目录。 (注意：只匹配目录，只需要在模式的最后加上`/`) - `ignore`：添加模式或glob模式的数组去排除匹配。注意，`ignore`模式下总是`dot:true`，其他设置无效。 - `follow`：`**`模式下，访问符号链接目录。注意 它可能会导致在出现循环链接时出现大量重复引用。 - `realpath`：设置为`true`会在所有结果中调用`fs.realpath`。在无法解析的符号链接下，返回匹配结果的完整绝对路径。(虽然它通常是一个失效的符号链接) - `absolute`：设置为真，将会在匹配的结果中接收到绝对路径。与`realpath`不同，它也会影响`match`事件中的返回值。

I'm currently doing this, which seems pretty ugly:

```javascript

glob(
  "{!(node_modules)**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/*+(name)*test.js," + 
  "!(node_modules)**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/**/*+(name)*/*.test.js}",

comments.author: ghost
comments.body:
this is what I'm using right now, not sure if there's a better solution:

```javascript
const {execSync} = require('child_process')
const glob = require('glob')

const match = process.argv[2] || ''
const globPattern =
  `!(node_modules)**/{`
  + `**/**${match}*/**/*,`
  + `*${match}*/**/*,`
  + `*${match}*/*,`
  + `*${match}*,`
  + `**/*${match}*`
  + `}.test.js`

const tests = glob.sync(globPattern)
console.log(`${globPattern} matched: `)
console.log(tests)

execSync(`tape "${globPattern}"`, {stdio: 'inherit'})
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
A few notes about `**`: First, it is only relevant when it's the only item in a path portion.  So, `foo**/bar` is the same as `foo*/bar`.  Second, `**` will match zero or more path portions, so `foo/**/**/**/bar` is the same as `foo/**/bar`.  For clarity, never do `**` except separated by a slash, and never follow `**` with another `**`.  There's no point.

Try this:

```
!(node_modules)/**/*name*{,/**/*}.test.js
```

The path portions there are:

- The first is not `node_modules`
- Then zero or more items
- Then either:
    - a path portion matching `*name*.test.js`
    - a path portion matching `*name*` followed by zero or more path portions, followed by `*.test.js`

Am I understanding the intention properly?

Note that this _won't_ match a `*.test.js` in the current directory.  (There has to be a first section that is not `node_modules`.)  If you want to include the current dir, you can do `{.,!(node_modules)}/**/*name*{,/**/*}.test.js`.

issues.title: Is there a maximum number of subdirectories to watch?
issues.body:
I have my Sass files organized in a set of subdirectories: `dir1/dir2/dir3/dir4/dir5 ` 
And I set up the following rule to watch the changes in there: `src('**/*.scss', { cwd: './dir1' })`

Changes on `dir1/dir2/dir3/dir4/_somefile.scss` are picked correctly, however changes on `dir1/dir2/dir3/dir4/dir5/_someotherfile.scss` are ignored for some reason. Is there a max limit for nesting that I'm not aware of?
issues.author: Javi-er

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
There's no limit imposed by this module, unless you specifically set `maxLength` to a number other than `Infinity`.  (`maxLength` removed in glob v9.)  Your filesystem may impose a limit if there's as recursive symbolic link, but that wouldn't be followed anyway for `**`.

So I don't think this is a glob bug, unless you're using a very old version (even old as of this comment from 3.5 years ago).

issues.title: Rewrite this library
issues.body:
JavaScript has come a long way since I wrote this, and while it's used by a lot of people, it has some definite shortcomings that make it hard to improve and modify.

- Rather than the "pile of functions" approach, with everything re-implemented for sync globbing, this should use a class-based approach, where all fs ops are abstracted into methods that can be overridden in a sync child class, like how the internals of node-tar or npm-packlist work.
- Consider not relying on regular expressions or minimatch.  Ie, write a parser that can take a glob pattern (after splitting up the `{,}` sections) and a path, and return the following possible states:
    - NO: definitely not a match, and cannot match children, stop
    - NO, BUT: not a match, but child entries may be a match. Return the portions of the pattern that may match child entries.  Eg `a/**/x/y` against `a/x` would return `**/x/y` and `y` as patterns that could match against child entries.  If the entry is not a dir, this is a NO response.
    - YES AND: a match, and may match child entries (eg `**/x` would match both `a/x` and `a/x/x`, so comparing it to the `a/x` path should say "yes, and").  Return value should include the bits that can match child entries.  So, `a/**/x/**` against `a/x` would return a "yes and" response with `**/x/**` and `x/**` as remaining patterns.  If the entry is not a dir, this is a YES BUT response.
    - YES BUT: a match, but cannot match child entries
    - YES IF: a match, but only if it's a dir (eg `a/*/` matches the dir `a/x/`, but not the file `a/x`)

    For all of these, walk the string and compare with a finite state machine rather than converting to a regexp.
- Fix all the windows stuff.  Not sure what to use as a reference implementation here.  Mingw bash? LSW?
- Consider using a WASM'ed implementation of glob written in rust?  Especially if we're not reliant on JS regexps, this makes a lot of sense.
- Create a custom error type

I will set aside some vacation time to do this if a handful of people decide to [fund this library](https://github.com/sponsors/isaacs).
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: bensampaio
comments.body: Would this also result in the use of promises instead of callbacks?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yes.

comments.author: valango
comments.body: Could it be something like [this](https://github.com/valango/duke)?

comments.author: IonelLupu
comments.body: @isaacs  Do you consider using typescript?

comments.author: cdhowie
comments.body:
In addition to a promises API, I'd love to see an async iterator implementation. This would allow async traversal of huge trees without buffering the entire result set in memory, and be easier to use (`for await ... of`) than the current event/pause/resume interface.

In fact, this would make a promises API redundant; there are already libraries that will iterate an async iterator and collect the results in a promise for an array (such as [async-iterator-to-array](https://www.npmjs.com/package/async-iterator-to-array)).

comments.author: dandv
comments.body: Has [tiny-glob](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tiny-glob) done most of the work? It's written in TypeScript, uses Promises.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I am not intending to use typescript for this.  Nothing against TypeScript, but it's not my preference.

I have not investigated whether tiny-glob has the same adherence to Bash 4's glob semantics.  It's no slight against tiny-glob if it does not, as there are several places where this necessitates arguably less than optimal implementation choices, but there is a value in having a library with a constraint, so I would not suggest that as a replacement unless it does.

I figured it would make sense to implement this as an `objectMode:true` [minipass](http://npm.im/minipass) stream, so that Promise and async iterator behavior would be included without any additional work, since minipass streams are async iterators, and have a `.collect()` method which returns the entire contents in one array, and easily support synchronous operation as well.

issues.body:
I have following structure:
```
data

    └── file2.json

When I run

glob.sync('**', { ignore: ['!data/section/**'] }

I expect to get `[ 'data/section/file1.json' ]` but got an empty array.

comments.body: Pattern negation isn't supported, in the main pattern or in the ignore option.  Why not just glob for the thing you're un-negating?

comments.author: justingrant
comments.body:
> Why not just glob for the thing you're un-negating?

Here's a concrete examples of why glob negation is helpful in the configuration of [cSpell](https://cspell.org/configuration/): I want to spell-check my english localization strings, but not localized strings in any other language:
```json
    "src/locales/**",
    "!src/locales/en.yaml",
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@justingrant Ok, so use an `ignore` option, and a pattern.

```js
glob('src/locales/**', { ignore: 'src/locales/en.yaml' })
```

comments.author: justingrant
comments.body:
The challenge (at least in this use case) is when the list is already negated, like in a .gitignore file or (like in this case) a JSON config file that can't call `glob` directly. Here's the ugly hack I ended up with. Not sure if there's a better way to do it.

```json
  "ignorePaths": [
    "src/locales/[^e]*.*",
    "src/locales/es.yaml",
  ],
```

issues.title: Section with a single option returns empty match
issues.body:
```js
var glob = require("glob")
glob.sync('/*');          // returns a bunch of entries
glob.sync('{/*}'); // empty []
glob.sync('/{}*'); // empty []
glob.sync('/{*}'); // empty []

```
issues.author: asklar

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, that's by design.  `{}` without any commas is a literal `{}`.  Try it in bash.

```
$ for i in {/*}; do echo $i; done
{/*}
```

You could argue that it's a bug in the design of the glob format, and I'd probably agree with you, I mean, it's freaking weird.  But it is what it is.  The goal of this module is to implement glob, not fix it.

comments.body:
There's no option to get an object tree structure, but there are plenty of modules on npm that do give you folder trees.

You could do something like this in the meantime, using `mark:true` to mark directories with a `/` and then sorting them yourself

```js
const matches = glob.sync('test/fixtures/a/**', { mark: true })
const sorted = matches.sort((a, b) => {
  const adir = a.substring(a.length - 1) === '/'
  const bdir = b.substring(b.length - 1) === '/'
  return adir === bdir ? a.localeCompare(b, 'en') : adir ? -1 : 1
})
console.log(sorted)

issues.title: Windows paths normalized to forward slashes, not platform-native backslashes
issues.body:
#### Description of issue

As many folks are aware, much of the world has sandardized on forward slashes as path / folder delimiters (eg `/path/to/your/file.txt` or `https://domain.com/path/to/your/view`).

Windows, however, has historically relied on backslashes (eg `C:\path\to\your\file.txt`).

Node, of course, respects both per-platform:
- *nix: `console.log(process.cwd()) // /path/to/current/folder`
- Win: `console.log(process.cwd()) // C:\\path\\to\\current\\folder`

When `node-glob` returns results on Windows, it outputs them with forward slash-normalized paths. This can cause cross-platform compatibility problems.

Example:
```
let files = glob.sync('*')
let relativePaths = files.map(file => file.replace(path.join(process.cwd(), path.sep)))
```
In Windows, the above code would not work, as using methods intended to ease cross platform compatibility (like `path.sep`) break with `node-glob`'s forward-slash normalized output.


#### Expected behavior
- Glob would output file paths that are platform-native and that can be used without additional denormalization steps


#### Repro steps
- Glob files on Windows; file paths are normalized to forward slashes
- Compare to `process.cwd()`, etc. in Windows; file Node presents backslash-normalized file paths
issues.author: ryanblock

comments.author: jakearchibald
comments.body: I ran into this too. Node's [`path.resolve`](https://nodejs.org/api/path.html#path_path_normalize_path) helps.

comments.author: SnirBroshi
comments.body:
On version 7.1.7:
```js
glob('/**') //= absolute with backslashes
glob('**') //= relative with forward slashes
glob('**', { realpath: true }) //= absolute with backslashes
glob('**', { absolute: true }) //= absolute with forward slashes
```
What is going on

It seems that [tiny-glob](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tiny-glob) works mostly the same and solves this issue, always returning native separators (unless you use an absolute path glob).

comments.author: ryanblock
comments.body: I'm pretty sure 8 resolves this by normalizing everything to forward slashes? Unless anyone disagrees, I am so incredibly happy to close this issue.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yep, that was the intent.  Closing!

comments.author: liudonghua123
comments.body: I use `filename.replace(/\\/g,'/')` like `glob.glob("./**/*.ts").then( filenames =>filenames.map(item => item.replace(/\\/g,'/'))).then(files=>console.info(files))`.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
You can also use the `path-scurry` objects which have `relativePosix()` and `fullpathPosix()` methods.

```ts
const results = (await glob('./**/*.ts', { withFileTypes: true })).map(p => p.relativePosix())
```

issues.title: absolute `ignore` pattern containing `..` does not work unless `nomount` is set
issues.body:
In this example (using glob@7.1.5) `foo` should be ignored, but is included in the output:

```
mkdir /tmp/glob-test
touch /tmp/glob-test/{foo,bar}
node -e "
const glob = require('glob');
glob('/tmp/glob-test/sub/../**',
  {ignore:'/tmp/glob-test/sub/../foo'},
  (err, files) => console.log(files));
"
[ '/tmp/glob-test', '/tmp/glob-test/bar', '/tmp/glob-test/foo' ]
```

The bug is avoided when `nomount` is true:

```
node -e "
const glob = require('glob');
glob('/tmp/glob-test/sub/../**',
  {ignore:'/tmp/glob-test/sub/../foo', nomount:true},
  (err, files) => console.log(files));
"
[ '/tmp/glob-test/sub/..', '/tmp/glob-test/sub/../bar' ]
```

Why am I putting `..` in the middle of absolute patterns? It wasn't my idea. I had `..` at the beginnings of relative patterns (the most typical place to use `..`), and glob-stream (gulp.src) insists on making the patterns absolute (using to-absolute-glob) for reasons I don't understand.

The bug doesn't seem to affect `ignore` patterns that end with `/**`:

```
node -e "
const glob = require('glob');
glob('/tmp/glob-test/sub/../**',
  {ignore:'/tmp/glob-test/sub/../**'},
  (err, files) => console.log(files));
"
[]
```

I wonder if this bug is related to #350 .

issues.title: Virtual file system option?
issues.body:
It'd be great if we could pass in a virtual filesystem as an option. There are several compatible with the `fs` API so it shouldn't require many changes:

- https://github.com/streamich/memfs
- https://github.com/jvilk/BrowserFS
- https://github.com/filerjs/filer (no sync API)
- https://github.com/isomorphic-git/lightning-fs (no sync API)

How it'd work:

```js
let glob = require('glob')
let memfs = require('memfs')

let volume = memfs.Volume.fromJSON({
  '/foo/bar/abc.txt': 'abc',
  '/foo/bar/def.log': 'def',
  '/foo/bar/xyz.txt': 'xyz',
})

glob.sync('/foo/bar/*.txt', { fs: volume }) // returns ['/foo/bar/abc.txt', '/foo/bar/xyz.txt']
```

This would allow usage of `glob` in lots more places without adding any real maintenance burden or requiring users to monkey-patch. All of the hard work would be done by one of the above libraries, and glob could be used in the browser, with dropbox, S3, and many others.

It'd also be useful for speeding up the testing of libraries that rely on `glob` - instead of creating fixtures that touch the real filesystem, the same operations could be done with in-memory objects using `memfs` (although monkey-patching is less risky in test code).

Of course, to make sure it doesn't add overhead to maintenance of this library, it should come with the disclaimer that `glob` doesn't take responsibility for incomplete/imperfect `fs`-emulation. If users have issues with symlinks/missing functionality, they should raise an issue with the library being used to get a virtual fs rather than glob.

comments.author: juliangruber
comments.body:
> Apparently this bug is consistent with some bullshito logic in a linux shell... because... yeah.. lets copy bugs from one system to an unrelated system

`brace-expansion` was designed with the sole purpose of reimplementing the bash logic. If node-glob wants to diverge from that, then one can either switch to another module or add an option to `brace-expansion` that would change its logic. Or of course one could special case the consumer logic to create a different string if there is just one option.

Which way to go is the choice of `node-glob`'s maintainers.

@WORMSS please try to use friendly language, we're all here to collaborate on good solutions.

issues.title: Memory leak in inflight dependency
issues.body:
Hello,

This project uses `inflight` as a dependency, which is known to have a memory leak in it: https://github.com/npm/inflight/issues/5

Inflight has not been updated since 2016.  Would it be possible to remove `inflight` from this project's dependencies?
issues.author: mgoetz-ia

I'm not immediately aware of what it would take to resolve this if it is a breaking change to remove inflight as suggested.

isaacs

comments.body: It was removed in v9. Current version is 10. Done.

issues.title: Error: EISDIR: illegal operation on a directory - docker windows image only
issues.body:
Hi, I have found weird issue, where glob.sync uses scandir syscall on a file. This behaviour only affects glob running in the docker container on windows daemon.

### Code
`glob.sync(path.join(".", "*", "*.csproj"))`

Using nodir option does not fix this issue.

### Configuration tested
node: v14.15.4
node: v12.18.3
image1: stefanscherer/node-windows:12.18.3-windowsservercore-2019
image2: mcr.microsoft.com/powershell:latest
node-glob: 7.1.6
node-glob: 7.1.4

### ERROR
```
Error: EISDIR: illegal operation on a directory, scandir 'C:/src/docker-compose.ci.yml'
    at Object.readdirSync (fs.js:1021:3)
    at GlobSync._readdir (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:288:41)
    at GlobSync._processReaddir (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:137:22)
    at GlobSync._process (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:132:10)
    at GlobSync._processReaddir (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:207:10)
    at GlobSync._process (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:132:10)
    at new GlobSync (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:48:10)
    at Function.globSync [as sync] (C:\src\node_modules\glob\sync.js:26:10)
    at u.getCsprojPaths (my_minified_project.js:1:xxxx)
    at u.run (my_minified_project.js:1:xxxx) {
  errno: -4068,
  syscall: 'scandir',
  code: 'EISDIR',
  path: 'C:/src/docker-compose.ci.yml'
}
```

### Filesystem Info
<details>
  <summary>Filesystem</summary>


- Volume Name : 
-   Volume Serial Number : 0xcc6a5586
-   Max Component Length : 255
-   File System Name : NTFS
-   Is ReadWrite 
-   Is Thinly-Provisioned
-   Supports Case-sensitive filenames
-   Preserves Case of filenames
-   Supports Unicode in filenames
-   Preserves & Enforces ACL's
-   Supports file-based Compression
-   Supports Disk Quotas **(not available in docker but in host machine)**
-   Supports Sparse files
-   Supports Reparse Points
-   Returns Handle Close Result Information
-   Supports POSIX-style Unlink and Rename
-   Supports Object Identifiers
-   Supports Encrypted File System
-   Supports Named Streams
-   Supports Transactions **(not available in docker but in host machine)**
-   Supports Hard Links
-   Supports Extended Attributes
-   Supports Open By FileID
-   Supports USN Journal **(not available in docker but in host machine)**


</details>

### Privileges
PS C:\src> **icacls .\docker-compose.ci.yml**
.\docker-compose.ci.yml NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM:(F)
                        BUILTIN\Administrators:(F)
                        S-1-5-21-2625033213-654498683-907056245-1002:(F)

Successfully processed 1 files; Failed processing 0 files
issues.author: plachta11b

comments.author: bartecargo
comments.body: @plachta11b I know it's a year on from your initial report, but do you recall if you resolved this problem? We've run into the same problem when using a local Windows directory mounted into a Windows container.

comments.author: plachta11b
comments.body: @bartecargo Yes it is too long ago and I forgot what was the cause. I remember that I have big issues with non lts microsoft windows images. Maybe it is linked to this issue too.

comments.author: plachta11b
comments.body: It affects all tested (both servercore and windows) windows containers with hyperv virtualisation. As workaround we are moving project files out of mounted folder and then copy output files back in CI.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Wow, that is really confusingly weird.  It's saying "This is a directory, which is why I can't readdir it" when the error you'd expect there is `ENOTDIR`, because it's clearly not a directory.  Of all the error codes to mix up 🤦

The good news is that v9 is much better about this already.  Any error encountered anywhere just means "nope, can't do that thing".  If it saw the file, but then readdir failed, oh well, guess it's not a dir, move on and handle as if it's a file or some other thing.

issues.title: npm@8 generates an invalid `fs.realpath` dependency for this module
issues.body:
Using npm from Node.js v16.13.0:

**package.json**:
```json
{
  "devDependencies": {
    "tap": "~15.0.10"
  }
}
```

**.npmrc**:
```yaml
save-prefix=~
package-lock=false
legacy-bundling=true
legacy-peer-deps=true
```

With the above, run:

```sh
$ npm i
$ npm ls fs.realpath
```

The result is:

```
❯ npm ls fs.realpath
tap-glob-test@ /private/tmp/10/tap-glob-test
└─┬ tap@15.0.10
  ├── fs.realpath@1.0.0 extraneous
  └─┬ glob@7.1.7
    └── fs.realpath@ invalid: "^1.0.0" from node_modules/tap/node_modules/glob

npm ERR! code ELSPROBLEMS
npm ERR! extraneous: fs.realpath@1.0.0 /private/tmp/10/tap-glob-test/node_modules/tap/node_modules/fs.realpath
npm ERR! invalid: fs.realpath@ /private/tmp/10/tap-glob-test/node_modules/tap/node_modules/glob/node_modules/fs.realpath

npm ERR! A complete log of this run can be found in:
npm ERR!     /Users/jsumners/.npm/_logs/2021-11-10T17_08_57_284Z-debug.log
```

issues.title: Group backreferences?
issues.body:
In regex, you can do groupreference: ```([A-Z])\1``` 
this would match: ```AA```. ```JJ``` and so on.
but wouldnt match ```AZ```.

I cant figure out a way to use ```glob.sync()``` in a way it would match: ```A/A.json```
but wouldnt match: ```A/B.json```
issues.author: OsaPL

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: No way to do that in glob expressions, sorry.  You're gonna have to just use regexps.

issues.title: Incorrect results when no read/execute permissions
issues.body:
This can't be right, no?

```
#!/bin/sh

whoami
ls -ld supervise

cat > test.coffee <<EOF
g = require "glob"
g.glob "*/does-not-exist.txt", (e, files) ->
  console.log e, files
EOF

coffee test.coffee
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2641335/149313719-54fffecc-b24a-4c3f-9993-d9167c6183e2.png)

(There are other readable files and directories in the CWD too.)
issues.author: smblott-github

issues.title: */** matches top level directories
issues.body:
I've noticed what _looks_ to me like a bug: I would expect the pattern `*/**` to match all paths with a minimum depth of one, but it appears to match directories (not files) at the top level as well. A concrete example:

```
$ mkdir -p node_modules && npm install glob@7.2.0
$ mkdir -p test/1/2/3 && cd test
$ echo 'require("glob")("*/**", (_, f) => console.log(f))' | node
[ '1', '1/2', '1/2/3' ]
```

I would have expected `[ '1/2', '1/2/3' ]`, but `1` was also matched.

_minimatch_, on the other hand, behaves as I'd expect:

```
$ mkdir -p node_modules && npm install minimatch@3.0.4
$ echo 'console.log(require("minimatch")("1", "*/**"))' | node
false
$ echo 'console.log(require("minimatch")("1/2", "*/**"))' | node
true
$ echo 'console.log(require("minimatch")("1/2/3", "*/**"))' | node
true
```

I'd be happy to help out - either with a patch if it is indeed a bug, or some documentation if it's defined behaviour, but I'd first need to know which one it is, couldn't mange to find out on my own.

(Tests above were performed with Node.js v16.10.0 on macOS 12.1.)
issues.author: fhd

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Nope.  `**` can match _zero_ or more path portions.  So `*/**` matches `a` as well as `a/b/c/d`.

Observe:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

set -e

shopt -s globstar
shopt -s extglob

rm -rf a b c

mkdir -p {a,b,c}/{1,2,3}
touch {a,b,c}/{1,2,3}/{foo,bar,baz}
# tree

for i in */**; do
  echo $i # includes a, b, c
done
```

```
a  <-- here
a/1
a/1/bar
a/1/baz
a/1/foo
a/2
a/2/bar
a/2/baz
a/2/foo
a/3
a/3/bar
a/3/baz
a/3/foo
b  <-- here
b/1
b/1/bar
b/1/baz
b/1/foo
b/2
b/2/bar
b/2/baz
b/2/foo
b/3
b/3/bar
b/3/baz
b/3/foo
c  <-- here
c/1
c/1/bar
c/1/baz
c/1/foo
c/2
c/2/bar
c/2/baz
c/2/foo
c/3
c/3/bar
c/3/baz
c/3/foo
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The pattern that does what you want is `*/**/*`

That is: one path portion, then zero or more path portions, then another path portion.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> I'd be happy to help out - either with a patch if it is indeed a bug, or some documentation if it's defined behaviour, but I'd first need to know which one it is, couldn't mange to find out on my own.

Technically it's documented that this library matches the behavior of recent versions of bash (v5, I think?  or 4.3?  I think those are the same anyway), so it's _sort of_ documented, but yeah, that could definitely be improved.  Patch welcome for that, as long as it's not just dumping `man bash` into the readme 😆

comments.body: Thanks! I suppose that does make sense indeed - maybe just a bit unintuitive. If I can find a good place to mention this I'll do a PR! But yeah, if it's just consistent with Bash, might indeed not make a lot of sense...

issues.title: Miniver ^3.0.4 dependency breaks when minimatch 3.0.5 is installed
issues.body:
Hey,

Node v0.12.18.  Our version of pm2 depends on a version of shelljs, which depends on glob, which depends on ^3.0.4 of minimatch.  New release of minimatch is busted due to newer syntax used.  I suppose there are a lots of way to look at it, but this arguably breaks semver, IMHO.

  const path = (() => { try { return require('path') } catch (e) {}})() || {

Would you be ok if I pushed a PR changing the dependency version requirement for glob to be 3.0.4 instead of ^3.0.4 until minimatch fixes it?

Thanks,
Matthew Fredrickson

issues.title: Splitting the LICENSE file?
issues.body:
Hi Glob folk,

I'm a part of the Open Source Program Office at AWS and wanted to let you know of a licensing painpoint. When pulling down updated npm packages, the glob package, and packages that include glob, always raise an eyebrow because of the CC-BY-SA license for the logo being mentioned. CC-BY-SA is a curious license for software, thus I get alerts to review. It's the more frustrating because the logo isn't included in the npm package file :)

I thought I'd share this because it's very unique to this project. "Oh, it's the glob text" might be something I mutter on a whole bunch of import-alerts :) I don't expect you to rate my pain highly, frankly the logo is awesome and it's great that it's being called out. But I did wonder if the license of the logo could move out into a separate LOGO-LICENSE file, which then isn't included in the npm package?

Apologies if this is too selfish a suggestion.
issues.author: hyandell

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Would it solve your problem if the CC-BY-SA was called out in the README.md but not in the license file?  Since it's not distributed with the code on npm, that seems like it'd be fine.  Though, I guess it _is_ distributed with the code via GitHub.  @tanyabrassie you own the copyright on that little guy, what's your thoughts?

comments.author: tanyabrassie
comments.body: @isaacs @hyandell that's fine with me!

comments.author: kellertk
comments.body:
Hello glob people!

Another friendly AWS dev here - I'm unrelated to the team that validates licensing at AWS but I am a consumer of packages that consume glob as a dependency. For some more context, we have automated (and non-automated) systems that review open source licensing to make sure that we're in compliance, and because they're unusual for software, the Creative Commons licenses require manual review. Other packages (for example, [@vercel/ncc](https://github.com/vercel/ncc#readme)) are bundling glob's LICENSE file in their distribution, which is flagging the licensing exception - as well as any AWS package that includes glob as a first level dependency.  

I've created a simple PR (#463) that removes the information from the LICENSE file. Please let me know if this isn't appropriate.

comments.author: hyandell
comments.body: Thank you @isaacs and @tanyabrassie for being so understanding :)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Just to repeat what was already said above:

- This is a change that occurred in a semver major.  It should not have broken anyone unexpectedly, unless you are depending on `*` or `>=` or something foolish like that.
- It is dropping support for a use case that has not been supported, explicitly in the readme, for 10 years.
- It _fixed_ the use of escape characters on Windows platforms (which _was_ broken before).

A v8 minor will come with the ability to explicitly choose to use `\` as a path separator _and not an escape character_ in #468.

Since `cwd` and `root` are always paths, and not patterns, we can add `/`-coercion to those automatically.

issues.title: feat: coerce all paths to forward slashes as a config option
issues.body:
With the recent issues in glob v8 (see the conversation in #467), I thought it would be a good idea to add a config option that would coerce the inputted glob path to use forward slashes instead of just forcing users to convert their paths to forward slashes.

Some use cases allow users to predictably use forward slashes (e.x. using relative paths in glob calls), but most don't allow that.

I'm not sure of a name for the config option, I'm open to suggestions. Once I get approval from the owners, I'll go ahead and open a PR.
issues.author: nicolas377

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body: Just clarifying, the config option would be disabled by default.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
How about this: `glob('c:\\some\\path\\*.js', {windowsPaths: true})`
or: `glob('c:\\some\\path\\*.js', {pathSeparator: '\\'})`

With the caveat that if you set `windowsPaths: true` (or `pathSeparator:'\\'`) then there is no way to escape glob characters, meaning it is _impossible_ to ever match a file literally named `*` or `?`.

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body:
I like `pathSeparator`, especially since it can be easily set to [path.sep](https://nodejs.org/api/path.html#pathsep).

Most of my use cases for glob involve resolving paths to an absolute path, and then appending to that string. I'm not sure if my use case is common, but it seems like it would be common, and I think that `pathSeparator` would help a lot with that use case.

comments.author: ath0mas
comments.body:
I like `pathSeparator` with `path.sep` too,
but it may feel "strange" and be misleading for users to have to set it explicitly while the most common or expected implicit default value?

> then there is no way to escape glob characters, meaning it is impossible to ever match a file literally named `*` or `?`

or "better" be an option to explicitly disable this possibility, `disableGlobCharMatch` or so, to allow to match "like before" without any replace or option about the "path separator" thing?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
There really is no way to have glob work as expected, if the escape character and the path separator are the same character. How can it know whether `foo\*` should match "any files within the `foo` folder" or "a file named `foo*`?

Either windows users cannot escape glob chars (which they've complained about), or they have to use / as path separators (which has been the recommendation for a decade now).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: So you can explicitly opt in to using \ as your path separator (and not have escaping), or maybe we make both the path separator _and_ the escape character configurable? But "just use the implicit default path separator, and also be a bash style glob" isn't logically coherent.

comments.author: juergba
comments.body:
On Windows the path separator is always escaped. So `glob('c:\\some\\path\\*.js')` should still work in v8.0.1, because it's unambiguous. I haven't tested though.

In your examples above, it would be: `foo\\*`  which is equivalent to `foo/*`.

Edit: couldn't you just replace every `\\` by `/` whenever the platform is Windows (eg. set by a new option `pathSeparator`), before processing the pattern? Then you have both, the `\\`  path separator and the escaping of glob chars.

`'c:\\some\\path\\*.js'.replace(/\\/g, '/')` => `c:/some/path/*.js`
`'foo\*'.replace(/\\/g, '/')` => `foo*`
`'foo\\*'.replace(/\\/g, '/')` => `foo/*`

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body: The issue that I was describing was when dealing with paths run through either path.join or path.resolve. Most other cases still work, and don't need changes.

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body:
> couldn't you just replace every \\ by / whenever the platform is Windows

That's actually pretty close to the implementation I was thinking of. My idea of the implementation of `pathSeparator` would go something like this:

1. If `pathSeparator` isn't a string value, carry on with the regular glob function.
2. When it is, [split](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/String/split) the given glob pattern across `pathSeparator`.
3. [Rejoin](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Array/join) the split array with the path separator minimatch expects. (currently `/`)

Here's a quick implementation:

```js
const { pathSeparator } = opts;

if (typeof pathSeparator === "string") {
  pattern = pattern.split(pathSeparator).join("/");
}
```

Here's a couple things I don't like with that implementation:

- It creates a new variable `pathSeparator`
  - can be condensed easily, I'm not sure how this project weighs the extra variable vs readability
  - if readability is preferred, I can make the implementation a lot more readable
    - i'll post an example if this turns out to be the case
- It mutates the function parameter `pattern`
  - Not exactly the best practice, but it does what it needs to without having to mess with variable names throughout the rest of the program.

So, after all of this, I just have two questions for @isaacs:

1. In this specific project, do you value readability or a cleaner variable namespace?
2. Does this project follow the principles of functional programming?
    - Not all of them, but specifically, treating parameters as immutable

Once I have answers, I'll go ahead and link a draft PR to this.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/468#issuecomment-1112433063

@juergba You're confusing "escaped in the JavaScript code" with "escaped in the string".  For example:

```js
console.log('c:\\foo\\bar\\*\\baz')
// prints: c:\foo\bar\*\baz
```

So that `*`, is it escaped?  It's ambiguous.  There's no way around that as long as the escape character and the path separator are the same.

Say you want to find all the files in a directory that have a `?` in them.  Today, like in bash, you could use the pattern: `/path/to/dir/*\?*`.  But if you do `glob('/path/to/dir/*\\?*')`, and we swap all `\` chars with `/`, then on windows, that would be: `/path/to/dir/*/?*`.  Or, in english: "files in a subdirectory of `/path/to/dir` whose basename has 1 character followed by zero or more other characters".  Not the same thing, not even close!

> Edit: couldn't you just replace every \\ by / whenever the platform is Windows (eg. set by a new option pathSeparator), before processing the pattern? Then you have both, the \\ path separator and the escaping of glob chars.

This was exactly the problem with Glob v7 and before.  It meant that there is almost no way to match filenames containing `?`, `*`, `[`, `]`, and so on, because the escape character was interpreted as a path separator.  That's why there's been a caveat in the docs for 10 years telling you not to do that.

One possible approach to consider (semver minor change):

- Add _two_ new options, `escapeCharacter` (default: `\`) and `pathSeparator` (default: `/`).
- If `escapeCharacter` and `pathSeparator` are equal, throw an error.  (Windows users can use `\` as their path separator, but only if they _also_ choose a different escape character.)
- Do not replace anything in the string itself, just use the escape character and path separator throughout the codebase instead of the `\\` and `/` literals.  If you set `\` as your path separator, then it's on you to make sure you don't _also_ have `/` in there.  (This is weird, right?  Because _both_ \ and / "work" on windows?)
- So maybe the pathSeparator needs to be _multiple_ characters, none of which can be the escapeCharacter?  I'm honestly sure what is the best blend of "usable" and "sensible" here.

Another possible approach: if a glob pattern matches `/^[a-z]:\\/`, throw an error telling the windows user not to use `\` as a path separator, because that makes posix glob pattern matching impossible to do correctly, with a link to the documentation explaining why.  (Semver major change)

But "just allow `\` to be both a path separator and the escape character" is not the right answer.

@nicolas377 Regarding PRs and readability vs whatever, in this or any of my repos, here are some good rules of thumb:

- Make the change as surgical as possible.  Change the minimum necessary to do what you're trying to do.  (Larger cleanup/refactoring work should be separated from functionality changes.)
- Follow the patterns of the surrounding code as best you can understand them.  Ideally, no one should be able to tell it wasn't there the whole time unless they look at the commit history.
- Ensure that all code you add is fully covered by tests.
- Ensure that the test you add _fails_ if you take out the code you add.  (Ie, the test should be relevant, even if no additional coverage was required.)

This module is marked for rewriting once I get time to do it (maybe months, maybe years, idk, but it's on my list) so definitely try to keep any change as tight as possible ;)

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body: Alright! I'll work on this tomorrow, hopefully I'll have the PR ready for review tomorrow as well.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Another point about code style: I try to follow [Most Restrictive Production](https://blog.izs.me/2022/04/principle-of-most-restrictive-production/), so yes, use consts where possible, small functions, etc.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Maybe another take on this, less general, but YAGNI, so maybe better: just let Windows users specify a `windowsPathsNoEscapes: true` option.  When this option is set, _all_ `\` characters are path separators, _and not escape characters_, so glob patterns like `**/file\[asdf\].\?.js` will be interpreted in that scenario as `**/file/[asdf/]./?/.js` and would match `/path/to/file/[asdf/]./x/.js`, but would _not_ match a file literally named `/path/to/file[asdf].?.js` or `/path/to/filea.x.js`

Then the code change is just `if (options.windowsPathsNoEscapes === true) { pattern = pattern.split('\\').join('/') }`, and it behaves the same on windows as on posix.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Updating minimatch and everything to make the `\` escape character be configurable, and splitting on more than one path separator, not really a good approach.  Way too much complexity and room for gotchas.

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body:
I do have a few ideas, here they are.

Here are two ways I want users to be able to support windows (while removing support for escaping literal glob chars, which can be a feature to be discussed in the future).

Keep in mind that all of these examples are just api calls that I would like to work. They also assume `const glob = require("glob")` and a path run through `path.win32.resolve()` stored in `path`.
```js
const { sep } = require("node:path/win32");

glob(path, { pathSep: sep }, () => {});
```
```js
glob(path, { supportWindowsPaths: true }, () => {});
```

Let me explain these two options (I'll implement them this weekend):

## pathSep

pathSep should be a string that the user intends to use as their path separator.

Internally, pathSep should just perform a replacement on the inputted path, switching the given `pathSep` with either a hardcoded `"/"` or `path.posix.sep`. Here's a pseudocode implementation, assuming the passed options are at the object variable `options` and the passed pattern is available at the string variable `pattern`:

```
If options.pathSep is set to a string that is not empty:
  Replace all instances of options.pathSep in pattern with a forward slash.
```

## supportWindowsPaths

supportWindowsPaths is intended to just be a switch to flip so that glob does all the work on the backend.

Here's a pseudocode implementation, assuming the passed options are at the object variable options and the passed pattern is available at the string variable pattern:

```
If options.pathSep isn't set, and options.windowsPaths is truthy, and windows is the current platform:
  Split the pattern into an array by a regex of path.win32.sep joined with path.posix.sep, and rejoin it with path.posix.resolve()
```

For right now, I'd close that PR, and I'll try to open up a PR this weekend. Expect a draft PR up today or tomorrow.

issues.title: Glob 7.2.0 -> 8.0.3 glob.sync returns different result, why?
issues.body:
Hi guys.

I'm using Windows with node 16.12 and npm 8.1.0

Couldn't figure this out by myself.
I have a simple scenario:

`npm i glob@7.2.0 `
```
const glob = require('glob');
const $path = require('path');

const files = glob.sync($path.join(__dirname, '*.txt'));
console.log(files)
```
Result = [ 'C:/DEV/glob/a.txt', 'C:/DEV/glob/b.txt' ]

`npm i glob@8.0.3 `
```
const glob = require('glob');
const $path = require('path');

const files = glob.sync($path.join(__dirname, '*.txt'));
console.log(files)
```
Result = []

Not sure what I should do here. Tried googling for some time but couldn't find an explanation.

Can anybody help me?
Thanks!
issues.author: fabiohaertel

comments.author: nicolas377
comments.body:
glob v8 updated minimatch, the library that does glob's matching, to v5. Minimatch v5 breaks windows glob matching by forcing all inputs to use forward slashes. Backslashes are considered escape characters. Since you're using the path module, it will automatically use backslashes for you since you're on windows. There are two solutions:
1. import `path/posix` instead of path
    -  that is a submodule of path that provides the *nix version of the path module. You will be able to use it just as you will `path`, but it will automatically use forward slashes
2. manually convert the string to use forward slashes
    - you could use `path.join(__dirname, '*.txt').split(path.sep).join("/")` to split by the separator the path module is using, and recreates the string using forward slashes.

comments.author: fabiohaertel
comments.body: Thanks for the explanation.

issues.title: [Question] Glob for exclude 'node_modules', 'package.json', 'package-lock.json' and include all other files and folders
issues.body:
Hello 👋 

Could someone help me with glob string? I need to exclude 'node_modules', 'package.json', 'package-lock.json' and include all other files and folders.

I tried something like this

`**/!(core|node_modules|.git|package.json|package-lock.json|style.css)/*.*`

But no success.

Thank you!

Best regards, 
Alex
issues.author: aldanchenko

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
That string says "any number of path segments, then something other than (...), then a file with a `.` in it".  So it'll match `a/b/c/core/d/e.f`, because the `**` matches `a/b/c/core`, the `d` is not in the exclude list, and `e.f` matches the `*.*`.  But it _won't_ match, for example, `./x.y`, because there's no "something other than (...)" preceding it.

Maybe you want something like this? `./!(core|node_modules|.git|package.json|package-lock.json|style.css)/**`  That'd pick up all files and directories that do not _start_ with the listed path portions.

Or you could use combine it with an explicit `ignore` option, and do something like this:

```js
glob('!(core|node_modules|.git|package.json|package-lock.json|style.css)/**', {
  ignore: '**/(core|node_modules|.git|package.json|package-lock.json|style.css)/**',
})
```

which would pull in all files everywhere, except those that match the exclude pattern (and avoid walking them at the first level, since you know that you'll be excluding those anyway).

issues.title: concurrent Globs behave incorrectly because of inflight usage
issues.body:
If I abort one `Glob` and start a new one on the same directory, the new one often fails to emit all of the valid matches.

Looking in the debugger, there are many places like this
```
Glob.prototype._readdir = function (abs, inGlobStar, cb) {
  if (this.aborted)
    return

  cb = inflight('readdir\0'+abs+'\0'+inGlobStar, cb)
  if (!cb)
    return
```

In the new `Glob` run, `inflight` sees that there's still a running `readdir` call from the old, aborted glob.  `inflight` returns `null` and `_readdir` returns early.  But because the old glob is aborted, it never calls the callback, so the new `Glob` never gets the results.

Also, it's fundamentally incorrect to dedupe the requests with `inflight` this way, because one Glob may get results from a deduped request that's fulfilled by another Glob with different options.  (For example, the `readdir` results depend on the `mark` option).  Global caching/deduplication should be avoided.

## Reproduction
```js
const { Glob } = require('glob')

const glob1 = new Glob('*')
glob1.abort()

const glob2 = new Glob('*')
glob2.on('match', (f) => console.error(f))
glob2.on('abort', () => console.error('abort'))
glob2.on('error', (e) => console.error(e.stack))
glob2.on('end', () => console.error('end'))
```

### Expected output
Contents of the current working directory, followed by `end`

### Actual output
Nothing!  The event loop dies because `glob2` is waiting on the inflighted `readdir` callback, but it never gets called back because `glob1` was aborted before `readdir` finished.

If you comment out `glob1.abort()`, `glob2` produces the expected output.
issues.author: jedwards1211

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed in v9

Cypress is a test suite that I am using to run regression tests to ensure that my website is able to create downloads correctly. However, although Cypress has the means to test for the presence of files on disk and is able to read the contents of those files, Cypress lacks the ability to test for dynamically generated file names.

issues.title: Update to minimatch 6
issues.body:
Currently, glob requires [minimatch `~=5.0.1` ](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/3a5a70af353b163712ee1c142d293d7bf2a661cf/package.json#L23)

This prevents depending software to use minimatch 6

issues.author: wouterdb

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
It'll be done as part of https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/pull/489

I'm way down in a rabbit hole of making a path walking module that's (hopefully) as fast as JavaScript can be, replacing a ton of object-based caching logic with a general purpose linked list tree on uint32array slabs.

My first approach was ok, but still too slow, because it spent so much time (1) garbage collecting many small entry objects in the cache, and (2) string processing in path.resolve() calls. I'm almost done with a pretty nice general purpose pointer library, which makes it easy to build things like trees and linked lists in a general way, which will make this all a lot easier and hopefully much more performant. 

Once that's done, the bottleneck will probably be the actual string parsing, and if we want to go any faster at that point, it may require exploring a non-regexp based approach to the matching itself, which will mean that minimatch probably changes significantly anyway. We'll see.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
From the changelog:

> * Paths are returned in the canonical formatting for the platform in question.
>
> * Patterns ending in / will still be restricted to matching directories, but will not have a / appended in the results. In general, results will be in their default relative or absolute forms, without any extraneous / and . characters, unlike shell matches. (The mark option may still be used to always mark directory matches with a trailing / or \.)

The new algorithm no longer has the ability to track the use of extraneous dot and slash characters, because each path matched is an object in a tree, and patterns are optimized aggressively. This allows for much faster searching (like, 5-500x faster in many cases), but there's no way to know with certainty which form of the pattern matched any given path.

comments.author: msev
comments.body: thanks @isaacs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Ah, sorry, @romainmenke, I missed your other message:

> > Paths are returned in the canonical formatting for the platform in question.
> 
> Can you give examples of this? Is this the direct cause of what I am seeing?

So, in a nutshell, this means that the returned paths are the "normal" relative paths (or absolute paths if `{ absolute: true }` is used) for the platform it's running on.  So if you do `.////foo/./../bar/*.js`, then the returned paths on posix platforms will be like `bar/baz.js` rather than `.////foo/./../bar/baz.js`.  On Windows, they'll be `bar\\baz.js`, using the platform-specific path separator.

The reason for this is that it's not tracking the full string that it walked down to find the match, it's just doing a walk over the file system and hanging onto the parts of the pattern that are still relevant at any given point.  In other words, it's doing a file search, not a string expansion.  Doing it in this way is radically more efficient, and makes a lot of weird edge cases easier to handle, plus it makes deduplication tremendously easier, and allows for much more efficient handling of `..` and `**` within patterns, but it means I dropping the information about extra slashes and dots in the pattern.

> > Patterns ending in / will still be restricted to matching directories, but will not have a / appended in the results. In general, results will be in their default relative or absolute forms, without any extraneous / and . characters, unlike shell matches. (The mark option may still be used to always mark directory matches with a trailing / or .)
> 
> This section seems focussed on path endings, not path prefixes.

This part in the middle: 

> In general, results will be in their default relative or absolute forms, **without any extraneous / and . characters, unlike shell matches.**

comments.author: romainmenke
comments.body:
> The reason for this is that it's not tracking the full string that it walked down to find the match, it's just doing a walk over the file system and hanging onto the parts of the pattern that are still relevant at any given point. In other words, it's doing a file search, not a string expansion. Doing it in this way is radically more efficient, and makes a lot of weird edge cases easier to handle, plus it makes deduplication tremendously easier, and allows for much more efficient handling of .. and ** within patterns, but it means I dropping the information about extra slashes and dots in the pattern.

@isaacs That's fair.

The issue is also relatively simple to work around.

That others are facing the exact same issue seems to indicate that the ecosystem depends on the old behavior.

Webpack for example could handle `foo/bar.js` and `./foo/bar.js` in the same way, but it doesn't, likely because it didn't need to.

Arguably it might have been better to publish such an extensive rewrite as a new package.
At the same time other packages should not ossify around incorrect assumptions (e.g. relative paths start with `./`)

-----

Maybe good to call out this case in the changelog with an example?
I would not have bothered to create an issue if I had known this was intended behavior.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, as a maintainer, the thing about a community ossifying around your old code is that it's _really_ hard to know when going into a massive overhaul like this exactly what can be discarded and what needs to be preserved, so long-coming much-needed semver major upgrades usually involve a bit of this back and forth. No matter how you try to get the word out there, sometimes the only real way to measure the importance of a feature is by ripping it out, lol

I can see a couple of ways forward (not mutually exclusive).

1. Add a `dotRelative` option (spelling tbd) which would tell glob to prepend `./` onto any relative path it returns. (I'd push this to be an option in PathScurry, as well.)
2. Yes, I agree this should be called out more clearly in the changelog.  It's sort of implicitly there, but clearly not as obvious as it needs to be, I agree.
3. I could track whether a pattern starts with `./`, and imply `dotRelative` like I imply `absolute` for absolute patterns. But, doing this would entail kind of a lot of extra work and be fairly kludgey. The relative/absolute bitflag would need to be a trinary, minimatch would have to change its pattern optimization, etc.  So I'm not very inclined to go that way.
4. Don't add support for it, be intransigent that `''` is a perfectly valid relative path, `./foo/bar` is the same as `foo/bar`, etc. That's not really my style, unless there's some compelling reason not to add a given option, but it's worth mentioning.  Webpack really _shouldn't_ be treating them differently, but it is what it is.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Oh, another question, if the result starts with `../` then does it still need the `./` ahead of it?  In other words, is `./../foo/bar` required, or is `../foo/bar` ok with webpack or other tools that require a dot ahead of relative paths?

I'm assuming that the differentiation here stems from the fact that `require('foo/bar')` is meaningfully different from `require('./foo/bar')`, in which case `../` would be just as good as `./` for establishing relative-ness.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@romainmenke Care to try out #500 and see if it solves your issue?

```

```

And then:

```

// or whatever function you're using to get glob results
```

issues.title: RFE: add sort option (for walk/walkSync methods only)
issues.body: Hello, thanks for building this package! It appears that since V9, sorting is no longer supported. But previous versions were sorted in the same order of the files on the file system. What's the recommended way to get this default behavior back? Thanks in advance!
issues.author: markcellus

comments.author: msev
comments.body:
Hi @markcellus, 

you can use `reduceRight` instead of `reduce`

comments.author: geekact
comments.body: `files.sort()` would help you.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: I could add a sort option, but it'd only be possible for the `walk()` and `walkSync()` methods. The streams and iterators just emit in a somewhat random order due to the parallelism in the algorithm.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
So the feature request is:

Add a `sort` option, which is a function that can be called with `results.sort(options.sort)`, and is only relevant to the `walk` and `walkSync` methods.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Actually, looking into this, it's pretty inconvenient to get the types right, since the things being sorted can be either a Path object or a string, and TypeScript gets annoyed when you try to reference the object type within a property type.  So it could be done, but any sort method you pass in would have to accept `Path | string` as the arguments, and then verify that it's actually a string (when you already know it has to be).

Whereas, if you just do `(await glob('**')).sort((a, b) => number)` it knows that `a` and `b` are strings, because at that point, the return type is fixed.

This is definitely just better done as a one-liner by the caller.

comments.author: geekact
comments.body: By the way, `Array.prototype.sort()` will sort items according ASCII code sequence without callback function. So, I recommend not to use callback function here for path array.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @geekact That doesn't work if you want to sort by something else, though, like locale comparison or dictionary order, or if you have Stats objects, sorting by modified time or something.

Whether you were requesting it or not, I did sort of think it was an idea worth pursuing, but it turned out that it's not actually a good idea, once looking closer at it, so I closed it.

issues.title: Where is the list of breaking changes for the version 9?
issues.body: Does this list exists?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yes, it's in the changelog. https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/main/changelog.md#90

issues.title: Bring back `nounique` option
issues.author: isaacs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: If it uses a memory bound Set, then there's no reason to have `nounique:true`, and instead, the caveat would be that it's only "unique" for up to some fixed number of items.  We could set that pretty high, like 250k or so, without it being much of a problem most of the time, but it could also be configurable, like `uniqueLimit` or something.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yet another approach would be to add a way for Path objects to store some arbitrary data associated with each Path object, and then we wouldn't need to track them in a `seen` set (or have the HasWalkedCache) at all.  Just set the field on the Path.  If it falls out of PathScurry's children LRU, then we might emit it again, but oh well.

Also, it occurs to me that _because_ PathScurry uses an LRU for child nodes, and re-creates them on demand when they fall out of cache and are walked again, we effectively _do_ have a theoretical unique limit of 16384.  However, because it's an LRU, and repeated walks tend to be close to one another, the limit is almost never relevant.  Ie, if you're walking a huge folder tree, then you're going to hit those repeated walks in relatively close proximity to one another _most_ of the time, so the children will be in the LRU and will still match in the `seen` set and `HasWalkedCache` any time they're tested.  To make this a problem, you'd have to have some pretty interesting patterns, or use `follow:true` and have distant symlinks deeply nested within the folder tree, etc.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Actually, looking closer at this, I just verified that even with the `seen` set, Glob can walk a folder tree of 1.5m entries no problem, as long as you use streams or iterators.  (When I try to create more than that, my fixture builder gets upset because it hits the ENFILE limit.)  It definitely gets slow, like it can take a couple of seconds or so, depending on what you're searching for, but it does work.

Even using the array-returning methods, it seems to Just Work.  So I think maybe the issue here is that glob v8 and before were just really inefficient, and probably caching things multiple times unnecessarily.  Which, no great surprise there, I guess.

So, closing this (and #375), but will reopen and investigate if it comes up again.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
There were api changes in the [semver](https://semver.org) major bump from v8 to v9. The first version number changing indicates breaking changes that require you to change your code. That is why npm depends on `^X.y.z` by default, which locks the major version.

The changes are summarized in the changelog.md file, and the new api is described in detail in the readme.

issues.title: platform option not works with cwd
issues.body:
On `windows` when `cwm` option is used and `platform` option is set to `linux` - glob returns nothing.

```
const files = glob.globSync( "**/*.js", { cwd: "d:/projects/tmp", platform: "linux" } );
```
I expected that it will return found files with the `linux` path separators.
issues.author: zdm

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: You've said "this is Linux". Then you've passed in a cwd that is not an absolute path (on Linux). So it resolves it to find the absolute cwd, which is {process.cwd()}/d:/etc. And that doesn't exist.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Does it work if you use `/projects/tmp` as the cwd, and run it in the d: drive?

comments.author: zdm
comments.body:
Yes, it works, when `/projects/tmp` is used.
But `d:/projects/tmp` is valid absolute path for windows. 
What os the sense in `platform` option?
I am using platform linux because I need linux path separator in results.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The `platform` option is there to tell Glob which platform you're using, for cases when it cannot guess correctly for some reason.  For example, in tests where you may be simulating the file system anyway, and want to verify Windows behavior on a mac or linux machine (or vice versa).

> But d:/projects/tmp is valid absolute path for windows.

Yes, it is, but you've told it you're on linux, so it's not treating that as an absolute path, because it's not an absolute path on linux.

If you use `{platform: 'linux'}`, you're going to be using linux path _resolution_, because you've told it that's what you want.  And `path.posix.resolve('d:/projects/tmp')` is `./d:/projects/tmp`, so that's what you're seeing.  This resolution is required at the start, because the `cwd` parameter might not match the actual process.cwd(), so PathScurry does all filesystem operations with absolute paths.

If you need posix `/` path separators in the results, you can just do `results.map(r => r.replace(/\\/g, '/'))`.

Why do you need `/` path separators on Windows?  I'm open to adding an option to do this, if it's a common enough use case.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Closing for now, since this is working as intended.  We can open a new issue if there's a need to add the "use posix path separators on windows" option.
