issues.title: Regression with memfs
issues.author: gawi

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
It's failing because you're giving it a cwd that doesn't exist, according to the fs it has to work with.

Either give it an explicit cwd in the glob options like this:

```js
    const expandedFiles = await glob('/**/*', { nodir: true, cwd: '/' });
```

Or add the `process.cwd()` to memfs (but doing so will mean that it turns up in the glob results, of course).

I think this is a bug, though, tbh.  It shouldn't require that the cwd exist unless you have a pattern that mounts on the cwd.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Fixed in v9.3.1

comments.author: gawi
isaacs
comments.body: Huh, that's weird. I did add a test using memfs, but I'm not using jest. It's possible it's mocking differently, but also possible I just missed an edge case in there. I'll see if I can reproduce, should be an easy fix if so.

comments.author: gawi
comments.body:
In your test, you added `{fs: memfs}` and when I do that, the test passes. But this is not something I can do in my code (i.e. `glob` should work with `fs`, regardless of the implementation (well, assuming it's complete). 

It may be that we are hitting a `memfs` limitation preventing `glob` to work correctly:
* https://github.com/streamich/memfs/blob/HEAD/docs/api-status.md  (memfs fs implementation status)
* https://github.com/streamich/memfs/issues/735 
* https://github.com/streamich/memfs/issues/663

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
So I can reproduce it in Jest, but _only_ in Jest.  If I just run the program, and mock the fs with the mockfs, then it works.

My only guess is that somehow Jest is not mocking the fs reliably?  Or not everywhere?  I really don't know how Jest works, it took some doing just to figure out where I had to put the test file to get jest to run it, and then when I did, apparently they cripple `console.error()` so it doesn't print object dumps?

Idk, whatever is going on here, it _really_ seems like Glob is doing the right thing, and Jest is not mocking the environment like you think it is.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Oh!! Derp, it's because it needs to mock `fs/promises` as well as `fs`.

Change this line:

```js
jest.doMock('fs', () => memfs);
```

to this:

```
jest.doMock('fs', () => memfs);
jest.doMock('fs/promises', () => memfs.promises);
```

And it'll work.

comments.author: isaacs
gawi
comments.body: Many thanks! Great job.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I guess I should probably make path-scurry just load fs and get stuff from the promises object rather than loading fs/promises. I don't remember why I did it that way, there might be a good reason 😅 
Thanks for the tip ;)

issues.title: ! pattern not working
issues.body:
This glob pattern is not working in both version 8 and 9, however, it works in version 7.

`?(drawable-!(xxxhdpi)|raw)/**/*.@(png|svg|jpg)`

File structure

```
root/
├── raw/
│   └── a.json
├── drawable-hdpi/
│   └── b.jpg
├── drawable-mdpi/
│   └── c.jpg
├── drawable-xhdpi/
│   └── d.jpg
├── drawable-xxhdpi/
│   └── e.jpg
└── drawable-xxxhdpi/

```

In both 8 and 9, it will only filter the files under `raw` folder.

But in v7, all the images and json files can be filtered.

```js
glob(
  '?(drawable-!(xxxhdpi)|raw)/**/*.@(png|svg|jpg)',
  {

    dot: true,
  },
  (error, files) => {

    console.log(files)
  },
)
```

issues.title: Package cannot be used without the esModuleInterop flag
issues.body:
Hello, @isaacs,

First of all, thank you for your work. I see that glob@9 has taken a big step forward and now can compete with the `fast-glob` solution.

Unfortunately, I ran into a problem when upgrading to the new version. Right now this package cannot be used without the `esModuleInterop` flag in the TypeScript configuration.

```ts
node_modules/glob/dist/mjs/glob.d.ts:3:8 - error TS1259: Module '"<…>/OpenSource/glob-issue/node_modules/minipass/index"' can only be default-imported using the 'esModuleInterop' flag

3 import Minipass from 'minipass';
         ~~~~~~~~

  node_modules/minipass/index.d.ts:150:1
    150 export = Minipass
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    This module is declared with 'export =', and can only be used with a default import when using the 'esModuleInterop' flag.
    
// …

node_modules/path-scurry/dist/cjs/index.d.ts:4:8 - error TS1259: Module '"<…>/OpenSource/glob-issue/node_modules/lru-cache/index"' can only be default-imported using the 'esModuleInterop' flag

4 import LRUCache from 'lru-cache';
         ~~~~~~~~

  node_modules/lru-cache/index.d.ts:869:1
    869 export = LRUCache
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    This module is declared with 'export =', and can only be used with a default import when using the 'esModuleInterop' flag.
```

Repository for reproducing the problem:

https://github.com/mrmlnc/glob-typescript-issue

I might have a solution to that problem. Change all import's for `minipass` and `lru-cache` packages:

```diff
-import Minipass from 'minipass';
+import * as Minipass from 'minipass';
```

This is a change without having to do anything in your dependencies. Or do something like [this](https://github.com/isaacs/minipass/pull/46) for your dependencies.

All right, maybe there's another solution to all this.
issues.author: mrmlnc

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Hm, the change to minipass was straighforward enough, but lru-cache is written in TS and has a workaround to support setting the default export in CJS without losing type information entirely or requiring people to do `const LRUCache = require('lru-cache').default`

I'll take a look at that example repo, maybe see if there's a way to make this stuff all work.  It's very challenging to get ESM+CJS+TS all building properly from a single codebase in a way that doesn't break.  I suspect that I might just have to make a clean break on all my modules that use a default export, and just use named exports everywhere, which would be a shame.

> First of all, thank you for your work. I see that glob@9 has taken a big step forward and now can compete with the fast-glob solution.

Thanks, you flatter me, but I'm not sure about that. Node-glob might be more correct in a handful of edge cases, but fast-glob is just unrealistically fast. It's black magic, very impressive ;)  If you have any suggestions to make glob faster, I'd be grateful, but I suspect it just comes down to different choices. Mirroring Bash 100% faithfully means handling some really bizarre stuff.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Aha, it seems that `allowSyntheticDefaultImports` is also related to this somehow.

Without either that or `esModuleInterop`, having both `export default Minipass` and `export = Minipass` blows up.  The error message says that `esModuleInterop` is required, but actually either one works.

I'm pretty sure that `import * as Minipass from 'minipass'` won't work with the ESM build though, because it'll get `{ default: Minipass }`, not the actual Minipass constructor.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, so... playing with this more, there doesn't seem to be any way to have a default export from a commonjs file that can be default-imported (without `.default`) into commonjs, with accurate types, unless either `esModuleInterop` or `allowSyntheticDefaultImports` are supported.

So the options are:
- I stop shipping default exports.  (Breaking change to all modules doing so.)
- Use ESM
- Use one of those two flags.

I think I'm going to start moving all my modules to solely using named exports, but that's definitely gonna take a minute.  In the meantime, I recommend using `esModuleInterop` or `allowSyntheticDefaultImports`, or pull in the ESM builds.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Removed all default exports from glob as of v10.  Upgrade and this problem goes away.  I'm flipping the CJS/ESM/TS table on this one lol

issues.title: Globstar pattern returns an empty string as an element of the results array
issues.body:
#### What the problem?

The `**` pattern returns an empty string as an element of the results array.

#### Steps to reproduce

```js
const ng = require('glob');

// MaxDepth option only for shortening the list of results
const entries = ng.globSync('**', { maxDepth: 1 });

console.dir(entries, { colors: true });
```

#### Actual behaviour

```js
[
  '',
  'tsconfig.json',
  'src',
  'package.json',
  'out',
  'node_modules',
  'index.js',
  'herebyfile.mjs',
  'fixtures',
  'README.md',
  'LICENSE'
]
```

#### Expected behavioud

```diff
[
- '',
  'tsconfig.json',
  'src',
  'package.json',
  'out',
  'node_modules',
  'index.js',
  'herebyfile.mjs',
  'fixtures',
  'README.md',
  'LICENSE'
]
```
issues.author: mrmlnc

comments.author: isaacs
mrmlnc
comments.body:
I think this is a very inconvenient solution for the end user. An empty string cannot be used in many standard methods for working with the file system.

```js
> fs.readdir('')
Uncaught:
TypeError [ERR_INVALID_CALLBACK]: Callback must be a function. Received undefined
    at __node_internal_captureLargerStackTrace (node:internal/errors:465:5)
    at new NodeError (node:internal/errors:372:5)
    at __node_internal_ (node:internal/validators:224:11)
    at makeCallback (node:fs:186:3)
    at Object.readdir (node:fs:1369:14) {
  code: 'ERR_INVALID_CALLBACK'
}

> fs.lstatSync('')
Uncaught Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, lstat
    at Object.lstatSync (node:fs:1529:3) {
  errno: -2,
  syscall: 'lstat',
  code: 'ENOENT'
}
```

And every time the user will have to exclude this element from the results array.

And, yeap, makes sense with the options that you mentioned above.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Hm.  Maybe I could have `dotRelative` default to true when the result is an empty string?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @mrmlnc Can you check out https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/pull/520 and see how that works for you?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Someone else pinged me about the same thing, so just further validation that this is legitimately confusing.

Landed in 10.1.0, `''` will never be returned now, only `'.'`.

comments.author: mrmlnc
comments.body: Sorry to keep you waiting. I keep thinking that in most cases this is extra information for the user, but I have a similar request in fast-glob (https://github.com/mrmlnc/fast-glob/issues/47) and I am also going to implement it in the near future.

issues.title: Allow option for returning linux paths (like in version 8)
issues.body:
I think this library is great! Thanks for all the effort put into it.
I also think the recent support for windows is great as well!
Having said that, when upgrading to version 10 a lot of places in my code started failing due to the support for windows as we run the same code on windows and linux.
Some where easy to fix but other were less.
The following is an example of something that broke:
```js
const allTestsRoot = path.join('.', 'test', 'integration', 'query', 'tests');
let globPattern = path.join(allTestsRoot, '**/style.json');
globPattern = globPattern.replace(/\\/g, '/'); // this is needed for windows
const testStyles = globSync(globPattern);
for (const styleJson of testStyles) {
    const testCaseRoot = path.dirname(styleJson); // this will return '.' on windows
    const caseName = path.relative(allTestsRoot, testCaseRoot); // this will return '../../../..' on windows
```

Before version 10 the content of `style.json` would be with `/` on windows, which allows path.dirname to return the correct dir.
Would be great to have some options to "bring back" this behavior - something like `returnLinuxPath` that can be used in windows as well.
Let me know if more information is needed.
issues.author: HarelM

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Hm, yeah that would be handy in a lot of cases for me, too.

The flag should go in path-scurry, and then glob can just pass it along. `returnPosixPath: boolean` or something?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: (I've used BSD systems for long enough that calling macOS "linux" feels deeply wrong, lol)

comments.author: HarelM
comments.body: sure :-) I can't argue here... I knew I was writing a wrong name for the parameter but couldn't remember the right term...

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This was fixed by a1cba4da1612fd0b8d7adffd7da536f75a729454, I just put the wrong number in the commit message 😅 

Landed as `{ posix:boolean }` option, published in 10.1.0.

issues.title: 10.0.0 introduces [ENOTSUP] NO_ABORT_CONTROLLER on Node 14 (latest)?
issues.body:
I lack the means to investigate at the moment (sorry), but the Dependabot PR for glob 9.3.4 to 10.0.0 fails CI on Node 14 (only) for the markdownlint-cli project:

https://github.com/igorshubovych/markdownlint-cli/pull/384

Every test fails with the following output:

```
  - `(node:1802) [ENOTSUP] NO_ABORT_CONTROLLER: AbortController is not defined. If using lru-cache in node 14, load an AbortController polyfill from the \`node-abort-controller\` package. A minimal polyfill is provided for LRUCache.fetch(), but this may cause undesirable behavior in other APIs that use AbortController.
```

glob's package.json still declares support for latest Node 14, so this seems unexpected?

PS: Parallel CI run to demonstrate the base branch of the PR passes on all platforms: https://github.com/igorshubovych/markdownlint-cli/actions/runs/4670376350
issues.author: DavidAnson

comments.author: DavidAnson
comments.body: I should note that while this IS a semver-breaking change for glob, the change log only calls out "No default exports, only named exports".

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
It's only "breaking" if you have Node configured to throw on warnings.

I can make the lru-cache warning even more buried, so it only prints if you actually _use_ cache.fetch, I suppose?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
But in any event, it's (a) a completely valid warning, you are using code that might behave oddly if not provided with the appropriate polyfill (and it is hard to argue that warning is worse than _not_ warning, for an issue that was already present), and (b) the remediation is fairly straightforward.

So, no, this is not a breaking change, it is a bugfix.

(Edit: That is, on the latest updated version of lru-cache, you can set `LRU_CACHE_IGNORE_AC_WARNING=1` in the environment, and it won't print the warning.)

comments.author: DavidAnson
comments.body: Sorry, I was not clear. I was saying that the version change from 9 to 10 allowed for breaking changes in glob, so it was not wrong for this change to be present. However, because it introduces failures to passing CI runs in the default GitHub configuration, I suggest the impact of this change is breaking-like. :) I am not arguing with your assessment, but I suggest that including this in the change log would be helpful for others.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Ah, ok.  Sorry if my pushback was a little too forceful then, I misinterpreted 😅

> I suggest that including this in the change log would be helpful for others.

Not a terrible suggestion, but I think this is even better: I just updated lru-cache so that it'll only print the warning if `cache.fetch()` is actually used.  That's way less disruptive anyway.

`npm update lru-cache` and verify that `npm ls lru-cache` shows v 9.0.3, and I think this ends up being resolved.

comments.author: DavidAnson
comments.body: Love it! That PR now passes as-is and has been merged. Thanks!

issues.title: Ignore option does not work with lowercase drive letter (Windows)
issues.body:
My VS Code extension accepts user-defined include/exclude patterns. I resolve relative paths with `path.resolve(...)`, then convert all `\\` to `/` with `absPath.replace(/\\/g, "/")` (I've been burned by using `\\` in glob pattern on Windows in the past, figured out that glob requires `/` everywhere from [this S/O post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72313482/glob-paths-dont-work-in-my-windows-environment)).

I then find matches like so:
```ts
// include/exclude are string arrays
const matches = glob.sync(include, {
  ignore: exclude,
});
```

This mostly works as expected (no issues on macOS), and even works on Windows if the user's excluded path is absolute, with an uppercase drive letter. The issue comes up when the user's path is relative, ~~because resolving (which includes normalizing) the paths returns lowercase drive letters on Windows~~ (EDIT: Just discovered that resolving does not lowercase the drive name; the lowercase is from how VS Code returns the root directory path).

This lowercase drive letter works fine for included patterns, it returns all of the paths I'd expect to see. However, the lowercase letter causes the ignored patterns to not work as I would expect, given that it _does_ work with the included paths.
 
For example, I would expect the following to match all non-XML files within the source folder:
```
"include": [
  "**/*"
],
"exclude": [
  "**/*.xml"
]
```

These relative paths resolve to `"include": ["c:/blah/**/*"]` and `"exclude": ["c:/blah/**/*.xml"]`, and using these paths as illustrated by my first code snippet returns every single file within `blah`, including `*.xml` ones.

However, if I instead use:
```
"include": [
  "**/*"
],
"exclude": [
  "C:/blah/**/*.xml"
]
```

It works as expected, and all XML files are excluded from the results.

For the time being, I suppose I can just add a hack that forces the drive letter to uppercase on Windows, but since the lowercase letter works in included patterns, I would expect it to also work in ignored ones. Is there a technical reason why this is not the case, or is this a bug? If it's intentional, do you have any best practices for using relative paths in the way I've described?
issues.author: frankkulak

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, I'd expect all those same things to work, so this feels like a bug to me.  My guess is that the settings used for Minipass in the Ignore class aren't quite right.  (Ignores are just a minipass match, not a glob walk, so there's a bit of a semantic difference which is easy to not get exactly correct.)

Just to confirm, you're using the latest version of node-glob?  I'll take a look at this tonight.

comments.author: frankkulak
comments.body:
> Just to confirm, you're using the latest version of node-glob? I'll take a look at this tonight.

Looks like I am one minor version behind, I'm on 10.2.2 and 10.2.3 was released 5 days ago. I just updated and tested again, and the issue is still there.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: The logic used to case-insensitively compare UNC paths like `//?/C:/...` to their drive letter equivalents `c:/...` was forced to be case-insensitive on windows, but _not_ if neither was a UNC path. Fixed that on the latest release, now any drive/unc pattern that is a literal drive letter will be compared against a corresponding literal drive letter in the file, in a case-insensitive way.

```
export async function copyFolder(sourceDir: string, destDir: string, exclude?: string[]) {

    if (exclude) {
        globOptions = { ignore: exclude };
    }
```
this is a snippet of code where the IOptions error occurs

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The minipass error is fixed on the latest release, `npm update minipass` should fix it.

```ts
   let globOptions: glob.IOptions = {};
```

`glob.IOptions` isn't a thing anymore. That was just a DefinitelyTyped-ism.

Do this:

```ts
import type { GlobOptions } from 'glob'
export async function copyFolder(sourceDir: string, destDir: string, exclude?: string[]) {
    let globOptions: GlobOptions = {};
```

If you know that you will/won't use `withFiletypes: true`, you can also use `GlobOptionsWithFileTypesTrue` or `GlobOptionsWithFileTypesFalse` explicitly, so that your return values will be more conveniently `string` or `Path` instead of `string | Path`.

issues.title: How can it be used against a set of paths in an array?
issues.body:
Suppose what I want is not to scan the file system at all, but I have a bunch of path strings in an array and I want to apply them to a glob to see which matches? 

Does this library have such a feature?
issues.author: justinmchase

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Glob doesn't, but minimatch (the library glob uses to perform matching) does.

```console
$ npm install minimatch
```

```js
import { minimatch } from 'minimatch'
// or: const { minimatch } = require('minimatch')
const paths = ['/a/b/c', '/a/b/x', '/x/y/z', '/i/j/k/x']
const fn = minimatch.filter('**/x')
console.log(paths.filter(fn))
// output: [ '/a/b/x', '/i/j/k/x' ] 
```

comments.author: justinmchase
comments.body: perfect, thanks.

issues.title: Possible to set a "nice" level or a throttle of some sort?
issues.body:
First of all, thanks for this excellent library!

I'm using it in an electron app and the end user can point the app at any directory they'd like, and then the app finds and processes particular types of files in that folder using a glob pattern.

If the user points it at a particularly large folder then the glob process can take several seconds (or minutes!) and the application becomes very sluggish while this happens.

It would be awesome to be able to set a "nice" level or somehow be able to indicate that a slower walk is acceptable in exchange for not clobbering the system.
issues.author: jagthedrummer

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yes that would be a good idea. In extreme cases, it may even be faster to throttle, if it saves switching and memory management costs.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I took some time to try to make this work, by setting a max number of `readdir` calls that could be in progress at any one time. 

At least a simple naive approach to this sent performance going off a cliff if the max parallel number was less than a couple thousand. ("Cliff" = about a 20% reduction in performance.) Afaict, the slowdown happens just due to the added work of pushing into an array and checking the status. 

Putting the throttling up higher (ie, around walkCB2 or walkCB3) isn't possible, because it's a "fan out" kind of action, so it'll deadlock if it hits the limit. 

Doing this properly without a significant perf hit looks like it might be a pretty significant refactor.
Información sobre ivex

issues.title: Error: invalid pattern - glob in CommonJS doesn't seem to accept arrays of patterns like glob in ESM does
issues.body:
Working on trying to understand how glob and globSync works in a CommonJS module.  When I attempt to follow the docs to require globSync, I get back `undefined`.  With ESM, everything works great, so I attempted to load glob using a dynamic import:

```
const glob = (await import('glob')).default;
```

The object I get back contains the following methods:

```
glob
Glob
GlobSync
hasMagic
sync
```

I have attempted to use glob.sync, but it only allows simple pattern matchers as strings, whereas the glob I import in ESM modules works with an array of matchers.  I get an error inside Minimatch because the pattern is an array instead of a string, whereas glob in ESM accepts arrays of strings.

Even with dynamic import, like so:

```
const glob = (await import('glob')).default.glob;

const fileMatchers = [
        './test/**/*.spec.js',
        './test/**/*.test.js',
        './test/**/*.spec.cjs',
        './test/**/*.test.cjs',
        './test/**/*.spec.mjs',
        './test/**/*.test.mjs',
    ];

// this line throws "invalid pattern" because fileMatchers is not a string. Only a problem when using require or dynamic import.
const testFiles = glob(fileMatchers, { ignore: 'node_modules/**' });
```

I see the same error even when requiring glob as per the docs:

```javascript
async function getTestFiles() {
  const { glob } = require('glob'); 

  const fileMatchers = [
        './test/**/*.spec.js',
        './test/**/*.test.js',
        './test/**/*.spec.cjs',
        './test/**/*.test.cjs',
        './test/**/*.spec.mjs',
        './test/**/*.test.mjs',
    ];

  const testFiles = await glob(fileMatchers, { ignore: 'node_modules/**' });
  return testFiles;
}
```

**Error message:**
```
de_modules/minimatch/minimatch.js:275
    throw new TypeError('invalid pattern')
```

When I trace the code with the debugger, I don't find minimatch used when running in ESM modules, only CommonJS.  Not sure why.  Hope this information is helpful.  

As an aside, the docs mention using `const { globSync } = require('glob');` to get the sync version, but it returns undefined.

Please let me know if I can provide any further details.  Thank you for your work on this module!
issues.author: jamesmortensen

comments.author: jamesmortensen
comments.body:
After further investigation, I discovered the problem was that I was using an older version of glob in the project, but a newer version inside a module I wrote that also used glob.  The module was using the latest version, 10.2.7, but the main application was using the glob which was loaded by mocha, v7.2.0.

After installing glob in the project dependencies, everything worked as expected.

Thus, there is no issue here.  Hope this ticket somehow helps others!

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yup, that was my guess. Dep management is tricky sometimes. :)

The only reason why process.cwd() would be weird in a glob expression is windows, where the cwd has / path separators.

Is the cwd the same in both cases? Ie, are there files in the cwd that ought to match on the Linux machine?

issues.title: Move glob CLI to separate package?
issues.author: voxpelli

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Originally, I'd thought a glob cli wasn't needed, because there were existing packages that did that. But, it turned out, the existing packages seem to all be abandoned and/or way out of date, so it seemed like it might be a good idea to release an officially supported one.

I can split it into a separate package, but meh. The added deps are tiny compared to path-scurry and minimatch, and people seem to find it convenient to have it built into glob. If jackspeak was vendored, you probably would not have even noticed.

comments.author: voxpelli
comments.body: Thanks for your quick response and your thoughts, I'm happy with that and this issue will answer it for others as well then :)

comments.author: kilburn
comments.body:
@isaacs: do note that several of the packages enventually pulled in by `jackspeak` are esm-only. Hence, due to having this dependency in `glob`, the setups that cannot work with esm-only packages break when updating.

I'm not saying this must get you to reconsider, but it is an important point to take into account :)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @kilburn Sure, but, it only pulls in the esm-only packages when loading via `import`, and the `engines` specification should restrict it to versions of node that can handle exports forking. What setups is this a problem? I'm open to workarounds, if any exist.

comments.author: kilburn
comments.body:
In my case jest started crashing due to the esm-only packages (string-width / strip-ansi). This is because jest-cli used older versions of those that were not esm-only yet. With your update requiring the newer versions, yarn decided to use these newer ones for jest-cli too, leading to an instant crash when calling jest.

I worked around this by forcing the older versions through package.json resolutions rules. This may have broken the glob cli in my installation, but I don't use the cli so I don't care (I would prefer if the cli stuff was split to its own package so I didn't have to deal with that, but I understand that your POV is different, and you are the one doing the work so this isn't a complain!).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> yarn decided to use these newer ones for jest-cli too

Ok.  So.

In my opinion (and I have spent a fair bit of time on this subject) a package manager's most important job -- you could argue, it's _only_ job -- is to provide dependencies according a program's stated dependency contracts. Every time I bump the major version of something with a breaking change, I see issues reported by Yarn users because their package manager served invalid versions of dependencies. No warnings, no install errors, just a crash at runtime.

Seriously, like, we designed a whole module system and package manager in specifically to make this a solved problem, since way back in 2011. Just use npm. It's done this one job correctly forever. Since npm v7, you can even keep using a yarn.lock file, and it'll update it properly, with a correctly resolved dep tree. It's fast, it's reliable, and it actually does its one job.

Or, idk, if npm is just gross to you, maybe there's some newer version of yarn that doesn't have this problem that you could upgrade to? I never hear this problem from pnpm users, you could also give that a try? Even if you have a workaround for this one case, I can pretty much guarantee it'll bite you again at some point eventually. Your package manager is not doing it's job, you should definitely fire it or fix it.

comments.author: Sytten
comments.body:
I mean JS package managers do suck because they don't have the concept of features.
There is really no need to pull the dependency `jackspeak` if I am never going to use it, it only increases your supply chain risk (literally it pulls over 10 packages transitively) and liabilities around licensing.
In that sense the CLI should be a different package.

issues.title: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'withFileTypes')
issues.body:
Hi my friend,

just wanted to let you now that code on current version from npm "^10.3.2" has this beauty in it which leads to the error message given in the title:

node_modules\glob\dist\cjs\src\glob.js:65:37
```
    constructor(pattern, opts) {
        this.withFileTypes = !!opts.withFileTypes;
```

If you call ``const g = new Glob("./data/whatever/*.json")`` without options param, it leads to this error.

Kind regards.
issues.author: heartforit

comments.author: heartforit
comments.body: Could be prevented with  ``constructor(pattern, opts = {}) {``

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Yeah, the options param is required for proper TypeScript behavior. That's how it infers the match type, by detecting whether `withFileTypes` has been provided. If it's defaulted to `{}` then you could do `new Glob<{ withFileTypes: true }>(pattern)` and TypeScript would think it's in `withFileTypes` mode but it actually wouldn't be. And since the template type can't be detected at run-time, there's no way to provide a default that is guaranteed to match the template type. That's why TypeScript doesn't allow defaults for arguments that are set as template types, unless the default is a valid instance of every possible template type.

comments.author: heartforit
comments.body:
Yeah, I see. thanks for your detailed reply. My ticket wasn't meaned to bother you anyhow. I suspected its connected to typescript.
Thanks for still comming back to me, I tought it will be dismissed in the first place, but was surprised that I got teached some stuff. Thanks mate. Keep it up.

comments.body: Don't worry at all, nothing in your request was bothersome. I'm happy to share, you're way more polite than you need to be 😅

issues.title: Feature Request: Get star substitution values
issues.body:
Essentially RegExp substitutions but for globs.

For example, with the regex `/abc(.*)xyz/` matched against `"abc123456xyz"`, the result has a value that corresponds to the string match by `(.*)` (ie. `$1` is `"123456"`).

I'd like to be able to do the same thing for globs.
So if I have the glob pattern `"abc/*/xyz"` which finds `"abc/123/xyz"` and `"abc/456/xyz"`, I'd like to be able to easily extract out the `*` substitution from each result. (e.g. `$1` is `123` for `"abc/123/xyz"`).

Sorry if this already exists, I didn't see it documented anywhere.
issues.author: RebeccaStevens

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This has been requested a few times, and I can certainly understand that it'd be useful.

However, for matching glob patterns, which don't really have any clear semantics for capture groups, and especially given how aggressively node-glob and minimatch are tuned for performance, it is quite challenging to figure out how to go about doing.

For very simple cases, with a single pattern with simple magic bits, like those mentioned here, it'd be somewhat straightforward. When walking the pattern, we'd just have to keep track of whether a regexp matched a path portion, and put it into an array whenever that happens.

It gets a bit more complicated when the patterns aren't as simple as just `*` matching an entire path portion.  For example, if you had `abc/*foo*/xyz`, which matches against `abc/barfoobaz/xyz`, you'd probably want the match results to be `['bar', 'baz']`, right? But, in practice, that `*foo*` is turned into a single regexp, so it'd mean using capture groups when we build the regexp. Fair enough, minimatch already wraps things in parens for most patterns, it just uses `(?: ... )` groups to _prevent_ capturing, because performance. So there'd be a somewhat tricky process of figuring out exactly which groups should be treated as captures, and which remain uncaptured, as in some cases we have to nest quite a few groups in order to implement dot-matching semantics, meaning a glob like `*(?)` turns into this:

```
/^(?:(?:(?!\.)[^/])(?:(?!(?:^|\/)\.\.?(?:$|\/))[^/])*?)?$/
 | |              | |                        | |     |
 | |              | |                        | ^^^^^^^-- then dots allowed
 | |              | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^-- rest cannot be . or .. only
 | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^-- first instance cannot start with .
 ^-- start of pattern
```

So in a case like that, the entire thing would have to be captured, I guess?  But when dealing with nested extglobs and many patterns together, it can be a bit convoluted.

A glob pattern matches a _bunch_ of things, so it's not just like a RegExp, where you have a single string and a set of captures in that one string.  So we'd need a capture set for every match that is found.  Ie, if `abc/*/foo` expands to `['abc/x/foo', 'abc/y/foo']` you'd need two capture sets, like `[['x'], ['y']]`.

Globstar patterns like `a/**/b` that can match `a/b`, `a/x/b`, or `a/x/y/z/b` would be a bit challenging implementation-wise, because globstar is handled somewhat differently than the other single-path-portion matchers, since it needs to fork the process and test without the globstar, with the sub-path without the globstar, and with the subpath _with_ the globstar, to walk down the tree. In each globstar process, it'd need to keep track of where it started globstar-matching which is doable, but not a thing it's doing right now, and would involve additional moving parts.

Lastly, while all of that is just "more work than it seems", but not particularly difficult to work out how to do, where the semantics are probably most challenging, is with patterns where it's not clear _which_ portion should be the "match".  For example, if you have something like `a/**/*/**/b`, minimatch will note that that's equivalent to `a/*/**/b` and remove the first globstar. So if that matches `a/x/y/z/b`, what should the match results look like?  Any of `['x', 'y', 'z']`, `['', 'x', 'y/z']`, or `['x/y', 'z', '']` are defensible, and could be chosen arbitrarily. But, once chosen, it means any changes to that pattern optimization become semver major breaking changes, whereas now, they can be simple patches, since the internal mechanism isn't exposed. (Ie, as long as it matches when it should, and doesn't when it shouldn't, it doesn't matter why.)

So, where I usually land on this is, "glob is a file path expander, not a general purpose string matcher, use RegExp if you want capture groups, sorry".

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Just to add a bit of salt on it, minimatch also converts `<start>/**/*/<rest>` to `<start>/*/**/<rest>`, because globstars are less costly if they occur later in the pattern, and single-star patterns are fast-pathed, so you could have a confusing case where it looks like your `*` matched multiple path portions, and your `**` only matched one.

And there are some cases where we have some known race conditions if multiple patterns can match a single path. Right now, we can just say "oh, that path already matched, skip it" when we encounter that, but if you're depending on the captures being reasonable, that is no longer an option. Consider a pattern like `a/{**/c,c/**}/d`. As it currently works, when considering the path `a/c/d`, only one of those two patterns `a/**/c/d` or `a/c/**/d` will actually be tested, and there are situations where the ordering of them is dependent on filesystem response times, so it's actually nondeterministic. That's fine, as long as it doesn't matter _how_ a path matched, only whether it did or not.

issues.title: Memory leak detected due to inflight
issues.body:
Hi,

issues.title: [request] separate glob-cli from glob
issues.body:
I was investigating CommonJS <-> ESM interop issues on yarn v1 and found the CJS dependencies installed by `glob` > `jackspeak` > `@isaacs/cliui` as the cause of this. See this comment too: https://github.com/yargs/cliui/pull/139#issuecomment-1537061717

TBH, I had no idea that `glob` has its own built-in CLI, and my question is now why? We use `glob` rather as a library than as an executable.

I would suggest extracting the CLI into a separate package.
issues.author: alumni

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yarn allegedly supports package aliases, but resolves deps incorrectly on update. This is a yarn bug. Use npm. It works. Or, blow away your yarn.lock and do a fresh install if you're really attached to yarn, as that seems to be a workaround people have found.

I'm not going to work around yarn bugs in my libraries when pnpm and npm can handle it just fine, sorry.

comments.author: alumni
isaacs
comments.body:
I'm sorry to hear that deleting the yarn lock and reinstalling didn't fix the issue. I thought someone else had gotten it to work that way, I'll try to dig up the older issue when I'm at a full keyboard, maybe they have some other steps that they could share. 

The added deps are tiny. If they were vendored, or if yarn didn't have this bug, you wouldn't notice the added disk usage. And it's only "not a core feature" from the point of view of someone who doesn't use it. For those who do, it's a reason they use this package. 

I'm not going to stop using dependency aliasing when it's useful. As more parts of our ecosystem go esm-only, it's likely that those of us writing hybrid packages will need to use that workaround again in the future. And, I use jackspeak in every cli I write these days. So, it is almost certainly going to bite you again eventually.

In fact, it may have already, in a more insidious way; if the aliased deps in question were not esm-only, thus causing a crash when loaded, it's likely that your programs already just get the wrong versions of aliased deps, which can cause incorrect behavior in ways that are not as easy to detect early.

Your package manager is failing to resolve dependency contracts correctly, and putting the work on you to define resolutions manually. Use a package manager that can manage packages. I'm not trying to be harsh, but having spent over a decade working on npm, it's somewhat shocking to me that such an obvious bug has persisted for so long.

issues.title: Way to stop traversal after match?
issues.body:
I am making a node_modules cleaner, and there are multiple levels of node_modules folders all through my project. I just want to stop traversing if it finds a match, because I will be deleting them and having glob traverse down just adds more.

right now I am globbing for **/node_modules

Thank you very much for looking at this question. We rely on glob here for many things and it's fantastic.
issues.author: httpete

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Hm, this is a good question.  There isn't really a way to do this right now.  You can _sort of_ get close to it if you're iterating rather than just fetching the list, since if you stop consuming the iterator, then the stream will get backpressured and pause, but it'll still keep looking until the _next_ match, so it's not quite what you're looking for.  In fact, if you delete `x/node_modules` while iterating, and when you delete it, it's in the process of finding `x/node_modules/y/z/node_modules`, then there's a chance you'll even end up raising errors as it keeps trying to walk over a directory tree that no longer exists.

I think some sort of option to say "do not look for matches that are children of a match" would be what you need, and should be possible to do. My first thought was "oh, just use rimraf(), since it expands globs already" and then realized, yeah, rimraf has exactly this problem, and will do a bunch of unnecessary work in that case, just like you're describing. Like, if you do `rimraf('a/**')` then it first makes a list of every folder and file under `a`, and then recursively deletes each of them, but since the first one it deletes is `./a`, it's just going to be getting ENOENTs for all the rest, which is potentially a lot of extra syscalls just to make sure it's really super deleted 😅

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Tested this out with iterators, it actually will work as you want if you do it synchronously, since then you can delete it before `glob` has a chance to readdir (or rather, the `readdir` will raise ENOENT, and it'll just abandon that branch of the search).

```console
$ mkdir -p {b,c,d}/{b,c,d}/{c,d,e}/{c,e,f}

$ node
Welcome to Node.js v18.16.0.
Type ".help" for more information.
> const rimraf = require('rimraf'); const glob = require('../'); const it = glob.iterateSync('**/c'); for (const f of it) { console.log(f); rimraf.sync(f) }
c
d/c
b/c
d/d/c
d/b/c
b/d/c
b/b/c
d/d/e/c
d/d/d/c
d/b/e/c
d/b/d/c
b/d/e/c
b/d/d/c
b/b/e/c
b/b/d/c
true
>
```

However, in the async case, you do potentially get one extra match returned, because it does the `readdir` before emitting the match on the async iterator, so there's a race there:

```console
$ mkdir -p {b,c,d}/{b,c,d}/{c,d,e}/{c,e,f}

$ node
Welcome to Node.js v18.16.0.
Type ".help" for more information.
> const rimraf = require('rimraf'); const glob = require('../'); const it = glob.iterate('**/c'); for await (const f of it) { console.log(f); await rimraf(f) }
c
d/c
b/c
c/c  <<<<<<<<----- extra match, unnecessary rimraf
d/d/c
d/b/c
b/d/c
b/b/c
d/d/e/c
d/d/d/c
d/d/c/c
d/b/e/c
d/b/d/c
b/d/e/c
b/d/d/c
b/b/e/c
b/b/d/c
undefined
>
```

comments.author: httpete
comments.body: Thanks for the investigation @isaacs but I don't use rimraf just glob. Maybe this is the only case to have this recursive N level repetition, as clearly only a machine would do such a thing.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Right, but the point is, however you're deleting the folders, if you do it synchronously in a sync iterator, you're not going to get any extras. Otherwise, yes, it'll find extra subfolder matches.

It's probably possible to get glob to have an option to omit any matches that are children of other matches, but it's not high on my todo list. Patch welcome if anyone wants to take a run at it.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
While it's not quite exactly what you're asking for, `includeChildMatches: false` did just land in version 10.4

This is a best effort kind of thing; it just adds each match as an ignore once encountered. But if you `glob(['a/b/c/d/e', 'a/**'], {includeChildMatches: false})`, then `'a/b/c/d/e'` will be emitted as a match before its parent is encountered, so it'll still be included, even though its parent is in the result set.

issues.title: tilde "c:\progra~1\" doesn't work with windowsPathsNoEscape: true
issues.body: Pattern `c:\progra~1\*.ext` with `windowsPathsNoEscape: true` should works the same as "c:\program files\\*.ext"
issues.author: anthony-redFox

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, what's happening there is that it's reading the `c:\` directory, and finding `['Program Files']`, but testing against the `progra~1` doesn't find anything.

What it really should do, which would also have the benefit of being faster in the vast majority of cases, is concatenate all non-magic string portions of the pattern, and just readdir at that first location. Then it'd call `readdir` on `c:\progra~1` in this case, which does return the results expected. Of course, it means you'll get results like `c:\progra~1\Whatever`, but I assume that's probably fine, yes?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: OH, heh, there's code to do this already, but it's breaking out if we haven't seen the path yet, and it is anything other than `'..'`. That looks like a mistake.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Didn't impact perf to any significant degree that I could determine from the benchmarks, even trying to hand it a benchmark that would benefit as much as possible from the shortcut. I guess readdir is just pretty fast 😅 

But fixed on 10.3.4.

```
export 'fileURLToPath' (imported as 'fileURLToPath') was not found in 'url' (possible exports: Url, format, parse, resolve, resolveObject)
```
I think it is related to https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/main/src/glob.ts#L11 where some hoisting issue with conflicting url packages casuses this.

comments.author: colinrotherham
comments.body: TypeScript won't enable [`resolvePackageJsonExports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#resolvePackageJsonExports) by default, but can help for now https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/556#issuecomment-1733508381

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I'll add the `types` field back in, but you _really_ need to start doing `"module": "node16"` or `"module": "nodeNext"` in your tsconfigs, or you're almost certainly getting subtly wrong types any time you import a hybrid module, especially if you are building commonjs.

If you have that option enabled, but are still seeing these warnings, make sure that the module emitting the warning is covered by the tsconfig in question (ie, included in `files` or `include`, and not excluded by `exclude` options).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Ah, actually, no, that leads to incorrect types being exported for the commonjs build, which is why tshy enforces that top level `types` remain unset.

Enable [resolvePackageJsonExports](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#resolvePackageJsonExports), that is the correct way to address this.

comments.author: d-ellis
comments.body:
Did some more digging. The issue seems to be because when using 10.3.6, the types are imported from `./node_modules/glob/dist/mjs/index.d.ts`, whilst in 10.3.7 the types are coming from `./node_modules/@types/glob/index.d.ts`
Looks like the types are now included in `./node_modules/glob/dist/esm/index.d.ts` but they aren't being automatically used when importing from glob

comments.author: colinrotherham
comments.body:
Commit https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/b7d7667df0698b0e2c5589319b2b9b801eae35c0 removes the default exports in favour of package exports only:

```patch
-  "main": "./dist/cjs/src/index.js",
-  "module": "./dist/mjs/index.js",
-  "types": "./dist/mjs/index.d.ts",
```

TypeScript won't doesn't enable [`resolvePackageJsonExports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#resolvePackageJsonExports) by default:

>`true` when [moduleResolution](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/compiler-options.html#moduleResolution) is `node16`, `nodenext`, or `bundler`; otherwise `false`

@d-ellis Hopefully this is something you can enable for now?

Ideally both top-level exports and package exports will be needed for compatibility. There's a similar issue with ESLint plugins not using package exports too https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/557#issuecomment-1733497303

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: dupe https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/555

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Enable [resolvePackageJsonExports](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#resolvePackageJsonExports)

comments.author: ljharb
comments.body:
Looks like https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/compare/v10.3.6...v10.3.7 switched to building with tshy, but since v10.3.6 has "main", and v10.3.7 does not have "main", that's potentially a breaking change in a patch version.

An argument can be made that since `engines.node` is `>=16 || 14 >=14.17`, and all of those versions understand "exports" and don't need "main", that it's not breaking, but `eslint-plugin-import` doesn't yet understand "exports" (since `resolve` doesn't yet understand it), so that's what's causing the issue here.

comments.author: ddolcimascolo

comments.author: colinrotherham
comments.body:
Confirming https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/557#issuecomment-1733405748 from @ljharb commit https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/b7d7667df0698b0e2c5589319b2b9b801eae35c0 affects `eslint-plugin-import` by removing:

```patch
-  "main": "./dist/cjs/src/index.js",
-  "module": "./dist/mjs/index.js",
-  "types": "./dist/mjs/index.d.ts",
```

TypeScript also doesn't enable [`resolvePackageJsonExports`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#resolvePackageJsonExports) by default:

>`true` when [moduleResolution](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/compiler-options.html#moduleResolution) is `node16`, `nodenext`, or `bundler`; otherwise `false`

Which shows up as this compiler error:

>error TS2307: Cannot find module 'glob' or its corresponding type declarations

comments.body: I don’t think it makes sense to demand a typescript config change in a patch version.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
This isn't just a pedantic position, and it's not about me demanding anything.

The `"types"` field being listed in the top-level package.json field was not correct, because in TypeScript, the type of something exported as commonjs is different than something exported as esm. There's no way to make it actually work correctly for both with a single `types` field, if the package exports multiple main exports.

You can enable `packageJsonExports` support in TypeScript in a number of ways, but if you aren't enabling it _somehow_, then you're getting the wrong types for this module. And if you're not actually using a node version that supports package.json exports, then there's no way that you could actually be using this module anyway.

As of TypeScript 5.2, `module` and `moduleResolution` must be compatible. To get the correct types to load from this module you can:

- set `module` to `node16` or `nodenext` and `moduleResolution` to the same value.
- set `moduleResolution` to `bundler` (I'm not familiar with all the particulars of this, because I don't use bundlers, but people are using it successfully with webpack and the like, I believe.)
- explicitly set `resolvePackageJsonExports: true`

I am not the one demanding this. TypeScript is. All that I've done is stopped incorrectly advertising the wrong types to modules that are incorrectly importing this library without package.json exports enabled. If you were loading this module without exports support enabled, you already had a bug in your config. My bug was keeping yours hidden, and I fixed mine.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @vitonsky You are already using configs that are compatible only with ts 5.1, and not 5.2. What's stopping you from changing `moduleResolution` from `node` to `node16`? That would resolve this issue, with the added benefit of your build getting the correct type information.

I'd really like to only move forward with this, not backwards. Shipping incorrect types isn't good, and `main` is an affordance only needed for _very_ old node versions that aren't supported by any of my code anyway.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@ljharb 

> Quite a lot of effort, unfortunately, which is why it's still not yet done.

Is there a way to make it not a lot of effort?

I just sent a PR draft, but I see now that you already had one that's been in progress for quite a while. Exports support was added in node 12, 7 years ago, and the last version of node to lack it was EOL'ed over 2 years ago. I know the goal is to support node versions back to the dark ages, but at this point, failing to support `exports` feels like it's not living up to being a correct implementation of require.resolve. It strikes me as a weird choice to support node <=10 behavior _instead of_ the current active LTS versions (and even the LTS version that's been dead a year).

> I totally agree, it's just that a single line in package.json seems like a small cost to pay to me for now ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

The problem is that the "single line" is incompatible with `"type": "module"` if it's commonjs, so it's still either a footgun of subtly incorrect types, or reverting all my tests, bins, etc, to commonjs, or still a breaking change anyway, since anyone resolving to that main entry and then trying to `require()` it will throw an error. The easiest and most consistent approach overall is to ditch top level `main` and `types`, and use the modern export mechanism.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
#559 

Seems just setting main and types doesn't actually solve the issue.

I think the only way forward here is to tell TSC to load modules properly.

comments.author: joshkel
comments.body:
If I understand the situation correctly: (I apologize if some of this has already been covered - I'm trying to verify my own understanding and see if everyone's on the same page. Please let me know if I've made any mistakes - corrections welcome.)

First, 10.3.7 causes many common configurations to stop working. So, even if it resolves an issue with the types, it does so by breaking things for many other users. This seems like a semver-major breaking change to me.

Second, in practice, I don't think anyone has complained about type issues in 10.3.6 and prior (even if they were technically incorrect).

Third, the recommended solution of changing how tsc's configuration (i.e., setting `"moduleResolution": "node16"` or setting `"resolvePackageJsonExports": true`) is a major change for projects using glob - requiring, to some extent, converting each project from CommonJS to ESM. That's quite a lot of work.

Fourth, the [resolve](https://www.npmjs.com/package/resolve) package is also affected, and updating that would also be quite a lot of work.

Fifth: I don't think the types in 10.3.6 were actually broken.

According to [Are the types Wrong?](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/):

* [10.3.6](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=glob%4010.3.6) was good.
* [10.3.7](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=glob%4010.3.7) and [10.3.9](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=glob%4010.3.9) fail to resolve types using TypeScript's `"node"` / `"node10"` resolution mechanism (the issue we're discussing here).
* [10.3.8](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=glob%4010.3.8) doesn't raise errors on "Are the types wrong?", but it introduced another error (see #559, which I'm not able to reproduce locally). It also changes its own package.json type from CommonJS to module, which I thought was a breaking change.

If I understand correctly, the specific types issue that @isaacs is trying to address is described by "Are the types wrong?" as [FalseCJS](https://github.com/arethetypeswrong/arethetypeswrong.github.io/blob/main/docs/problems/FalseCJS.md) and [FalseESM](https://github.com/arethetypeswrong/arethetypeswrong.github.io/blob/main/docs/problems/FalseESM.md). However, I don't believe that the problems they describe apply to glob 10.3.6 - they say that the "golden rule of declaration files" is that you can't try to use one `.d.ts` file to cover two different (e.g., CommonJS and ESM) `.js` files, because it will cause problems for `"moduleResolution": "node16"`. However, glob 10.3.6 didn't do that:

* If you're using `"moduleResolution": "node10"`, then tsc evaluates 10.3.6's `"main"` and `"types"` fields (and, as I understand it, ignores 10.3.6's `"module"` field - [that's a feature that bundlers added](https://stackoverflow.com/q/42708484/25507) for ESM support before Node.js standardized on `"exports"`, and it's ignored by Node). In this mode, tsc doesn't support ESM, so there's no possibility of a mismatch between CommonJS and ESM. (Note that 10.3.6's actual `.d.ts` files are identical across the CommonJS and ESM builds - therefore, using an ESM .d.ts _file_ with a CJS .js _file_ is okay, as long as tsc doesn't become confused about which _type_ it thinks each file is.)
* If you're using `"moduleResolution": "node16"` (which enables tsc's ESM support, so tsc now has to care about ESM versus CJS), then you've also enabled using package.json exports. 10.3.6's package.json correctly uses separate types within its package.json exports for `"import"` versus `"require"`, so it was fine.

As additional evidence, I tested every combination of tsconfig `"module"` / `"moduleResolution"`, default versus namespace import of `glob`, and top-level package.json `"type": "module"` versus CommonJS for glob 10.3.6 and 10.3.7 that I could think of, and I could not find any circumstances where 10.3.6 had type errors or where 10.3.6 worked worse than 10.3.7.

(Because package.json's `"module"` field is a bundler feature, I should add that I did not attempt to test bundlers. Since glob is a Node.js module and doesn't make much sense in a browser, Rollup is probably the only bundler that applies here? I'm less familiar with it, but [@rollup/plugin-typescript](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@rollup/plugin-typescript) uses TypeScript, and Rollup ought to support package.json exports, so I'd expect it to act the same as tsc.)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Node 10 is not supported. It hasn't been supported for quite some time, since v8 3d6c4cd2f3a6b7bdfdbf01fe9483badd5486fd71. The fact that it can't be loaded using the node10 module resolution _is actually correct and intended_.

> In this mode, tsc doesn't support ESM, so there's no possibility of a mismatch between CommonJS and ESM.

If you are using node10 style module resolution with a hybrid module, it is impossible to guarantee that you are getting correct types. AreTheTypesWrong doesn't cover these scenarios. It is a valuable and useful tool, and I really like it, but the cases it covers are all situations in which the tsconfig matches the program loading the module.

If I set `main` and `types` to cjs, and you load the module from ESM or via async `import`, then ts in `moduleResolution:'node10'` mode will compile the code using the CJS types, but in fact, the ESM will be loaded at run time.

If I set `main` and `types` to esm, and you are compiling in node10 resolution mode, then you'll get an errors like #559 (among other things). It apparently doesn't respect `"type": "module"` in package.json in that mode, so there's no way to give it a main that _isn't_ commonjs, in node10 module/moduleResolution mode.

I understand that since glob does not use default exports or other features that will today trigger acute problems when loaded with the incorrect types in your programs, it might seem like a needlessly pedantic point. But:

1.  this is a bug waiting to happen, I have run into several issues as a result of this sort of discrepancy in the code I've been writing and consuming,
2. at best, it's going to make tsc needlessly wrap the import in its esModuleInterop code, which is suboptimal, and  
3. I'm trying to make my corpus of open source manageable at scale, and that is only possible by relying on consistent patterns that can be made universal across the modules I maintain.

The only way forward is to make your tsconfig match what your program is doing, or avoid using modules that use `exports` in package.json to ship hybrid support for both CJS and ESM.

comments.author: colinrotherham
comments.body:
This isn't the fault of `glob` but lots of popular incorrectly-typed packages now don't work alongside it:

>The current file is a CommonJS module whose imports will produce 'require' calls; however, the referenced file is an ECMAScript module and cannot be imported with 'require'

For example, lots of tooling on GOV.UK Frontend can't use to ~`Node`~ `NodeNext` as shown here:

* [Problems with `@rollup/plugin-terser`](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=%40rollup%2Fplugin-terser%400.4.3)
* [Problems with `@rollup/plugin-replace`](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=%40rollup%2Fplugin-replace%405.0.2)
* [Problems with `@percy/puppeteer`](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=%40percy%2Fpuppeteer%402.0.2)
* [Problems with `filesize`](https://arethetypeswrong.github.io/?p=filesize%4010.0.12)

Sadly, these packages come from a very popular era of CommonJS with ES module types

Until these packages update, TypeScript 5.2 [`"moduleResolution": "Node10"`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#moduleResolution) supports them:

>`'node10'` (previously called `'node'`) for Node.js versions older than v10, which only support CommonJS `require`. You probably won’t need to use `node10` in modern code.

But the release of `glob@10.3.7` does lose [`"moduleResolution": "Node10"`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#moduleResolution) compatibility:
https://github.com/alphagov/govuk-frontend/actions/runs/6310135700/job/17131564591

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I apologize for not breaking this sooner. That would have made this transition easier.

Glob hasn't supported node v10 for a very long time now. It was a mistake to ever have `main` and `types` at the top level of the package.json while shipping hybrid with `exports`. I did not realize the implications of that at the time, but thankfully (albeit somewhat annoyingly), typescript 5.2 has forced the issue.

If you are in fact using node 10, then you need to not be using this version of glob. If you are building for modern node, then you need to be telling typescript about it. Until you can get your program up to date with modern platforms that match and are in sync, the only safe way alternative is to be extremely cautious, pin all deps, only pull in updates after a careful review, etc. It sucks and is inconvenient, but it is what it is.

comments.author: colinrotherham
comments.body: Makes sense, yet is it too late to move the breaking change to v11?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I mean, the presence of top level `main` and `types` is a bug in v10, v9, and v8. The real question is, should it be backported to the last 2 majors? (The answer is no, I ain't got time for backporting to old majors lol.)

The question of whether it's even a breaking change is very debatable. I'd argue that if you are using a tool A that depends on tool B behaving incorrectly, it's not a breaking change to correct the behavior of tool B, even though doing so does require changes in tool A. The "breaking change" here is dropping support for node v10, but that already happened, 2 majors ago. If you have your toolchain configured to _pretend_ to be node 10 (which is what it amounts to if you tsconfig `"moduleResolution":"node10"` and rely on tools that only support node 10 loading semantics), and that's breaking, well, node 10 isn't supported, hasn't been for a long time, so if you're pretending to be node 10, things might not work.

comments.author: joshkel
comments.body:
Thanks for the reply, @isaacs. And thanks for your work on glob.

> Node 10 is not supported. It hasn't been supported for quite some time, since v8 3d6c4cd.

I understand that Node 10 isn't supported, but that isn't relevant to `"moduleResolution": "node10"`. `"node10"` is what TypeScript used to call `"moduleResolutionNode": "node"`; it's the original, pre-ESM (so CommonJS) Node.js module handling. In other words, `"node10"` doesn't mean "pretend to be Node 10"; it means "stick with CJS module resolution, as supported by Node 10 (even though lots of other more modern packages also still use CJS, for a variety of reasons)." There are still large portions of the ecosystem on CJS (see, e.g., [here](https://blog.isquaredsoftware.com/2023/08/esm-modernization-lessons/)); it's not legacy in the same way that Node.js 10.x itself is.

Much of your perspective seems to be "it's called `node10`, so it's obsolete." (If I've misunderstood your position, I apologize.) But Node 20 itself still fully supports [Node 10-compatible CommonJS resolution](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#modules-loaders). Using an older interface / standard that's compatible with Node 10 isn't the same thing as pretending to be Node 10. Node 20 still doesn't provide full equivalents for ESM that it did for CJS (as seen in some of Jest's issues), and so `"node10"` doesn't mean "obsolete since Node 12 was released."

> The fact that it can't be loaded using the node10 module resolution is actually correct and intended.

If not working with TypeScript and CommonJS resolution is correct and intended, then that's... surprising, considering that glob 10.x did everything I would expect a CJS-compatible module to do (no `"type": "module"`, a CJS `"main"`, a `"types"` that caused no reported issues for CJS users, and a `cjs` directory). But that's your prerogative.

> If you are using node10 style module resolution with a hybrid module, it is impossible to guarantee that you are getting correct types. AreTheTypesWrong doesn't cover these scenarios.

Yes, it does. That's what its `node10` line is checking.

> The cases it [AreTheTypesWrong] covers are all situations in which the tsconfig matches the program loading the module.

Maybe this is contributing to our differing views? My tsconfig _does_ match the program loading the module: I have a project that, for lots of reasons, still uses CommonJS, so I use `"module": "commonjs", "moduleResolution": "node10"`.  So tsc expects to load CJS code at compile time, and it transpiles to `require` so that Node.js loads CJS code at runtime. Since glob 10.3.6 shipped both CJS and ESM code and shipped types compatible with tsc-expecting-CJS, everything worked.

> If I set main and types to cjs, and you load the module from ESM or via async import, then ts in `moduleResolution:'node10'` mode will compile the code using the CJS types, but in fact, the ESM will be loaded at run time.

This is true, but it seems like a rare scenario? As I understand it, typical TypeScript usage is to use straight CommonJS (`"module": "commonjs", "moduleResolution": "node10"`) or straight ESM (`"module": "node16', "moduleResolution": "node16"`). As I described in my earlier comment, main and types as CJS works just fine in this case.

The only way I've found to create the scenario you've described (main and types as CJS, load as ESM) is to force it - deliberately mix and match tsconfig.json options (e.g., `"module": "esnext", "moduleResolution": "node10"`), and use a default import (`import glob from 'glob'`) (because ESM and CJS are otherwise too similar for type differences to cause an issue), and enable [`esModuleInterop`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#esModuleInterop) (because tsc otherwise complains that it can't find a default export in glob 10.3.6).

That is a bug, but it's easy to work around (switch from default import to namespace import), and, like I said, I'm guessing it's a rare configuration to begin with. (Considering the huge number of tsconfig.json options that TypeScript exposes, it wouldn't surprise me if any nontrivial package can find multiple configurations that don't work; I'm not sure that automatically means a bug in those packages?)

And glob 10.3.7 seems like a step backwards - from what I can tell, it went from "rare (?) combinations of options and use cases with CJS resolution don't work, with workarounds available" to "CJS resolution doesn't work."

> If I set main and types to esm, and you are compiling in node10 resolution mode, then you'll get an errors like #559 (among other things). It apparently doesn't respect "type": "module" in package.json in that mode, so there's no way to give it a main that isn't commonjs, in node10 module/moduleResolution mode.

Yes, that matches my understanding - main and types should use CJS, so that they can serve as the fallback for non-ESM-aware code and modes. Main and types as ESM would be incorrect.

> I understand that since glob does not use default exports or other features that will today trigger acute problems when loaded with the incorrect types in your programs, it might seem like a needlessly pedantic point...

This is your choice, of course, and I'll respect whatever you decide. But, if I understand correctly, this is effectively dropping CJS support (making glob ESM-only) for a good chunk of TypeScript users. I humbly suggest that that's a breaking change and that a good solution would be to restore 10.3.6's type definitions for 10.x then release the new approach as 11 (just like Sindre Sorhus did when he switched to ESM-only modules). Even if glob v8, v9, v10 were not intended to support CJS resolution for TS users, Hyrum's law seems to apply.

And, again, thanks for your work. And I apologize for my long-winded post, and I apologize if I'm coming across as too argumentative. I'll accept whatever you decide, no need to reply unless you want to.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> Much of your perspective seems to be "it's called node10, so it's obsolete." (If I've misunderstood your position, I apologize.) 

No apology needed, it's my own lack of clarity, but no, that is not my position.

It's not the name that makes it obsolete, I don't care about that. It's the lack of support for `exports` that is a problem.

"CJS module resolution", as you're calling it, is "use main, do not resolve exports". However, in practice, if you are _not_ running your program in node 10, the actual behavior is "use exports, do not resolve main".

> (Considering the huge number of tsconfig.json options that TypeScript exposes, it wouldn't surprise me if any nontrivial package can find multiple configurations that don't work;

Lol, you're tellin me. 😅

> If not working with TypeScript and CommonJS resolution is correct and intended, then that's... surprising, considering that glob 10.x did everything I would expect a CJS-compatible module to do (no "type": "module", a CJS "main", a "types" that caused no reported issues for CJS users, and a cjs directory). But that's your prerogative.

You seem to be under the impression that glob no longer supports CommonJS. But it absolutely still does. Try `require('glob')` in a repl, you'll see it works just fine in any node version going back to v12.

The only reason it was not using `"type": "module"` is that my tests were written in CJS. And that, only because it was using a version of tap that didn't support ESM. Tap 18 does support ESM, and it's a more ergonomic way to write tests, so that's what I'm moving towards. The `package.json` files in `dist/{commonjs,esm}` set the module type for the distributed modules, _assuming that they are resolved via the exports object_, which they are at run-time, and so the `"type"` at the top level of the package ought to be irrelevant to your build, because it is irrelevant to your actual build target.

However, when loading with `"moduleResolution": "node10"`, it does not parse exports, it _only_ looks at `main`, because you are telling TS that you are building for an environment that supports looking up package names in node_modules which have a CommonJS `main` or `index.js`, but _not_ resolving `exports`. This results in 4 possible confusing/suboptimal situations:

- main pointing to ESM + `"type": "module"` AFAICT, this makes TS try to load the types as CJS, and fail when on the types in `dist/esm/...`
- main pointing to CJS + `"type":"commonjs"` Unnecessarily restrictive, running tests in CJS mode, loading code from CJS. This is annoying because while ESM code can load CJS no problem, loading ESM from CJS is a pita.
- main pointing to ESM + `"type": "commonjs"` Incorrect, not sure what the outcome would be. But it has the same ergonomic issues with testing and building.
- main pointing to CJS + `"type": "module"` Incorrect, unsure what the outcome would be. But maybe this one could work? I suspect that it may cause problems with any node version that doesn't support exports but does support `"type":"module"` (if there are any), but since it's clear idgaf about node pre-14 (usually pre-16), maybe that's worth trying?

> But, if I understand correctly, this is effectively dropping CJS support (making glob ESM-only) for a good chunk of TypeScript users.
> And glob 10.3.7 seems like a step backwards - from what I can tell, it went from "rare (?) combinations of options and use cases with CJS resolution don't work, with workarounds available" to "CJS resolution doesn't work."

That is not correct. `moduleResolution: node16` has been around about as long as node 16, and if you are using that setting, then it will know to load the CJS or ESM code and types appropriately, because it will properly resolve using the `exports` object, as every version of node shipped in the last 7 years has done, and as your program almost certainly does at run-time.

So CJS still works, you just have to tell TS that your program is using a version of node that resolves package.json exports.

> The only way I've found to create the scenario you've described (main and types as CJS, load as ESM) is to force it

TypeScript 5.2 makes this footgun harder to access, so this might not be allowed there, but in TS 5.1 and earlier, you could do this when package.json had top-level main/types fields:

```ts
// tsconfig: { "module": "commonjs", "moduleResolution": "node" }
// very common setup!
import('glob').then(glob => {
  glob
  // ^ ESM glob at runtime, but TS sees CJS types, wrong
})
```

In this contrived example, it's not so bad. But it gets difficult to unwind when there's a chain of dependencies, the types are coming to you second-hand reexported, etc.

comments.author: doberkofler
comments.body: I completely agree with @joshkel and would also suggest to "simply" delay this change to the next major release.

comments.author: colinrotherham
joshkel
comments.body:
@isaacs, thanks for your patient explanation. I had failed to realize that setting `"moduleResolution": "Node16"` did not automatically enable ESM, so that's a major mistake in my previous comment.

The challenge I'm running into (and much of the reason I was confused) is because it seems that several popular packages I'm using have broken package.json `"exports"` types. As long as their top-level `"main"` and `"types"` are correct and package.json exports can resolve to CJS, this is fine.  The result of the glob changes still kinda feels like dropping support for TS with CommonJS (because, as a result of all these bad exports types, sticking with CommonJS and `"moduleResolution": "node10"` appears to be the most reliable setup), and it seems that the ecosystem is a good several years behind the "package.json exports is mature and standardized, so just use it" ideal that you're describing, but my technical understanding of the details was wrong.

And I realize that none of this has to be any of your concern, but forcing downstream projects to deal with it all at once, as a result of one package's semver-patch upgrade, makes working through other packages' type issues trickier.

> main pointing to CJS + `"type":"commonjs"` Unnecessarily restrictive, running tests in CJS mode, loading code from CJS. This is annoying because while ESM code can load CJS no problem, loading ESM from CJS is a pita.

Understood. It's sure nice to not break existing code, though. 🙂  (Of course, there are always workarounds, such as renaming tap tests to .mts or putting a `{ "type": "module" }` directory in the test folder - but, again, that's your prerogative, no justification needed.)

> TS 5.1 and earlier, you could do this when package.json had top-level main/types fields...

I'm not sure that this is correct. If I compile the sample program you gave in glob 10.3.6, then `import('glob')` is converted to `Promise.resolve().then(() => require('glob'))`. (Even if it did use ESM imports, glob's ESM and CJS types are the same, so it doesn't appear to be an issue for glob. As you mentioned, other scenarios, such as default exports, may be trickier, and I think this is turning into a tangent from the original issue.)

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Looking into this further, it seems that a commonjs main and `type:module` is actually not a hazard for any node version that can parse the actual code here, so that's fine.

You'll quite easily get the wrong types if you manage to have TS configured with `"module"` set to `"node16"`, `"nodenext"`, `"es2022"`, or `"esnext"` along with `"moduleResolution": "node"`, but thankfully, TS 5.2 does not allow that any more. It's *somewhat* concerning to me that there are people who've reported just such a setup being broken by this change, which is part of why I've been so stubborn about it; breaking their build is actually the ethical thing to do in that case, because it's currently running broken.

But the goal of hybrid modules is maximal support for the greatest number of scenarios, so may as well exploit the loophole.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> The challenge I'm running into (and much of the reason I was confused) is because it seems that several popular packages I'm using have broken package.json "exports" types. As long as their top-level "main" and "types" are correct and package.json exports can resolve to CJS, this is fine.

Gah! It's definitely not "fine", that's terrible. Don't use those packages! The type information that you're getting from their top level `types` and `main` field isn't corresponding to what your program actually loads. That means that _your_ libraries that use those packages will likely export incorrect types as well, if you expose any of their types.

issues.title: v10.3.7 is broken
issues.body:
Had a heck of a time figuring out why my CI/CD pipeline broke today with no changes since last week.  It looks like I was grabbing the latest package of `blob` published in npm (v10.3.7) and getting this error (note: using TS here): 

```
error TS2307: Cannot find module 'glob' or its corresponding type declarations.

import { glob } from 'glob'
                     ~~~~~~
```

I downgraded to v10.3.6 and this works without errors.  It appears this [related commit](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/1d3fdd22d4f398abe32d344a69fce27207d59cab) is failing some GitHub checks as well.  Just thought you'd like to know. :slightly_smiling_face:

comments.body:
