comments.author: jmjones-ping
comments.body:
Definitely thankful for the package - but please just realize even restoring things to the way they are supposed to be causes significant rework for some of us - especially in larger repos.

We're on a typescript version where `packageJsonExports` isn't available and in a monorepo, so we're stuck updating everything and testing, which I wouldn't really expect in a patch release update.

Thanks for sharing all the threads that share the reasoning as that makes sense, but just wanted to share a bit why some of us are a bit surprised by the patch version update.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@jmjones-ping It is never fun to learn that there's been a subtle bug lying in wait threatening to cause undefined behavior at run time, or process gaps where you are on the bleeding edge of one piece but unmaintained legacy versions of another. I'm sensitive to the surprise and frustration, and also glad that you found out this way, rather than another. 

If you are using a very old typescript version (or node, express, react, etc, any significant platform dep), I strongly recommend pinning all dependencies and being extremely cautious about any upgrades, until you can update the platform dep to a current supported version. Most library authors do current work on current platform versions, and expect that they are configured correctly in order to behave correctly.

comments.body:
I can see how it'd be useful. Unfortunately, just having a cli script at all is pretty controversial, and past experience with npm has taught me that properly handling `.gitignore` "just like git does" is much less straightforward than one might expect. Your wrapper works fine for your use case, but once something like that is there, people start to expect it to properly handle absolute paths (which are mounted on the folder of the `.gitignore` file, not on the root of the fs as they are in this script), nested `.gitignore` files it finds, negated ignores within ignored folders (which aren't actually supported by glob ignores currently), and so on.

comments.author: udarrr
comments.body: Let my explain. One of your dependency is being used strip-ansi since version 7.0.0 has been converted to ESM https://github.com/chalk/strip-ansi/commit/7cda68dcadde18b19bfa31b6223e9f0e60b3e319 the dependency has been included to @isaacs/cliui. It's some kind of breaking changes and can't be used for packages with commonjs approach.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@udarrr Trust me, I am fully aware of what packages are being used.

Jackspeak depends on @isaacs/cliui, which depends on:

```
    "strip-ansi": "^7.0.1",
    "strip-ansi-cjs": "npm:strip-ansi@^6.0.1",
```

What this does is that it tells npm (or any package manager that understands this syntax, which has been in npm for a _very_ long time at this point, dating back to npm version 4, iirc) to install the version 7 of strip-ansi in `node_modules/strip-ansi`, and install version 6 of strip-ansi in `node_modules/strip-ansi-cjs`.

At run time, the cjs implementation of @isaacs/cliui does this, in `build/index.cjs':

```js
const stripAnsi = require('strip-ansi-cjs')
```

and the ESM implementation in `index.mjs` does this:

```js
import stripAnsi from 'strip-ansi'
```

The module that gets loaded depends on whether you use `import` or `require` to load it, by referencing the `package.json`'s `exports` field:

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": [
      {
        "import": "./index.mjs",
        "require": "./build/index.cjs"
      },
      "./build/index.cjs"
    ]
  }
}
```

This works with CommonJS, and it works with ESM, provided that you are using a package manager that is up to date and complete, and a node version since node 12.

That's why I asked, what are you installing and running it with?

Because I think you are using a broken package manager, and that is the problem.  Usually when people report these sorts of problems, it's because they are using yarn version 1.  If that's what you're using, I suggest you stop doing that.

comments.author: udarrr
comments.body: Hi @isaacs yes you are right... Thank you for the clarification!

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
What do you mean by "return value format"? It should always be an array of strings, unless you specify `{ withFileTypes: true }` in the options.

If you are saying that the order of the returned results is inconsistent, then yes, that is true. It's dependent on the order in which the operating system returns values from the directory `read()` operation, which is nondeterministic.

If you want the results to be sorted, you can `globSync(...).sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b, 'en'))`.

issues.title: Clarify nocase documentation (specifically when setting it to not match OS behavior)
issues.body:
The docs state:

> nocase should only be explicitly set when it is known that the filesystem's case sensitivity differs from the platform default. If set true on case-sensitive file systems, or false on case-insensitive file systems, then the walk may return more or less results than expected.

1. It's not clear what is meant by `may return more or less results than expected` - perhaps some example could be provided?
2. Commit https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/02b76fd55cb978784355f6c391fc14002a652eac (following https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/526) suggests that explicitly setting `nocase` as to not match the OS behavior, even when it is known that the filesystem's case sensitivity differs from the platform default, is a legitimate scenario. If that is indeed the case, the wording above would ideally reflect that (as I read it, it currently suggests the opposite).
issues.author: ohadschn

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Good point, doc patch welcome or I'll get to it eventually. Brain dumping the answer here, unpolished.

You can do a case insensitive match on a case sensitive file system, though it's a bit less efficient, because it requires regexp comparisons in all path portions, instead of just those containing magic. 

However, the reverse is not true; you *cannot* correctly do a case *sensitive* match on a case insensitive file system, because `stat('FILE')` will succeed if `file` exists, and there's no efficient and reliable way to determine the "real" canonical case across platforms.

As to "more or less results than expected": if the case sensitivity setting doesn't match the system (and the heuristic mentioned in the PR isn't triggered; for example, a case sensitive macOS fs), then if there's a file at `foo/BAR`,

- `Foo/*` will not find a match, because `readdir('Foo')` fails
- `foo/Ba*` will return `foo/BAR` because the pattern is converted to a case insensitive regexp 
- `foo/Bar` will not find a match. 
- `Fo*/BAR` will find the match

which, all together, is a confusing 

In the other direction, where the fs is case insensitive, but we THINK it's case sensitive,

- `Foo/*` will find the match
- `foo/Ba*` will find not the match
- `foo/Bar` will find the match
- `Fo*/BAR` will not find the match

In other words:

- incorrectly set as `nocase:true`
    - patterns match more than they should
    - strings match less than expected
    - `Foo` will find a match, but `Fo*` will not
- incorrectly set as `nocase:false`
    - patterns match less than they should
    - Strings match more than expected
    - `Fo*` will not find a match, but `Foo` will

comments.author: ohadschn
comments.body:
@isaacs thank you for the quick and detailed response!

May I suggest breaking down the `nocase` setting to the following:
1. `fscase` - overrides the `node-glob` inference of the FS case sensitivity. For this setting, keep the instruction `should only be explicitly set when it is known that the filesystem's case sensitivity differs from the platform default.`. It should be pretty self- explanatory that unexpected results are to be produced when `glob` is fed misleading information. I wouldn't even bother with the examples above, as I suspect they might do more harm (confusion) than good.
2. `matchCaseSensitivity` - default to the resolved `fscase`. In case the latter is case-sensitive, allow case-insensitive matches (using the slower regex approach). In case the FS case is case-insensitive, throw an error if case-sensitive matches are requested.  Again - I don't think any example is necessary, it should be clear that one can't ask the FS for something it doesn't support (if it doesn't discern between cases, you can't ask for case sensitivity in your matches).

issues.body:
Hi! It seems in v10, results are in reverse alphabetical order.

Repro: go to https://npm.runkit.com/glob and paste in:

```js
var glob = require("glob")

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: They're not sorted at all, and the order is non deterministic, set by os latency. Do not depend on it.

comments.author: mmkal
comments.body:
That's good to know! They do seem to consistently be in reverse order though, for the cases I've tried. Do you think there's something that got changed recently that caused that? I'll make sure to add a `.sort()` wherever I'm accidentally depending on it, but I imagine there are others who will find the reverse order unexpected and might hit issues.

Failing that, would you accept a docs update pull request?

comments.author: 1j01
comments.body:
Version 9 removed sorting. To sort results [just as version 8 did](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/v8.1.0/common.js#L20), use:
```js
glob.sync(...).sort((a, b)=> a.localeCompare(b, 'en'))
```

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
> They do seem to consistently be in reverse order though, for the cases I've tried.

That is 100% a result of how your computer's file system chooses to return results. On a different operating system, file system, disk controller, ..., it could easily be returned in alphabetical order, in order of last access, entirely random, or any other arbitrary order.

Sort if you depend on it, don't depend on it if you're not sorting it yourself.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: And yes, doc patch welcome 🤗

issues.title: `follow: true` doesn't follow more that 1 symbolic link
issues.body:
"_By default, a ** in a pattern will follow 1 symbolic link if it is not the first item in the pattern, or none if it is the first item in the pattern, following the same behavior as Bash._". However, some dependent modules contain multiple nested symbolic links, and the option follow: true only operates for a single level of symlinks.

**dir_foo**
```
foo.txt
```

**dir_bar**
```
foo
```

**dir_baz** 
```
bar
```
In this case 
`glob.sync('dir_baz/**', {follow:true, nodir:true})` returns `foo` instead of `foo.txt`.

comments.body:

```
$ tree
./
├── dir_bar/
│   └── foo -> ../dir_foo/
├── dir_baz/
│   └── bar -> ../dir_bar/
└── dir_foo/
    └── foo.txt

6 directories, 1 file

$ node
Welcome to Node.js v20.11.0.
Type ".help" for more information.
> require('../').globSync('dir_baz/**', {follow:true})
[
  'dir_baz',
  'dir_baz/bar',
  'dir_baz/bar/foo',
  'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt'
]
> require('../').glob('dir_baz/**', {follow:true}).then(console.log)
Promise {
  <pending>,
  [Symbol(async_id_symbol)]: 143,
  [Symbol(trigger_async_id_symbol)]: 132
}
> [
  'dir_baz',
  'dir_baz/bar',
  'dir_baz/bar/foo',
  'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt'
]
> require('../').glob('dir_baz/**', {follow:true, nodir:true}).then(console.log)
Promise {
  <pending>,
  [Symbol(async_id_symbol)]: 257,
  [Symbol(trigger_async_id_symbol)]: 246
}
> [ 'dir_baz/bar', 'dir_baz/bar/foo', 'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt' ]

> require('../').globSync('dir_baz/**', {follow:true, nodir: true})
[ 'dir_baz/bar', 'dir_baz/bar/foo', 'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt' ]
```

comments.author: pamelalozano16
comments.body:
In the given example, shouldn't `> require('../').globSync('dir_baz/**', {follow:true, nodir: true})` return 
`[ 'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt' ]` only and not `[ 'dir_baz/bar', 'dir_baz/bar/foo', 'dir_baz/bar/foo/foo.txt' ]`?

I see that [`if (ifDir && this.opts.nodir) return undefined`](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/97611cd366e5906a4c58df3f5214af843b0a5e63/src/walker.ts#L187) uses [`.isDirectory()`](https://github.com/isaacs/path-scurry/blob/main/src/index.ts#L699) function from path_scurry, checks if the current `.path`, is a directory. However, when `.path` is a symlink that points to a directory it is not skipped.
The path is still added to `this.matches`, since `ifDir` equals `false`.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
@pamelalozano16 Yeah, this is interesting. It's *not* actually a directory, so maybe it should be included. But you've set `follow: true`, so maybe we should be testing the target rather than the entry itself. I'm somewhat torn.

@frontzhm 请在此处的评论中使用英语。

comments.author: nex3
comments.body: +1 for treating symlinks to directories as directories with `follow: true`. I think the expectation when that option is passed is that symlinks become fully transparent representations of whatever they link to as much as possible, and doing otherwise is likely to break users who aren't thinking carefully about edge cases. This also matches the behavior of earlier versions of `glob`, and so is less likely to cause breakages for users upgrading.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: @nex3 Yeah, I've noodled on it more since posting that comment earlier today, and I think you're right. `follow:true` should be "symlinks are treated as their targets in every way", not "follow symlinks, but still treat them differently than directories".

comments.body: Will publish once CI says it's ok

issues.title: Inconsistency when run in root drive path `/`, prefixes match with `/`
issues.body:
👋 First off thanks for your work on glob. 🙏

There's a bug in [npm workspaces](https://github.com/npm/cli/issues/7413) that I [tracked back to this unexpected result in glob](https://github.com/npm/cli/issues/7413#issuecomment-2093514472). 

Here's an easy way to demonstrate the issue:

```
➜  Desktop deno eval 'import("npm:glob@10.3.12").then(({ glob }) => glob("bin")).then(console.log)'
[ "bin" ]
➜  Desktop cd /
➜  / deno eval 'import("npm:glob@10.3.12").then(({ glob }) => glob("bin")).then(console.log)'
[ "/bin" ]
```

When run in `~/Desktop` it's matching the folder `bin`, and when run in `/` it's matching `/bin`.

Is this expected behavior?
issues.author: reggi

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Interesting, so the issue is that an entry in `/` has the `/` prefix attached to it?

That's not expected, no. Definitely a bug, because it's not how bash 5 does it.

comments.author: reggi
comments.body: @isaacs yep! thats the issue!

comments.body: Fixed in latest :)

issues.title: Update to path-scurry and glob broke npm temporarily
issues.body: This is working now, but there was an issue for a little while when installing `node-gyp` which uses `glob` as a dependency @ ^10.3.0: Temporarily glob could not find `path-scurry^1.11.0` which was causing our CI to break when installing node-gyp
issues.author: justin-masse

comments.author: justin-masse
comments.body: I'm curious, was the new version of `glob` just released before the new version of path-scurry? I don't know how that would be possible based on teh package-lock in that repo... or was this version published too quickly while `path-scurry` was still propagating to npm servers/etc?

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Sometimes "eventual consistency" is a little more "eventual" than we'd like.

I definitely published path-scurry first, but when they're within minutes of each other, I've seen that happen before. It's rare, but rare thing * millions of people installing millions of packages = daily occurrence.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
There's also your local `~/.npm` cache to consider. Say you fetch glob and then 2 minutes later fetch path-scurry. Then 3 minutes later (the glob cache has expired) you try to fetch both. `path-scurry` is still cached, but glob revalidates, and so you apparently get the later thing before the earlier thing.

Gets complicated. 🤷

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Huh. Yeah, that is indeed  weird shortcoming.

In the short term as a hacky workaround, you could implement your own IgnoreLike object to do whatever you want, but that's klunky af and not very well documented. It does work tho, and I'm relying on it in other places, so it's unlikely to break inside a major version. 

Maybe minimatch needs a "matchRoot":true option or something, so that `/x/*` would match `C:\x\y` on windows.

issues.title: Node 14 support broken due to jackspeak dependency
issues.body:
Trying to execute `npx glob` using Node 14.20.1 results in the following:

```
npx: installed 41 in 2.817s
file:///Users/seinopsys/.npm/_npx/4474/lib/node_modules/glob/node_modules/jackspeak/dist/esm/index.js:496
                cause ??= { name: field, found: value };
                      ^^^

SyntaxError: Unexpected token '??='
    at Loader.moduleStrategy (internal/modules/esm/translators.js:145:18)
```

The `??=` operator is only supported from Node version 15.14.0 onwards ([source](https://node.green/#ES2021-features-Logical-Assignment-----basic-support)).

According to the `engines` field in `package.json` node 14.20.1 matches the allowed version range but I do not see any actions validating that it actually works with this version.

I would like to suggest adding validations for all supported Node versions or dropping support for these older versions via removing them from the `engines` constraint if they are no longer actively validated.
issues.author: SeinopSys

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
If you're using EOL node versions, especially those that have been EOL as long as node 14 has, you must lock dependencies, and carefully review every update, even patch updates. That's what "EOL" means. Of course, it's best to just update to a node version that's actively supported, but that's not always an option.

The glob lib works in 14, it's just the bin that doesn't.

issues.title: v10.4.0 raised minimum required node version to 18
issues.body:
In https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/ed0d061c5bc6a9f4f05e94e3ba902a4f8649ebdf, a require for `node:url` was added which implicitly raises the Node version above what was previously supported and is still declared [in its engines](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/eef7ea35afe511079c5bf83862ed57ece2bbf7fa/package.json#L96). This wasn't detected because Node 16 checks were [removed earlier](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/b5d0f640893eba729bb8675a51a73ffbc0760b35).

This is probably worth a changelog entry at minimum
issues.author: connor4312

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The engines states `">=16 || 14 >=14.18"`.

Node 16.0.0 and 14.18.0 (the lowest versions in each line allowed by the engines range) both support `require("node:url")`.

```
$ node -v
v14.18.0

$ node -p 'require("node:url")'
{
  Url: [Function: Url],
  parse: [Function: urlParse],
  resolve: [Function: urlResolve],
  resolveObject: [Function: urlResolveObject],
  format: [Function: urlFormat],
  URL: [class URL],
  URLSearchParams: [class URLSearchParams],
  domainToASCII: [Function: domainToASCII],
  domainToUnicode: [Function: domainToUnicode],
  pathToFileURL: [Function: pathToFileURL],
  fileURLToPath: [Function: fileURLToPath],
  urlToHttpOptions: [Function: urlToHttpOptions]
}
```

```
$ node -v
v16.0.0

$ node -p 'require("node:url")'
{
  Url: [Function: Url],
  parse: [Function: urlParse],
  resolve: [Function: urlResolve],
  resolveObject: [Function: urlResolveObject],
  format: [Function: urlFormat],
  URL: [class URL],
  URLSearchParams: [class URLSearchParams],
  domainToASCII: [Function: domainToASCII],
  domainToUnicode: [Function: domainToUnicode],
  pathToFileURL: [Function: pathToFileURL],
  fileURLToPath: [Function: fileURLToPath],
  urlToHttpOptions: [Function: urlToHttpOptions]
}
```

So, I'm not sure what problem you're encountering with it, but it's not node version.

Are you running into issues with Jest? I've heard from a lot of people that the `node:...` module ids aren't supported until recently. Try updating that maybe?

comments.author: Mr0grog
comments.body:
> Your package manager should already be filtering out versions with a mismatched engines requirement, doesn't that work?

NPM, at least, will log a nice warning but proceed with installation of the incompatible version and give you a successful exit code. Nothing in most build pipelines, for example, is going to stop or flag a failure here, which could certainly cause people some surprising problems! This also poses a problem for libraries that depend on glob, since it implicitly raises their Node.js minimum requirements without any changes on their part (in fact, I’m glad I just happened to be scrutinizing this update — for other, abnormal reasons — since this is exactly the position I was about to be in).

I certainly thought this was pretty surprising in a patch release and I’d generally agree with @wghule that this is a breaking change for some people. *At the very least,* it deserves a callout in the [changelog](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/main/changelog.md).

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: npm should prioritize an engine match over a version that has an engine conflict. It only warns if it has to choose a version with an engines conflict, or throws if `--engines-strict` is set.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: https://github.com/npm/npm-pick-manifest/blob/1841ad297f2c22b8a76ede7de680dc639635a824/lib/index.js#L177

comments.author: Mr0grog
comments.body: Well, I can only say that I tried it and, in practice, it installed an incompatible version. (On Node.js 16, required `"glob": "^10.0.0"`, resulted in 10.4.3 installed.)

comments.author: Mr0grog
comments.body:
Maybe the code you’re pointing to is different in versions of NPM that are compatible with Node.js 16? I don’t know all the details of NPM’s own complex tree of dependencies across versions and, FWIW, I don’t feel like it is worth digging to understand what might be going wrong on a version of NPM for a version of Node.js that is probably no longer supported.

I think the bigger issue is just that, *in practice,* at least one dependency manager (and probably others?) don’t automatically solve this issue for users the way you are expecting it to.

comments.author: kerm1it
comments.body:
I think it is correct to change the baseline version of nodejs, but the best practice is to release a major version to do this, because many other npm packages rely on your dependencies and use the semantic version specification. Now you have upgraded the baseline version of node through the patch version, which has caused problems for many upstream dependencies. Because many of our projects still rely on node 16 and are not ready to upgrade to version 18, I think this change is a break change.

So see if you can release a new patch to restore the baseline version support of node, and then change the baseline of node version by upgrading the major version, so that we can upgrade to node 18 when we are ready.

comments.author: kerm1it
comments.body:
If we directly depend on these packages, it seems that we can solve it by locking the version, but there is such a situation:

`packageA` depends on glob, and the version number is `^10.0.0`, `packageB` depends on `packageA`, if I only use package B in the project, then I can't directly lock the version of `glob`.

In addition, some people may say that you can use yarn.lock or yarn resolutions to lock the version, but when `npm i -g a-cli` or `yarn global add a-cli`, these methods don't work, and only the latest version that meets the conditions will be resolved and installed according to the semantic version.

issues.title: "../" being added to files when cwd has trailing slash on Node 23
issues.body:
Hi folks! 

Just last night we started noticing an issue with glob on Heroku, where the 'files' array returned from glob has leading "../" characters, but only on Heroku (using Node 23) and only when the cwd has a trailing slash. 

When using this:
```js
glob("**/**", {
    cwd: "./static/",
    absolute: false,
    follow: true,
    nodir: true,
    dot: true,
  }
 ```

The output looks like this:

## Example output
```js
[
    "..",
    "../two.txt",
    "../one.txt"
],
```

It should look like this:

```js
[
    ".",
    "two.txt",
    "one.txt"
  ]
```

## Reproduction info
You can see the code I used here to reproduce this:
https://github.com/avodah-dev/glob-slash-weirdness/blob/main/index.js

Here's how it looks when deployed to Heroku, with the trailing slash on rootPath:
https://glob-test-aace9e4820c6.herokuapp.com/?s=1

And using the "s" query param here's how it looks without the trailing slash:
https://glob-test-aace9e4820c6.herokuapp.com/?s=0

## Digital Ocean example (working correctly)
For reference, it works correctly when I run it locally or on Digital Ocean (app platform) using Node 22:
https://sea-turtle-app-vg7i8.ondigitalocean.app/?s=1

## Background
We stumbled into this issue last week on Heroku, initially using a 3rd-party buildpack for pnpm, but then confirmed to be an issue using the official NodeJS buildpack, and using npm today.

It was working great yesterday morning on Heroku, but then started breaking on new builds last night (10/22/2024).

I'll create a support ticket with Heroku in case it's an issue completely on their end, but I was hoping glob maintainers might know what's going on too.

### Updated
- Added Node 23 distinction (Heroku just upgraded to use Node 23 while Digital Ocean is still using 22)
- Added source code example
issues.author: nathanbedford

comments.author: nathanbedford
comments.body:
Some extra info I just though of that might be helpful to track this down:

On Heroku, the exact same code from my project that ran correctly two days ago is breaking now. If I use Heroku's "rollback" feature, the old deployment package works correctly now, but when I use that same code and deploy again it break. My guess is that something in Heroku's infrastructure (libraries, packages, etc) changed and is conflicting now with something in glob.

Here's the ticket with Heroku too:

comments.body: Can you provide an example that's just using glob, not a web app running on heroku or digital ocean? I'm not really sure how to know what the issue is. Just a standalone node program with expectations and actual values would be great.

issues.title: Native Node test runner can't find tests located in folder containing square braces
issues.body:
Running following script will yield empty results (0 tests found) in Node v21 or greater:

```
node --test src/app/api/[\id\]/route.spec.js
```

The file exists since it would throw an error otherwise. Also, it works with Node 20.17.0.

I'm not sure if the error is in Glob or in Node but would like to track it down. Any help/hints are appreciated.
issues.author: MrBr

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
I'm not seeing a problem with glob, it's working as intended?

```
> require('glob').sync('src/app/api/[id\\]/*.txt')
[
  'src/app/api/[id]/a.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/b.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/c.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/d.txt'
]
```

The first `\` is unnecessary, since `i` doesn't need to be escaped, but it doesn't do any harm.

```
> require('glob').sync('src/app/api/[\\id\\]/*.txt')
[
  'src/app/api/[id]/a.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/b.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/c.txt',
  'src/app/api/[id]/d.txt'
]
```

I have no idea how Node is using glob, or what version they're using, but I suspect it's not this code, or else it'd be working.

issues.title: RangeError: Map maximum size exceeded - 2^24 file limit?
issues.body:
Hi, I have a long-running process using `glob`, and over time, it scans through lots of files.  Eventually, we get the following error:

```
/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:108
    normalizeCache.set(s, n);
                   ^

RangeError: Map maximum size exceeded
    at Map.set (<anonymous>)
    at normalize (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:108:20)
    at new PathBase (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:319:60)
    at new PathPosix (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:1264:9)
    at PathPosix.newChild (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:1282:16)
    at #readdirAddNewChild (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:842:28)
    at #readdirAddChild (/home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:837:37)
    at /home/ed/node_modules/path-scurry/dist/commonjs/index.js:1006:42
    at node:fs:187:23
    at node:internal/util:519:12
    at getDirents (node:internal/fs/utils:286:7)
    at req.oncomplete (node:fs:1479:7)
```

It looks like this is happening because of this global `normalizeCache` variable: https://github.com/isaacs/path-scurry/blob/main/src/index.ts#L177, which never gets cleared.  Is there a way that the cache can be cleared?

issues.title: MacOS ignore "/Contents/" by default
issues.body:
On MacOS if I run:
```
import { globSync } from 'glob';
const files = globSync('./plugins/**/*.*');
console.log(files);
```

The output is:
```
[
  'plugins/Surge XT Effects.app',
  'plugins/Surge XT Effects.app/Contents/Info.plist',
  'plugins/Surge XT Effects.app/Contents/Resources/RecentFilesMenuTemplate.nib',
  'plugins/Surge XT Effects.app/Contents/Resources/Icon.icns'
]
```

If I change to:
```
import { globSync } from 'glob';
const files = globSync('./plugins/**/*.*', { ignore: ['./plugins/**/Contents/**/*'] });
console.log(files);
```

The output is:
```
[
  'plugins/Surge XT Effects.app',
]
```

My assumption is that most users want want the "file" as shown in the UI, vs the contents of the package.

1. Should the default functionality be to hide the Contents directory?
2. Could there be a helper to switch this feature on/off without specifying an ignore negation rule?
issues.author: kmturley

issues.title: Feature suggestion: Do not follow symlinks
issues.author: kmturley

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Unfortunately, doing this as you describe would be a huge breaking change, and also, would deviate from the intended behavior of matching Bash 5.3.

Currently, symbolic links are followed _for globstar `**` pattern portions_, only if `follow:true` is set. Otherwise, exactly _one_ symbolic link is followed, but no more than that. Since this is the only type of pattern that can ever potentially result in an unbounded walk, the threat of a recursive loop is avoided already. If you do set `follow:true`, which is not recommended, and the docs and tooltip will advise you against, then a `**` pattern _can_ result many many more results, but will still eventually hit the `ELOOP` from the system, and stop the traversal.

For all other glob patterns (extglobs, `*`, normal non-magic strings), it would actually not improve performance, and in fact, in many cases, it could make it much worse, because it would mean that we'd have to test every portion of the pattern.

For example, consider a pattern like: `x/y/z/a/b/c/*/d`. When we expand this pattern, we don't bother to check what `x/y/z/a/b/c`, are.  We just `readdir('x/y/z/a/b/c')` straight away, because all of those are standard non-magical strings, so they can't possibly match anything else. If, say, `x/y/z` doesn't exist, or isn't a directory, then the `readdir` will fail, and we know there's no matches, and we've saved a lot of unnecessary filesystem operations. If the readdir doesn't fail, then for each entry we find, we prepend `x/y/z/a/b/c` to it, and append `/d` to it, and if that thing exists, then add it to the result set. Checking for symlinks would significantly add overhead, because it would mean checking for things that we don't currently check for.

So, you might say, leave aside non-magic path portions, and say that we provide a mechanism to only match non-symlink items for any magic glob pattern. So, if we had `x/y/z/a/b/c/*/d` then we would only match on the children of `c` that are not symlinks. Well, that still means we have to get those things' stats, which we don't currently do. Also, if `x/y/z` is a symlink, and we follow it (because it's expensive to do the work to know not to), then now we've got an option like `glob('x/y/z/a/b/c/*/d', { neverEverFollowSymlinks: true })`, and users might rightfully be upset!

So, really, I don't think that this is worth the overhead. I'm sorry that you spent a few hours of your precious human life on this, but the docs do say that it works the same as bash, so it's quite easy to see that it's doing the same thing just by dropping into a terminal and typing `echo <pattern>` to see what it matches.

issues.title: ERR_REQUIRE_CYCLE_MODULE when using glob@11.0.1 with Node.js v22.13.1 unless minimatch@10.0.1 is explicitly set
issues.body:
Hi team,

I'm encountering a runtime error when using glob@11.0.1 in a Node.js v22.13.1 environment. Unless I explicitly set minimatch to version 10.0.1, I get the following error:
```
Error [ERR_REQUIRE_CYCLE_MODULE]: Cannot require() ES Module
 ...brace-expansion/index.js in a cycle. (from ...minimatch/dist/commonjs/index.js)
```

This appears to be caused by a circular dependency involving brace-expansion and older versions of minimatch. After some testing, I found that explicitly setting minimatch to 10.0.1 resolves the issue.

Here’s the relevant portion of my npm list minimatch output:
```
@azure-tools/js-sdk-release-tools@2.11.1
├─┬ glob@11.0.1
│ └── minimatch@10.0.1
├─┬ @npmcli/package-json@6.1.1
│ └─┬ glob@10.4.5
│   └── minimatch@9.0.5
├─┬ copyfiles@2.4.1
│ ├─┬ glob@7.2.3
│ │ └── minimatch@3.1.2 deduped
│ └── minimatch@3.1.2
├─┬ npm-registry-fetch@18.0.2
│ └─┬ make-fetch-happen@14.0.3
│   └─┬ cacache@19.0.1
│     └─┬ glob@10.4.5
│       └── minimatch@9.0.5
├─┬ ts-morph@23.0.0
│ └─┬ @ts-morph/common@0.24.0
│   └── minimatch@9.0.5
└─┬ typescript-codegen-breaking-change-detector@0.5.6
  ├─┬ @typescript-eslint/typescript-estree@7.18.0
  │ └── minimatch@9.0.5
  └─┬ @typescript-eslint/utils@7.18.0
    └─┬ eslint@8.57.0
      ├─┬ @eslint/eslintrc@2.1.4
      │ └── minimatch@3.1.2
      ├─┬ @humanwhocodes/config-array@0.11.14
      │ └── minimatch@3.1.2
      ├─┬ file-entry-cache@6.0.1
      │ └─┬ flat-cache@3.2.0
      │   └─┬ rimraf@3.0.2
      │     └─┬ glob@7.2.3
      │       └── minimatch@3.1.2
      └── minimatch@3.1.2
```
It looks like multiple versions of minimatch are being pulled in transitively, and only 10.0.1 avoids the error.

There’s a related issue already filed in the minimatch repo: https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/issues/259 — just wanted to share this here in case it helps with diagnosing or documenting the compatibility between glob and minimatch under newer Node.js versions.

Thanks for your work on this package!

Yeah, looks like I need to fork balanced-match and brace-expansion to keep them CJS/ESM hybrid.

issues.title: [Feature Request] Add `Glob.test(file: string): boolean` function to test if a file would be included according to the `Glob` config
issues.body:
Mocha has been working on a PR to use `glob` and `chokidar` v4 for their watch mode: https://github.com/mochajs/mocha/pull/5379

Right now the PR copies a lot of `glob`'s internal logic so that it can match `glob`'s behavior.  They can't really afford to do an actual `glob` walk, since they expect the files to keep changing, instead they need to test each file reported by `chokidar` against the include/ignore glob patterns.

I think it would be nice if `glob` provided an API to test a file against its include/ignore rules so that it's easier and cleaner for Mocha and other packages to match `glob`'s logic.


`chokidar` v4 dropped support for glob patterns, so other packages that use it will have this same issue if they need to do glob watching.
issues.author: jedwards1211

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: Is it possible that you're looking for [`minimatch`](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch), the matcher that glob uses?

comments.author: jedwards1211
comments.body:
Well @Arnesfield can speak to this better than me, but since minimatch operates on a single pattern, dealing with dozens of include and ignore patterns in a large project would be pretty inefficient.  Glob uses Pattern to handle multiple patterns more efficiently, right?

Mocha may not be able to filter the applicable patterns down the way Glob can while walking a specific directory, but they evidently found something about what Glob does on top of minimatch necessary.

comments.author: jedwards1211
comments.body:
Okay another thing Mocha has to do is determine which directories to tell `chokidar` to watch based upon the input include and ignore patterns.  `Minimatch` doesn't really provide an API for that.  `Glob` determines its starting directories from the `Minimatch.set`s but doesn't publically expose that logic, so Mocha had to copy over what `Glob` is doing.

It's pretty nontrivial logic, so I think it would make sense if `minimatch` or some other package provided an API that accepts multiple include and ignore patterns, and can provide the set of directories to scan and a function for testing against all of those patterns (more efficiently than iterating through `Minimatch`ers if possible).  Then `glob` and glob-watching tools could rely on that shared logic.

comments.author: Arnesfield
comments.body:
The main use case here for mocha's watch mode was to be able to parse out the path without the glob part from the provided watch paths (e.g., `path/to/**/file` would become `path/to`). To do that (at least at the time), the `Glob.patterns` array was being iterated on, essentially using the `Pattern` class which is currently not exported by the `glob` package (and there was a need to use the `Pattern` class as an explicit type for some variables).

I clearly missed it at the time, but now that I actually look into glob's internals and read how the `Pattern`s are being created, I can see now that it really was just using `new Minimatch()`, and the `Pattern` class is more or less an extra wrapper for mocha's case, where `Minimatch.set` / `Minimatch.globParts` / `Minimatch.globSet` could have been used instead.

I guess this issue here is pretty much resolved. For mocha's updated watch mode, while it still works as intended, it would probably be best to refactor the path parsing logic to use `minimatch` directly instead of using `Glob.patterns`.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, I asked because it's entirely possible that I'm using something in Minimatch that is technically exposed, and likely very stable, but maybe not documented or "blessed" as public API. I of course can get away with that confidently, because I maintain both libraries, and because minimatch exists primarily to support node-glob anyway.

If you find that you _can_ use Minimatch directly, but would like to validate that what you're doing is safe and stable, then feel free to post an issue over on that repo, and I'll weigh in, update docs, whatever.

issues.title: Performance optimization opportunities: Directory traversal caching, parallel operations, and stream improvements
issues.body:
## Summary

As a critical infrastructure package used throughout the Node.js ecosystem, \`glob\` has significant potential for performance improvements that could benefit millions of projects. We've been exploring optimizations in our fork ([jdmiranda/node-glob](https://github.com/jdmiranda/node-glob), branch \`perf/glob-cache\`) and have identified several high-impact opportunities beyond our current work.

Our initial optimizations focused on:
- **Pattern compilation caching** - Reusing Minimatch instances to avoid redundant pattern parsing
- **Result memoization with TTL** - Caching glob results for repeated queries
- **Stat call reduction** - Caching filesystem stat results to minimize syscalls

These changes yielded **2-5% performance improvements** in our benchmarks across repeated glob runs and reduced memory growth under long-lived processes.

## File System Bottleneck Analysis

Modern glob operations are heavily bottlenecked by filesystem I/O. Profiling shows the primary pain points are:

1. **Redundant \`readdir()\` calls** - Directories are often traversed multiple times
2. **Serial stat operations** - Each file/directory requires individual blocking stat calls
3. **Symlink resolution overhead** - \`realpath()\` is expensive and uncached
4. **Ignore pattern re-evaluation** - \`.gitignore\` and similar patterns are parsed repeatedly

## Additional Optimization Opportunities

### 1. Directory Traversal Cache 💡

**Problem:** When using multiple glob patterns or repeated glob calls, the same directories are read multiple times via \`readdir()\`.

**Solution:** Cache \`readdir()\` results with appropriate TTL or invalidation strategy.

\`\`\`typescript
// Conceptual approach
const readdirCache = new Map<string, { entries: Dirent[], timestamp: number }>()

async function cachedReaddir(path: string, ttl = 5000) {
  const cached = readdirCache.get(path)
  if (cached && Date.now() - cached.timestamp < ttl) {
    return cached.entries
  }
  const entries = await fs.readdir(path, { withFileTypes: true })
  readdirCache.set(path, { entries, timestamp: Date.now() })
  return entries
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 5-15% improvement for multi-pattern globs or applications with hot glob paths.

---

### 2. Symlink Resolution Cache 🔗

**Problem:** \`fs.realpath()\` is extremely expensive (multiple syscalls per invocation) and commonly used in \`follow:true\` mode or \`realpath:true\` option.

**Solution:** Cache resolved symlink targets with LRU eviction.

\`\`\`typescript
const realpathCache = new Map<string, string>()
const MAX_REALPATH_CACHE = 10000

async function cachedRealpath(path: string) {
  if (realpathCache.has(path)) {
    // LRU touch: delete and re-insert to mark as recently used
    const resolved = realpathCache.get(path)!
    realpathCache.delete(path)
    realpathCache.set(path, resolved)
    return resolved
  }
  
  const resolved = await fs.realpath(path)
  
  if (realpathCache.size >= MAX_REALPATH_CACHE) {
    const firstKey = realpathCache.keys().next().value
    realpathCache.delete(firstKey)
  }
  realpathCache.set(path, resolved)
  return resolved
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 10-20% improvement when \`realpath:true\` is enabled, especially in projects with many symlinks (e.g., \`node_modules\`).

---

### 3. Ignore Pattern Optimization 🚫

**Problem:** Parsing \`.gitignore\` and other ignore files is repeated for every directory traversal. The current implementation re-evaluates ignore patterns frequently.

**Solution:** 
- Cache compiled ignore patterns per directory
- Pre-compile ignore patterns once and reuse
- Consider adopting a more efficient ignore matcher (like [ignore](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ignore) package internals)

\`\`\`typescript
const ignoreCache = new Map<string, IgnoreMatcher>()

function getCachedIgnoreMatcher(dir: string, patterns: string[]) {
  const key = \`\${dir}:\${patterns.join('|')}\`
  if (!ignoreCache.has(key)) {
    ignoreCache.set(key, compileIgnorePatterns(patterns))
  }
  return ignoreCache.get(key)!
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 3-8% improvement for projects with extensive ignore patterns (monorepos, large codebases).

---

### 4. Concurrent File System Operations ⚡

**Problem:** Current implementation processes stat calls and directory reads serially. Modern async I/O can handle multiple concurrent operations efficiently.

**Solution:** Batch stat calls and readdir operations using \`Promise.all()\` or \`Promise.allSettled()\` with concurrency limits.

\`\`\`typescript
import pLimit from 'p-limit'

const statLimit = pLimit(50) // Tune based on system limits

async function parallelStat(paths: string[]) {
  return Promise.all(
    paths.map(path => statLimit(() => fs.lstat(path)))
  )
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 15-30% improvement for large directory trees with many files, especially on SSDs with high IOPS.

**Caveat:** Must respect system file descriptor limits and be optional (could default to concurrency of 1 for safety).

---

### 5. Minimatch Call Reduction 🎯

**Problem:** Pattern matching with minimatch is called repeatedly for each file, even when patterns are simple or could be pre-filtered.

**Solution:** Implement fast-path matching for common simple patterns before falling back to minimatch.

\`\`\`typescript
function fastPathMatch(pattern: string, filename: string): boolean | null {
  // Fast path for *.ext patterns
  const simpleExt = /^\*\.(\w+)$/.exec(pattern)
  if (simpleExt) {
    return filename.endsWith('.' + simpleExt[1])
  }
  
  // Fast path for **.ext patterns
  const recursiveExt = /^\*\*\.(\w+)$/.exec(pattern)
  if (recursiveExt) {
    return filename.endsWith('.' + recursiveExt[1])
  }
  
  // Not a simple pattern, use minimatch
  return null
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 5-10% improvement for simple, common patterns like \`**/*.js\`, \`**/*.ts\`.

---

### 6. Stream-Based Results for Large Globs 🌊

**Problem:** For very large glob results (100k+ files), building the entire array in memory can cause pressure and GC overhead.

**Solution:** The existing stream API is excellent, but could be further optimized by:
- Implementing backpressure-aware directory traversal (pause readdir when downstream is slow)
- Batch emitting results to reduce event loop overhead
- Provide a \`Transform\` stream interface for easier piping

\`\`\`typescript
// Example: batch emitting
class BatchedGlobStream extends Minipass {
  private batch: string[] = []
  private batchSize = 100
  
  addResult(result: string) {
    this.batch.push(result)
    if (this.batch.length >= this.batchSize) {
      this.flush()
    }
  }
  
  flush() {
    if (this.batch.length > 0) {
      this.write(this.batch)
      this.batch = []
    }
  }
}
\`\`\`

**Impact:** 5-10% improvement for very large result sets (>50k files); reduces peak memory usage by 20-40%.

---

## Implementation Considerations

### Opt-in Defaults
Many optimizations (especially caching) should be opt-in or have conservative defaults to avoid surprising behavior:
- Cache TTLs should be tunable
- Concurrent operations should respect ulimit
- Cache sizes should be bounded with LRU eviction

### Cache Invalidation
For long-running processes, provide:
- Manual cache clearing APIs: \`glob.clearCache()\`
- Automatic TTL-based expiration
- Optional filesystem watchers for cache invalidation (advanced use case)

### Backwards Compatibility
All optimizations should:
- Maintain existing API surface
- Pass all current tests
- Be thoroughly benchmarked against existing behavior
- Document any new options clearly

## Collaboration Offer

We're happy to:
- Share benchmark harnesses and results
- Contribute PRs for any of these optimizations
- Help with testing and validation
- Provide real-world usage data from our production workloads

Our fork demonstrates the pattern/result caching approach, and we'd be thrilled to work with you on upstreaming these improvements or implementing additional optimizations.

## References

- Our fork: https://github.com/jdmiranda/node-glob (branch: \`perf/glob-cache\`)
- Optimization PR #1: https://github.com/jdmiranda/node-glob/pull/1 (sideEffects + packaging)
- Optimization PR #2: https://github.com/jdmiranda/node-glob/pull/2 (cache LRU + bounded pattern cache)
- Related work: [fast-glob performance techniques](https://github.com/mrmlnc/fast-glob#how-does-fast-glob-work)

---

**TL;DR:** Glob is critical infrastructure. We've identified 6 high-impact optimization opportunities (directory cache, symlink cache, ignore optimization, parallel I/O, fast-path matching, stream improvements) that could yield **15-40% aggregate performance gains**. Happy to collaborate on implementation.
issues.author: jdmiranda

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Some of these suggestions are a bit confusing. Every filesystem operation that glob does is performed through path-scurry, which _does_ cache everything, forever. Are you looking at the most recently released version?

I appreciate the work that you put into this, however, a single issue with so much content is much harder to engage with, and making large sweeping changes to important modules is a recipe for disaster.

Instead, let's please address _one_ issue at a time per github issue, and examine the problem, and weigh proposed changes.

issues.title: Document breaking changes for major versions
issues.body:
There is no easy to follow list of breaking changes for major versions. [This issue](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/issues/467) for glob@8 recognizes the same problem, but it persists for version 9 as well. 

This is particularly bad since version 9 has so many changes. This makes keeping up-to-date much harder than it should be since you have to go through the full changelog and figure out if a change is breaking or not. 

There is also as far as I can tell no migration guide or similar. That would be nice.
issues.author: PowerSupply

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
The changelog, readme, and git history are open source and freely available for you to review. This is not a problem; it's just something _you_ don't prefer. I disagree that it's bad. It's good, actually, because no time and energy was wasted doing this work.

I would accept a PR to the changelog that called out breaking changes. Perhaps some other users of this library would appreciate it as well. That is, if _you_ do it, I'll accept it.

However, I am personally not interested in creating migration guides or other excessive documentation, for free in my spare time. If you'd like to pay me to do that sort of thing, you can put $1500 [into my sponsorship](https://github.com/sponsors/isaacs), and I'll spend up to 3 hours creating migration guides and identifying which changes are breaking.

Otherwise, it's quite clear that you are getting far more than you are paying for.

comments.body: Oh, also, 10.3.7 isn't the first vulnerable version, 10.2.0 is. They researcher got confused because the `bin` file got renamed to `.mts` at that version, so it didn't show up in the git history before then.

issues.title: Is there a changelog ?
issues.body: In my project, I use glob 11 and I have just seen that two major versions have been released. However, I cannot find any changelog to find out what the breaking changes are.
issues.author: oliviermattei

comments.author: luyiourwong
comments.body:
There is a file on root: [changelog.md](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/blob/main/changelog.md)
Those major versions have breaking changes for the CLI part to fix vulnerability [GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/security/advisories/GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2).
If you are not updated to a higher version, you can use 11.1.0 which has patched the problem.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
# changeglob

## 13

- Move the CLI program out to a separate package, `glob-bin`.
  Install that if you'd like to continue using glob from the
  command line.

## 12

- Remove the unsafe `--shell` option. The `--shell` option is now
  ONLY supported on known shells where the behavior can be
  implemented safely.

## 11.1

[GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/security/advisories/GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2)

- Add the `--shell` option for the command line, with a warning
  that this is unsafe. (It will be removed in v12.)
- Add the `--cmd-arg`/`-g` as a way to _safely_ add positional
  arguments to the command provided to the CLI tool.
- Detect commands with space or quote characters on known shells,
  and pass positional arguments to them safely, avoiding
  `shell:true` execution.

issues.title: Clarification on deprecation notice for 10.5.0 and 11.1.0
issues.body:
Currently all versions, excluding 12.x and 13.x are deprecated with the following message:
> Old versions of glob are not supported, and contain widely publicized security vulnerabilities, which have been fixed in the current version. Please update. Support for old versions may be purchased (at exorbitant rates) by contacting i@izs.me

This also includes the 10.5.0 and 11.1.0 releases with patches for CVE-2025-64756.

@isaacs can you please confirm if these versions contain any new vulnerabilities? Thank you!
issues.author: tsvetomir

comments.author: frontzhm
comments.body: 我看邮件的反应很迟钝。。。如果有事发短信提醒我看邮件。。。。。。

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
They do not contain new vulnerabilities. But the fix on the v10 and v11 branches were incomplete.

The complete fix was a breaking change which appeared in bb521e5, in v12.

> commit bb521e5d188a8cbbe86d5cef3bf57a49fdee7c59
> Author: isaacs <i@izs.me>
> Date:   Thu Nov 13 15:08:37 2025 -0800

    Remove --shell option where unsafe to use
    
    This provides a more comprehensive and safe fix for CVE-2025-64756, by
    making the `--shell` option fully nonfunctional on systems where it is
    not known to be safe.
    
    Re: https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/security/advisories/GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2
    Re: CVE-2025-64756

comments.author: tsvetomir
comments.body: Got it, thank you!

issues.title: Is the default brace expansion limit supposed to be 1_000 or 10_000?
issues.body:
https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob/commit/c8574ebb3f9fd543b8fc97bfe18ca91ef0c5c78b#diff-a830c21fd29a1f8241fc77de09130b52f34cb29218965e0350fdad46e05eb5f7L341-L514

Looking at the diff for this commit, the change does not match the commit message and wanted to be sure where the error lay.  If it's in the commit message then it's just a typo, if it's in the code then it's an order of magnitude larger than it was intended to be.

Just pointing this out in case you think it's actionable.  If not, we can just close this issue and move on.
issues.author: wraithgar

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body:
Yeah, sorry, I did later update the docs to correct it, but 10,000 is still extremely safe for globs purposes, and a bit more ludicrously out of range of any reasonable fs search, so it's fine to leave it there.

If you want to set it to 1000, that's probably also fine. I found it started causing trouble around the 100k to 1M range.

comments.author: isaacs
comments.body: That is, this was a mistake in the commit message, not because I mistyped it, but because I thought that I'd landed on a different value, and only realized the error once it was already published 😅

issues.title: Minimatch ReDoS vulnerability
issues.body:
https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-3ppc-4f35-3m26 This package needs to update minimatch to version 10.2.1.

```
 $ yarn why -R --peers minimatch
└─ bascom@workspace:.
   └─ pyright@npm:1.1.408 (via npm:^1.1.408)
      └─ fsevents@patch:fsevents@npm%3A2.3.3#optional!builtin<compat/fsevents>::version=2.3.3&hash=df0bf1 (via patch:fsevents@npm%3A~2.3.3#optional!builtin<compat/fsevents>)
         └─ node-gyp@npm:11.4.2 (via npm:latest)
            └─ make-fetch-happen@npm:14.0.3 (via npm:^14.0.3)
               └─ cacache@npm:19.0.1 (via npm:^19.0.1)
                  └─ glob@npm:10.5.0 (via npm:^10.2.2)
                     └─ minimatch@npm:9.0.5 (via npm:^9.0.4)
```
issues.author: Tatsh
